A 'plant of slow growth' that reflects the cumulative knowledge acquired through all experiences from birth and is the decisive factor in mature reading comprehension.
A convenient index to a person's breadth of knowledge and the most reliable correlate to reading ability.
chapters.source_passage: vocabulary size is a convenient index to a person’s breadth of knowledge. Vocabulary size is the single most reliable correlate to reading ability.

chapters.author_definition: The discrepancy in the total number of words heard by children from different socioeconomic strata (professional, working-class, and welfare) by age four.
The Hegelian concept that the processes of nature, history, and the mind are infused with a divine essence that makes progress inevitable.
Policy makers and advocates who strongly emphasize testing and school accountability measures.
Components of science study that move beyond objective facts to include appreciation of beauty and the formation of values, mirroring the role of morality in humanistic subjects.
The theory that reading comprehension is a general cognitive ability that can be applied equally to any text regardless of its subject matter.
The educational theory, arising post-1940, that tactile and visual learning are more natural for children than abstract and verbal learning through books and lectures.
A Hegelian term meaning 'to cancel' while 'lifting up.' The author argues it represents a 'providential impulse' where historical progress is assumed to go upward toward a divine goal.
A term the author considers an oversimplification of the educational process, often used to justify content-light curricula.
The intentional cooperation among 19th-century textbook makers to standardize content to foster national patriotic sentiment.
A political entity that protects security and prosperity while allowing private affairs and local customs to remain autonomous.
The educational process governed by the principle that knowledge is required to gain more knowledge, leading to cumulative advantages or disadvantages.
The 1989 report, co-authored by Pierre Bourdieu and Jacques Derrida, which served as the intellectual foundation for the decentralization and skill-centrism of the Jospin Law.
Basic contractual stipulations regarding pay and working conditions that teacher unions focus on when the intellectual environment of the classroom becomes dysfunctional.
Summer-study books based on the national curriculum designed for students and parents to prepare for the upcoming year's work.
The shared set of facts, names, and concepts that members of a culture take for granted in communication; it changes slowly but can be influenced by cultural activism.
A state of American education characterized by soul-deadening, repetitive exercises aimed at developing nonexistent general skills, which ultimately fails to improve scores or abilities.
chapters.source_passage: A benign child-centeredness coupled with a faulty theory about general skills has led us to a child purgatory of skill drills.

chapters.author_definition: An educational philosophy where students elaborate their own curricula based on personal aspirations, capacities, and home cultures rather than a national standard.
chapters.source_passage: Education... shall be designed and organized for the benefit of pupils and students... Pupils and students will elaborate their own curricula according to their own aspirations and capacities.

chapters.author_definition: An educational environment that is caring, humane in pedagogy, and joyful, without sacrificing rigorous content or socialized values.
chapters.source_passage: I have long been an admirer of Japanese elementary schools, which are very child-centered in the good sense of the term.

chapters.author_definition: The limited mental capacity of young children to perform the dual tasks of mechanical decoding and meaning-processing simultaneously.
Individuals who possess the requisite background knowledge and vocabulary to effectively utilize modern information tools like Google and digital libraries.
chapters.source_passage: Instead of being an agent of equality, Google rewards cognitive insiders. It consolidates educational inequality.

chapters.author_definition: The structural and collaborative alignment within a school system where curriculum and teacher efforts across grades support one another.
chapters.source_passage: The incident illustrated how good teaching can often depend more reliably on the coherence of the wider system, and the cooperation it brings, than on virtuoso performances.

chapters.author_definition: The empirical finding that school quality affects disadvantaged students twice as much (both positively and negatively) as it affects advantaged students.
chapters.source_passage: Coleman’s insight that school quality affects disadvantaged students twice as much as advantaged children deserves to be memorialized as the 'Coleman Differential Effect.'

chapters.author_definition: A sense of communal belonging and shared mission that arises when all members of a school are focused on the same clear knowledge goals.
A system of schooling that advances a knowledgeable citizenry and social cohesion, serving as the foundation of modern democracy.
A strong, community-defined set of knowledge that is systematically imparted to all students to ensure social justice and civic competence.
An approach focused on socialization and the systematic acquisition of shared knowledge, language, and conventions of the public sphere.
chapters.source_passage: Early education should be chiefly communal—focused on gaining proficiency in the language and the conventions of the public sphere.

chapters.author_definition: The educational theory that schools should provide a shared, specific body of knowledge to all students to ensure social fairness and effectiveness.
chapters.source_passage: replaced by the far more effective and fair principle of communal knowledge.

chapters.author_definition: A preschool model characterized by a well-mapped-out sequence of shared knowledge domains delivered at a child-friendly pace to ensure universal participation.
A school system characterized by a well-developed, common curriculum shared by all students regardless of background.
A national curriculum or shared educational framework designed to narrow achievement gaps by providing common knowledge to all students.
The ability to write and speak effectively to strangers within the wider community as a mark of educational success and autonomy.
Broadly includes speaking, listening, reading, and writing, all of which Hirsch argues are dependent on vocabulary and shared knowledge rather than just technical or emotional intelligence.
An educational goal focused on the common knowledge and communal voice required for a child to participate in the wider public sphere.
A measure of text difficulty typically operationalized through quantitative scores like Lexiles, which the author argues is insufficient without content coherence.
Hirsch argues this is not a general ability but is actually a function of domain familiarity; knowing the subject matter is the 'skill' that allows one to handle difficult texts.
A method pioneered by John Guthrie that uses domain immersion to build reading comprehension and vocabulary.
A defect in a test that lies not in the test's construction but in the harmful, self-defeating educational practices it encourages in schools.
The principle that a test's technical validity is secondary to its actual impact on educational outcomes; if a test causes self-defeating educational practices, it is consequentially invalid.
chapters.source_passage: His phrase was “consequential validity.” His key insight was that the technical validity of a test is of little value by itself. The point of educational testing is to help education.

chapters.author_definition: A test that is technically accurate in its measurements but harmful in its practical effects on educational behavior and curriculum.
chapters.source_passage: They are “consequentially invalid.” They do more harm than good.

chapters.author_definition: The idea that children should construct their own learning, which Hirsch claims is often incorrectly attributed to Piaget's support for stage-based development.
chapters.source_passage: modern cognitive science, which is said to favor “constructivism” (encouraging children to construct their own learning—on the pattern of the unwinding ball of string)

chapters.author_definition: The failure of standards-makers to specify concrete knowledge or topics in reading curricula due to a fear of the political controversy that follows defining shared knowledge.
Educational standards that avoid specifying grade-by-grade knowledge and instead focus on abstract skills to give the appearance of rigor without requiring a shared curriculum.
chapters.source_passage: The substance of the reading curriculum had been described in the content-evading state standards as consisting of all-purpose techniques

chapters.author_definition: Educational standards that specify skills to be mastered but do not name the specific factual content or topics to be taught at each grade level.
A conceptual framework in Grade 5 science used to study the biological progression from germination to fruition and the birth to growth of animals.
The widely recognized decline in educational achievement and increase in inequality in France resulting from individualistic pedagogical theories.
An educational goal originating in Romanticism that prioritizes the child's internal imaginative development over communal knowledge.
A romantic or 'tribal' view of language and culture that treats social groups as separate, unchanging 'cells' that cannot or should not be integrated into a common public sphere.
A reproach used to discourage the teaching of a communal, shared curriculum, which Hirsch argues is misplaced when applied to the public sphere of a democratic 'internal empire.'
chapters.source_passage: We must not be intimidated by the reproach that imparting the communal knowledge of the public sphere is “cultural imperialism.”

chapters.author_definition: The nationally shared knowledge of the public sphere and the standard language that allows citizens to participate equally in a democracy.
The process by which a coherent national curriculum is broken down into random, non-cumulative, or localized subject matter, compromising student success.
Tests that are composed of passages taken from specific knowledge domains taught in a local core curriculum.
Standardized tests that measure general knowledge or skills without regard for the specific content taught in the classroom curriculum.
chapters.source_passage: Tests that are curriculum-blind cannot gauge how well a curriculum has been imparted.

chapters.author_definition: The body of shared, taken-for-granted knowledge that enables a national language to be deployed effectively among strangers.
A label used by educators to imply that certain academic topics are taught prematurely and should be withheld until a child reaches a certain maturational age.
chapters.source_passage: To label a topic “developmentally inappropriate” implies that it is being taught prematurely, like feeding small children oysters Rockefeller when they should be eating oatmeal.

chapters.author_definition: A traditional French classroom exercise requiring students to write spoken text with perfect spelling and punctuation; cited by Hirsch as an effective tool for teaching standard language forms.
A practice that attempts to match materials to each student's presumed ability level or 'learning style,' which Hirsch argues results in a 'dumbed down' and incoherent curriculum.
The chemical process of creating a solution, used to teach principles of weight conservation and solubility limits relative to temperature.
A hypothetical universal skill, such as critical thinking, that exists independently of specific subject-matter knowledge.
The French universal preschool system which emphasizes academic activities, a knowledge-based curriculum, and social training, delivered by highly trained teachers.
chapters.source_passage: The popular full-day programs emphasize academic activities... French preschool teachers have the same training, civil service status, and salaries as primary school teachers.

chapters.author_definition: The view that schooling is a process of initiating children into the values, aesthetics, and common knowledge of the public sphere.
chapters.source_passage: an indication of their view that education is socialization.

chapters.author_definition: A collaborative practice where teachers support each other's mastery of specific content knowledge and share lesson plans within a unified curriculum framework.
The human interaction between teacher and young student that the author argues is a non-negotiable requirement for primary education and cannot be replicated by technology.
Educational standards that specify formal skills (like finding the main idea) without specifying the substantive content or knowledge that students should learn.
A curriculum that provides the specific knowledge required to use language effectively and participate in the public sphere.
A derogatory term used by French progressives (Jospin, Bourdieu) to describe a knowledge-rich curriculum focused on 'piling up facts.'
chapters.source_passage: he had instructed the national council to avoid “mere encyclopedism” and the “piling up of facts.”

chapters.author_definition: A national policy where a common curriculum is mandated for all children, supplemented by extra resources for low-achieving students to ensure small differences in performance between schools.
chapters.source_passage: Sweden followed what he calls “equality-oriented centralism,” with extra resources allocated to low achieving students.

chapters.author_definition: A legislative principle in Sweden that replaced the common curriculum, mandating that education be adapted to each individual child's unique circumstances, background, and earlier experiences.
chapters.source_passage: A principle of “equivalence” was legislated to replace the common curriculum... Education should be adapted to each pupil’s circumstances and needs.

chapters.author_definition: Deep domain knowledge that experts use to perform tasks that novices mistake for general skill.

concepts.term: Exclusive trammels of the written word

chapters.author_definition: The pedagogical constraint of limiting a child's learning only to what they are currently able to decode and read themselves.

A curriculum model based on the belief that children's learning should start with their immediate personal environment and gradually expand outward, which Hirsch argues is based on ideology rather than cognitive science.
A state of performance where form and content, knowledge and skill, are inherently fused and cannot be easily dissociated in the mind.
chapters.source_passage: expertise implies an inherent fusion of form and content, knowledge and skill that can’t easily be dissociated in the human mind.

chapters.author_definition: The inherent fairness and educational productivity of a test that measures the specific content that students were actually taught in their curriculum.
The disappearance of early academic advantages, such as IQ or vocabulary gains from preschool, after several years of primary schooling.
The state of American classrooms where instruction and testing are based on the false premise that reading comprehension is a content-independent skill.
Content-independent strategies like 'summarizing', 'questioning the author', or 'finding the main idea' that are incorrectly believed to be the keys to reading comprehension.
The fourth stage of Piaget's theory, characterized by the ability to perform abstract logical reasoning independent of concrete experience.
chapters.source_passage: Upon reaching that final stage, a mature child or an adult should be able to solve the following problem: If A is bigger than B, and B is bigger than C, is A bigger or smaller than C or the same?

chapters.author_definition: The shared language of the public sphere used to communicate with strangers and large audiences, typically taught in schools.
A curriculum that lacks a coherent, sequential build-up of knowledge because it views specific content as merely a 'vehicle' for practicing general skills.
A vague, quasi-pantheistic belief system derived from Friedrich Froebel that views the child as a divine being and resists systematic, factual instruction in favor of 'natural' unfolding.
The view that reading is a transferable set of techniques or strategies that can be applied to any content regardless of the reader's background knowledge.
chapters.source_passage: The mistaken conception of reading as a general skill has led to a frantic short-term, quick-fix view of education

chapters.author_definition: The overarching idea that all children in a democracy deserve access to shared, enabling knowledge regardless of their home culture.
A modern educational concept emphasizing perseverance, used by the author as evidence of the return of 'training' models of education.
Specific pedagogical requirements or constraints that must be followed when teaching the specified scientific content to ensure uniform delivery.
A curriculum that lacks coherence and prioritizes general skills over specific, domain-based content.
The conscious, meticulous planning of knowledge domains (arithmetic, history, etc.) that underlies joyful, child-friendly preschool activities.
chapters.source_passage: Each child is engaged and makes progress because of this well-mapped-out hidden curriculum, clothed in the same garb of joyful activities that you will find in the jolliest and most expensive naturalistic preschool.

chapters.author_definition: A subject, like reading, that relies heavily on knowledge and vocabulary acquired in the domestic environment, making it more sensitive to socio-economic disparities unless the school compensates with a knowledge-rich curriculum.
The assumption that imagination is always positive and the primary goal of development, contrasting with the communal/factual goal of education.
A distinction borrowed from Coleridge where 'imagination' is a sacred, creative organ of divine insight, while 'fancy' is a lower, mechanical process of combining existing experiences.
chapters.source_passage: It was important to distinguish the two, because the good imaginations of the child had to be distinguished from the bad ones (fancy); only the good ones were sacred and authentic.

chapters.author_definition: The conversion of the scientific theory of evolution into a developmental theory where history and nature are grounded in a Divine goal.
The unconscious acquisition of vocabulary that occurs automatically when a student encounters new words within a familiar context.
The state of a curriculum where the lack of common, sequential content prevents teachers from knowing what students have already learned.
The act of a student reading a book on their own, which may be assigned by a teacher for communal discussion later, rather than just books they select themselves.
chapters.source_passage: independent reading doesn’t necessarily mean that; it can also mean reading books on one’s own that have been assigned and are then later discussed communally

chapters.author_definition: The belief, traced to Dewey, that education must be controlled at every point by the child's specific psychological capacities, interests, and habits.
The author's definition of education as the process of bringing children into the culture and knowledge of the community, as practiced since the dawn of human social groups.
Educational factors specifically located within the institution of the school—primarily curriculum quality and breadth—rather than external social or economic factors.
The dominant status of child-centered and skill-centric doctrines in American education, which prevents the consideration or adoption of knowledge-based curricula.
chapters.source_passage: because such an approach contradicts the child-centered, skill-centric doctrines that are still an intellectual monopoly in our education world.

chapters.author_definition: The scientific process where a finding is validated by being derived from multiple, completely independent approaches.
The specific shared knowledge and vocabulary that functions as a requirement for entry into the middle-class income level.
Educational programs (like Direct Instruction or Success for All) that focus heavily on the mechanics of reading and scripted curricula, but treat knowledge content as secondary to skill acquisition.
chapters.source_passage: That same view—that content is basically a vehicle for skills—also characterizes scripted programs like Direct Instruction and Success for All.

chapters.author_definition: A nation conceived as a union of quasi-sovereign states intended to protect security and prosperity while leaving private affairs alone, requiring shared 'systematic ideas' to function.
The phenomenon where efforts to raise test scores through drills actually cause scores to drop by displacing the knowledge building required for mature reading.
The deliberate, invented standardized English language developed by Samuel Johnson and Noah Webster to create a shared national culture.
The unspoken set of knowledge and vocabulary shared by all competent readers and writers in the public sphere, distinct from a literary canon.
chapters.source_passage: On the other hand, there is a canon of knowledge and vocabulary known to all highly competent readers and writers. That is axiomatic; possessing that knowledge is what makes them competent.

chapters.author_definition: The concept that existing knowledge acts as an investment that enables the acquisition of new information, similar to financial capital.
The true nature of the achievement gap, where disparities in academic performance are actually disparities in the specific knowledge and language children possess.
A cumulative, coherent, and multiyear plan of specific content learning that serves as the foundation for language competence and test performance.
Traditional, shared knowledge that provides an individual with entrée to the existing structures of success and influence in a society.
Segments of the Common Core Standards that explicitly recognize the need for a content-rich, intentionally structured curriculum to build knowledge.
Classroom activities focused on abstract skills (like finding the main idea) that take up time better spent on subject-matter learning.
A naturalistic method of teaching reading that focuses on whole words or meanings rather than explicit phonics, which the author links to reading failure.
The French term for progressive education, characterized by child-centeredness, naturalistic teaching, and the deemphasis of rote memorization.
The deliberate attempt to change or expand the shared knowledge and vocabulary of a nation through schools and media.
The mastery of standard American English and the traditional knowledge base that enables success in contemporary society.

A stage in word acquisition where past experiences have formed associations and narrowed possibilities for a word's meaning, paving the way for later confident knowledge, even if not yet detectable by tests.
Word knowledge and associations that are in the process of being formed but are not yet robust enough to be captured by standard, non-detailed vocabulary tests.
An all-purpose skill that is prioritized in progressive pedagogy over the instruction of specific academic content or information.
chapters.source_passage: The chief aim was the imparting of all-purpose skills like “learning to learn.”

chapters.author_definition: A system where books are rated by difficulty level based on technical formulas (sentence length/word rarity) and students are restricted to reading books at their 'correct' level.
A system where books are categorized by difficulty levels (often Lexile) and children are restricted to reading within their assigned level.
A primary content domain in the Japanese science curriculum covering biological structures, environments, and basic meteorology/astronomy (sun, moon, stars).
The stable core of language and knowledge that does not change quickly despite the addition of new 'slang' or technical terms.
chapters.source_passage: At the center of the amoeba there is a nucleus that does not readily change shape or constitution.

chapters.author_definition: Historically, all excellent and memorable writing across genres; later restricted by Romanticism to imaginative fiction and poetry.
The intellectual discipline of following the current of thought and evidence wherever it leads, even when it contradicts one's prior earnest support or party affiliation.
A pejorative term used by reformers to describe traditional, uniform, grade-by-grade curricula that required all students to learn the same content at the same time.
chapters.source_passage: Older forms of “lockstep” education were not only outmoded but also unfair.

chapters.author_definition: A 1989 French education law that replaced the national curriculum with locally determined, individualized curriculums focused on student-centered pedagogy.
The multi-year, cumulative process of building vocabulary and domain knowledge which eventually results in high reading proficiency.
Originally defined by Necker de Saussure in 1828 to describe the onward, natural stages of a child's development from birth.
A primary content domain in the Japanese science curriculum covering physical properties, forces (wind/rubber), light, magnetism, and electricity.
The ability to understand the complex language and specialized vocabulary found in newspapers, textbooks, and political speeches (e.g., Keynes's 'The Economic Consequences of the Peace').
chapters.source_passage: They can read Stupendous Sports Stadiums, but not The Economic Consequences of the Peace.

chapters.author_definition: The ability to sound out words fast and effortlessly (decoding and fluency), as opposed to higher-level comprehension.
chapters.source_passage: the testing regimens have clearly helped improve the mechanics of early reading (that is, the ability to sound out fast and effortlessly)—an important gain.

chapters.author_definition: Techniques like inference-making, predicting, and finding the main idea that are treated as transferable skills independent of subject matter.
chapters.source_passage: The substance of the reading curriculum had been described in the content-evading state standards as consisting of all-purpose techniques like inference making, predicting, and main-idea finding.

chapters.author_definition: The requirement that science instruction align with broader ethical goals, specifically cultivating an attitude of cherishing and conserving nature.
A quasi-whole-language approach to reading (known in English as 'balanced literacy') that occasionally incorporates some phonics but remains primarily naturalistic.
The educational overemphasis on innate, genetic components of learning at the expense of environmental factors like shared knowledge.
The overemphasis on innate, genetic components of learning and ability, which Hirsch argues is an implicit part of child-centered theories.
Pedagogical methods (often associated with whole-language) that prioritize an 'unfolding' of skills over direct instruction in phonics, grammar, or spelling.
The belief that early education should be a natural 'unfolding' of the child's inherent nature, prioritizing the child's internal world over external training.
chapters.source_passage: The romantic aim of education is to correct civilization through nature.

chapters.author_definition: A child-centered method of teaching reading that avoids explicit phonics and focuses on 'natural' engagement with text; described as failing in the French context.
A strand of Romanticism, labeled by M. H. Abrams, where religious impulses are translated into modern, acceptable, and quasi-secular terms such as 'Nature' or 'Spirit.'
An 18th-century nonsectarian religious movement that emphasized nature and reason over the sectarian dogmas that caused the wars of the 17th century.
A philosophical imposition suggesting that historical and educational progress is divinely mandated and therefore unstoppable/inevitable.
A curriculum style that limits instruction to surface-level observations of things students already know (e.g., 'water is wet') without integrating them into scientific concepts or a cumulative plan.
chapters.source_passage: Note that this document stays on the non-analytical phenomenal level, which is the antithesis of science.

chapters.author_definition: A trifling or ineffective result, used to describe the predicted impact of future skill-based Common Core testing.
A principle (borrowed from Woodrow Wilson) that school curricula should be published, explicit, and transparent regarding both content and the values they aim to teach.
chapters.source_passage: “Open covenants openly arrived at” should be the watchword of the published school curriculum.

chapters.author_definition: The root idea of child-centered education, identifying God with nature, thus rendering the natural child 'holy.'

The educational theory that by prioritizing a child's individual interests early on and neglecting a shared curriculum, schools actually restrict the child's future freedom and capacity to develop a unique individual identity.
The total group of students (e.g., all fifth-graders in the public sector) from which a representative sample is drawn.
The characteristic of having a specific, unique set of shared elements; Hirsch argues that democracies are 'particularistic' because they rely on a specific shared language.
A Byronic and Romantic symbol for the preference for untamed, wild nature over civilized paths and social order.
The cloud of taken-for-granted, unsaid knowledge shared by a language community that makes communication possible.
The integration of different disciplines of knowledge (art, history, literature) so they reinforce each other and speed up learning.
An early-nineteenth-century Middle European ideology (the 'new education') that took root in the US in the 1870s and focuses on natural development over shared social curricula.
chapters.source_passage: In Central Europe it was called “the new education,” and in America it was called “progressive education.”

chapters.author_definition: A technique designed to replicate hands-on, real-world experiences inside the school based on the principle of naturalism.
A specific, locally determined emphasis in French schools introduced by the 1989 law to individualize the curriculum.
The categorization of liquids into alkaline, acid, and neutral types and their capacity to dissolve gases or react with metals.
The romantic or ideological faith that the natural development of the individual child (or the market) will lead to optimal outcomes without central guidance or shared curriculum.
chapters.source_passage: Behind this move lay the same kind of thinking about following the providential impulses of the individual child, to which Sweden added the further idea of following the providential workings of the free market.

chapters.author_definition: The use of elaborate mathematical formulas and excessive decimal places to create an appearance of scientific exactitude where none exists.
The theory that stories and narrative structures are naturally suited to the human brain's processing mechanisms, making them the most effective way to transmit information.
A state that is not a 'natural unfolding' but is instead determined by the external education and the knowledge base a child has acquired.
chapters.source_passage: The stage of development—of readiness—is not entirely a natural unfolding.

chapters.author_definition: The act of making hypotheses about word meanings and building meaning from both text and prior knowledge; specifically, NOT a general skill.
A proxy for the total accumulated knowledge and vocabulary a student has acquired since birth, serving as a predictor of life outcomes.
The comprehension of natural events achieved when pupils connect abstract learning outcomes to their everyday life and experiential activities.
The actual knowledge and content that students learn, which Hirsch argues is the true measure of educational success.
chapters.source_passage: Educational success is defined by what students learn—the received curriculum.

chapters.author_definition: A state of exhaustion or desperation among policymakers and the public after decades of educational reform strategies (like back-to-basics or NCLB) have failed to produce results.
The scientific concept that physical materials and phenomena follow predictable, discoverable rules.
The predictable physical relationship between the force point, the strength, and the power required to tilt or balance a lever.
A systematic perspective in the Grade 5 curriculum focusing on identifying the consistent causes and factors (time, amount, temperature) that govern changes in substances and objects.
The traditional French centralized system based on a common curriculum, designed during the Revolution to create national unity and meritocratic opportunity.
chapters.source_passage: French republican education was a device for reproducing existing social stratifications... What appeared... as being rigid and constraining... had in fact been instituted to help ensure that all children were offered an equal chance in life.

chapters.author_definition: A proposed administrative strategy allowing a district to pause standardized skills-based testing to implement and evaluate a content-rich curriculum transition.

concepts.term: Resource person / Guide on the side

chapters.author_definition: A shift in the teacher's role from a lecturer/instructor to a facilitator of student-led discovery, mandated by progressive education colleges.
chapters.source_passage: Education colleges required a teacher to be called a “resource person,” a guide on the side.

chapters.author_definition: A form of multicultural activism that, while well-intentioned, risks economic exclusion by neglecting the necessity of shared national knowledge.
The belief that education should be a process of natural growth and maturation rather than specific training or content acquisition.
The author's metaphor for current educational standards that are so vague they can be interpreted in any way by the viewer, lacking objective content.
A sampling plan where schools are selected at random and all students within those schools participate in the evaluation.
A subject, like mathematics, whose content is primarily acquired within the formal schooling environment, making its outcomes more uniformly dependent on school quality.
A concept from Antonio Gramsci describing a unitary common school focused on general culture rather than narrow vocational or skill training.
Technical vocabulary and frameworks used by pupils to formally explain observed natural events and phenomena, rather than relying on informal or everyday language alone.
A Hegelian concept used by Dewey to describe how the 'Universal Mind' or deity realizes itself through the historical development of finite individuals (children).
The belief among educators and parents that their efforts can produce successful results, often triggered by seeing tangible evidence of curricular success.
chapters.source_passage: When people see the evidence of success with their own eyes... they gain a sense of efficacy.

chapters.author_definition: A metaphor for the elementary school as a shared, universal communicative environment that all children attend simultaneously.
Brilliant but misleading catchphrases used by the educational establishment to maintain the status quo and discourage content-based reforms.
chapters.source_passage: the highly definite viewpoints and brilliant slogans of skill-centric doctrine.

chapters.author_definition: The false belief that technology and computers can transform primary education by replacing the need for a knowledge-coherent curriculum.
chapters.source_passage: Those who hope to find amelioration of the “teacher quality problem” through the use of computers and “blended learning” may be fostering yet another skills delusion.

chapters.author_definition: The necessary transmission of communal knowledge and standard language that is essential to human development.
The theory by Bourdieu that education systems function to maintain and transmit existing social class structures and inequalities.
The pessimistic belief that a child's educational outcomes are entirely dictated by their family background and poverty, rendering school improvements largely futile.
chapters.source_passage: Coleman was distressed by both the minimal equity effects of current schools and by the self-protective reaction to his report by the education community. The data actually implied... schools could indeed matter a great deal.

chapters.author_definition: Periodic, detailed national assessments of student competencies and demographics conducted by the French Ministry of Education to gauge school effectiveness.
A 'curious aberration' in educational theory that views the child's brain as a naturally unwinding ball of string rather than something needing active socialization and formation.
chapters.source_passage: It produced curious aberrations like ‘spontaneity,’ which supposes that the child’s brain is like a ball of string that the teacher should help to unwind.

chapters.author_definition: The statistical distance (measured in standard deviations) between the performance of the top-performing demographic groups and the bottom-performing demographic groups.
Used by Hirsch as a common magnitude (like inches or millimeters) to compare across different test scales to show the significance of score declines.
chapters.source_passage: To many it is a confusing term in this context... it is used chiefly as a common magnitude like inches or millimeters, to put various test score scales on the same metric.

chapters.author_definition: English that is substantially uniform in spelling, grammar, pronunciation, and vocabulary across a nation.
A pejorative term used by Common Core critics to describe those who support standardized informational text requirements.
The continued momentum of content-coherent schooling following an early 'head start' to prevent academic fadeout.
A contextual effectiveness that arises from working within a coherent school system and curriculum, rather than an innate or permanent trait of an individual.
chapters.source_passage: The effectiveness of a teacher is not some inherent competence, as the phrase teacher quality suggests. Teacher effectiveness is contextual.

chapters.author_definition: A mechanism intended to prevent censorship of opinion, which should not serve as a shield for poor performance or low productivity.
chapters.source_passage: Tenure protections at universities were instituted to avoid censorship of opinion. But even in universities there is no tenure protection for “failure to meet a specified norm of performance or productivity”

chapters.author_definition: A normative standard where a test is only fair if it measures knowledge and vocabulary that the school has explicitly been mandated to teach.
A technical measure of text difficulty (based on factors like sentence length and word frequency) that is often used as a substitute for defining specific content knowledge.
The hypothesis that any subject can be taught effectively in some intellectually honest form to any child at any stage of development.
A term and book title by Francois-Xavier Bellamy referring to the generation of students deprived of their cultural heritage due to the anti-transmission ideology in schools.
The national standards document established by the Jospin law, characterized by Hirsch as being too short and vague to provide practical educational guidance.
A French educational concept introduced in 2005 meant to be a required base of learning for all children, but critiqued by Hirsch as too vague to be useful.
A visual and theoretical representation of the fact that listening comprehension exceeds reading comprehension until late middle school.
Cooperation, coherence, and cumulativeness; the curricular and organizational features that make teachers more effective collectively.
chapters.source_passage: The three Cs—cooperation, coherence, and cumulativeness—yield a bigger boost than the most brilliant efforts of teachers working individually

chapters.author_definition: The false belief that education should focus on general, content-independent skills rather than the transmission of a common body of knowledge.
A curricular characteristic where specific content and expertise levels are clearly defined for each point in the sequence, informing all stakeholders of what is learned and what remains.
A content-free K-3 social studies curriculum that focuses exclusively on the child's immediate surroundings (family, school, neighborhood) rather than history, biography, or legends.
chapters.source_passage: This curriculum of “me, my family, my school, my community” now dominates the early grades in American public education. It contains no mythology, legends, biographies, hero tales, or great events in the life of this nation or any other. It is tot sociology.

chapters.author_definition: A concept of America as a nation that incorporates all sexes, colors, and conditions into a unified community, overcoming natural tribal instincts.
The right of parents to have a high-quality neighborhood school, rather than just the right to transport a child to a different school elsewhere.
The root meaning of 'development,' implying that child growth is a natural process of internal purposes manifesting in the world.
The educational principle of offering every child the exact same curriculum and educational opportunities in their early years to ensure equity.
A statistical method of evaluating teacher effectiveness based on student test score gains from one year to the next.
chapters.source_passage: The theory is that such a system will energize teachers, boost their performance, and bring highly qualified people into the profession.

chapters.author_definition: The idea that early knowledge provides a sticky base to which subsequent knowledge can more easily adhere.
chapters.source_passage: The early knowledge advantage that has been gained by fortunate students is like Velcro; it is a base to which further knowledge sticks more readily.

chapters.author_definition: The cognitive process of determining what a text is saying, which is facilitated by familiarity with the domain, freeing up working memory.
chapters.source_passage: verbal comprehension is a form of problem solving. Topic familiarity makes the task of forming a situation model fast and accurate, leaving more space in working memory to figure out word relations

chapters.author_definition: The disparity in word knowledge between demographic groups, which Hirsch identifies as the molecular mechanism and primary driver of the reading comprehension gap.
The possession of a broad and deep range of factual knowledge stored in long-term memory, which serves as the prerequisite for critical thinking.
The nuanced meanings and associations of words that are accurately built up through multiple exposures in context.
The national scandal and policy soul-searching in Germany following the release of poor PISA results in 2000, which led to the adoption of aligned, specific curricula.
The general curricular principle of a specific, communal, grade-by-grade curriculum, as opposed to the specific trademarked 'Core Knowledge Sequence' program.
chapters.source_passage: I call the general principle 'lowercase core knowledge.' So understood, the scientific validation of core knowledge does not depend on the willingness of the educational research community to authenticate its effectiveness with large-scale random-assignment studies.

chapters.author_definition: French teacher-training institutes where progressive educational theories were disseminated to new teachers starting in the 1960s.

cases.name: PISA Top Performers (Korea, Japan, Canada, Finland)

chapters.description: Nations cited for having schools that successfully accommodate individual students within a framework of shared curriculum topics, rather than creating separate curricula for each child.
key_passages: ...accommodating shared curriculum topics for each child as the best schools do in the community-centered schools of Finland and Japan...

chapters.description: International comparison of problem-solving abilities showing that nations with communal, content-set curricula produce students who perform significantly better than American students on creative problem-solving tests.
key_passages: The most recent PISA scores for the problem-solving abilities of fifteen-year-olds place American students well below Korea, Japan, Canada, and Finland, nations with set, communal curricula in early grades
contestable_aspects: Whether the success of these nations is due to curriculum or other socio-cultural factors.

chapters.description: Used as an example of a content-cumulative system where a specific and coherent curriculum allows average teachers to be highly effective because they build on the work of previous grades.
key_passages: it is impossible for a superb teacher to be as effective as a merely average teacher is in the content-cumulative Japanese elementary school.

chapters.description: Japan is cited as a system where teachers do not face massive discrepancies in student preparation because they follow a specific and coherent curriculum.
key_passages: the Japanese teacher does not. In a system with a specific and coherent curriculum, the work of each teacher builds on the work of teachers who came before.

chapters.description: A Japanese fourth-grade classroom where a teacher used a specific analogy to solve a student's conceptual hurdle, cited as an example of the effectiveness of collaborative planning and common curriculum.
key_passages: One especially vivid story about collaboration in the Japanese elementary school was told to me directly by the late Professor Harold Stevenson... A student was having grave difficulty with a math problem and its concepts. After allowing the student to work on it for a short time, the teacher quietly made a surprising analogy

chapters.description: Canadian provinces use ancillary and illustrative materials to provide explicit curricular guidelines, effectively creating a uniform curriculum while maintaining the appearance of local autonomy and avoiding 'dictatorial' appearances.
key_passages: The official standards are not as explicit as those of, say, Japan. But there are ancillary materials that are highly explicit... ancillary illustrative materials gently induce the reality of arbitrary dictatorial impositions—a subtle approach that might well be tried elsewhere.
contestable_aspects: Whether ancillary materials actually function as a 'uniform curriculum' in practice.

chapters.description: Nations cited as examples where national, well-rounded curricula result in high performance and parental satisfaction without the need for alternative governance like charter schools.
key_passages: That’s why parents in Finland and Japan have seen no reason to institute charter schools.
It happens also in Finland and Japan where Core Knowledge–style schools are universal.

chapters.description: Nations cited as examples of the 'best national curriculums' where topics are sequenced over years and integrated across disciplines, resulting in superior PISA scores.
key_passages: the arrangement of the best national curriculums like those of Japan and Korea, whose efficacy is manifest in the PISA scores.

chapters.description: The Japanese curriculum is cited as a gold standard that is field-tested, topic-specific, and well-rounded, including arts, music, and morality alongside math and science. It consistently produces top-tier international results.
key_passages: Japan does a superb job under this criterion, and so did France in former times.
For example, the Japanese elementary curriculum, which consistently scores at the top in all subjects, exhibits all those virtues.

chapters.description: After American occupiers left, Japanese schools rejected American-style local control and returned to a specific national curriculum, which Hirsch claims led to their current high PISA rankings.
key_passages: Japan is a superb example, because right after the American occupiers left, Japanese schools abandoned American-style local control of elementary curricula and returned to a highly specific national curriculum on the grounds that it would work better in all respects.

chapters.description: Following WWII, the US occupation forces intervened in Japanese education to install 'democratic' values and decentralize the system by putting control in the hands of municipalities. The Japanese public, finding that decentralization was ineffective and that they valued a common curriculum and moral training, rejected these reforms after the Americans left in 1952 and returned to a centralized system.
key_passages: After World War II, when the Americans occupied Japan from 1945 to 1952, they intervened in the school system. They wanted Japanese children to learn democratic values.
The Americans also introduced the local control idea, taking control of the schools and curriculum away from the central government and putting it into the hands of the municipalities.

They returned control of the schools to the central government, primarily because decentralization did not work very well, and secondly because they thought both moral training and a common curriculum were sound educational ideas.
contestable_aspects: Whether decentralization 'did not work very well' or was simply culturally incompatible.
The characterization of the American intervention as an attempt at indoctrination vs. democratization.

chapters.description: The Japanese Grade 3 curriculum focuses on foundational physical properties (weight, magnets, circuits) and biological structures (insect and plant parts). It requires hands-on involvement, such as raising living things and building materials.
key_passages: To develop pupils’ ideas about properties of objects by examining weights and volumes
the body parts consist of the head, thorax, and abdomen
pupils must make at least three kinds of learning materials

chapters.description: A highly detailed and prescriptive national curriculum used as an example of domain-specific, knowledge-rich schooling. It specifies exactly which phenomena (air pressure, circuits, solubility, pendulum movement) should be taught and what specific facts students should derive from experiments.
key_passages: To develop pupils’ ideas about the properties of air and water by exploring the changes in their volume and pressure
the time taken for a weight on a string to swing back and forth does not change if the weight changes, but it does change when the length of the string changes.
With regard to item (2) in “B. Life/the Earth” in Content, the activities of at least two animals and the growth of at least two plants should be observed over the course of one year.
contestable_aspects: Whether such a rigid national curriculum stifles local teacher creativity
Whether the focus on specific scientific facts over broader inquiry processes is the optimal way to teach science

chapters.description: A highly specific, content-driven national curriculum that mandates identical scientific knowledge for all students in Japan. It features distinct transitions from Grade 5 (focusing on dissolution, electromagnetism, and plant biology) to Grade 6 (focusing on combustion and aqueous solution chemistry).
key_passages: The Japanese Early Science Curriculum... Grade 5... Grade 6
With regard to 'A. Matters/Energy' in Content, pupils must make at least two kinds of learning materials.

chapters.description: A curriculum characterized by deep, domain-specific knowledge in physics (matter/energy) and biology/earth science. It emphasizes the 'regularity' of physical laws, the chemical mechanisms of life and fire, and a systematic study of the Earth and space. It requires hands-on creation of objects and the learning of precise anatomical and geological terminology.
key_passages: To develop pupils’ ideas about the mechanism of combustion, by burning objects and by exploring the changes in them
lungs, the stomach, small intestines, large intestines, liver, kidneys, and heart should be dealt with as major organs.
Some considerations should be given to consolidating scientific knowledge and concepts and to developing scientific perspectives and ideas

chapters.description: The administrative and pedagogical guidelines issued for Japanese schools, which emphasize a balance between nature-based experience and the rigorous acquisition of specific scientific terminology and concepts.
key_passages: In designing the syllabus, consideration should be given to the following: (1) Some considerations should be given to consolidating scientific knowledge and concepts and to developing scientific perspectives and ideas by enriching observation, experiments, experience in nature, and scientific experience
It is necessary to encourage every pupil to take initiatives in solving problems, to make connections between the outcomes of learning and everyday life
contestable_aspects: The effectiveness of integrating moral education with scientific instruction
The degree to which specified lists of organs and rocks improve scientific literacy compared to broader conceptual teaching

cases.name: United States, France, and Sweden comparison

chapters.description: A period where France maintained a highly uniform national curriculum based on the egalitarian principles of the Revolution, ensuring all children, regardless of background, received the same instruction.
key_passages: For many decades the French elementary school had been the pride and the terror of the young, with every child, rich or poor, having to undergo the very same rigors under the same national curriculum.
The egalitarian impulse of this uniformity was expressed early in the Revolution by Condorcet... and re-expressed in the nineteenth century by Jules Ferry.

chapters.description: The passage of a law that radically decentralized French education, replacing the national curriculum with local 'projets' and shifting focus to individual interests and general skills.
key_passages: France passed a radical new education law—the loi Jospin—requiring all elementary schools to cease teaching the national curriculum and begin teaching locally determined curriculums.
The new law reflected those ideas: more attention was to be paid to the individuality of each student, to his or her native abilities, interests, and home culture.

chapters.description: A case where schools declined suddenly after a substantive curriculum was abandoned and replaced by skill-based educational theories, despite having highly qualified teachers.
key_passages: even with a staff of well-trained, highly qualified, and well-educated teachers, the schools can suddenly decline when a substantive curriculum is abandoned, and fallacious ideas about skills begin to dominate.
contestable_aspects: Whether the decline was caused by curricular changes or other socio-economic factors.

chapters.description: In the 1980s, French preschools were proven to narrow the achievement gap by age ten. However, after the loi Jospin (Jospin law) of 1990 altered the elementary school curriculum, these same preschools ceased to narrow the gap, and fadeout appeared. This serves as a natural experiment showing the elementary school, not the preschool, was the variable causing fadeout.
key_passages: In the 1980s, research proved that French preschools had not only avoided fadeout but by age ten had greatly narrowed the achievement gap. But after the radical loi Jospin (“Jospin law,” described in chapter 7) went into effect in 1990, the excellent French preschools suddenly ceased narrowing the achievement gap. Preschool fadeout had come to France. The excellent preschools had not changed; only the elementary schools had.

chapters.description: The author compares the reading outcomes of disadvantaged students in the U.S. against other nations, asserting that countries that avoid hyper-individualistic methods achieve better results.
key_passages: right now in the United States we are not improving the reading comprehension of disadvantaged students as well as other nations that don’t follow these hyper-individualistic methods.

chapters.description: The author describes the French school system as a 'textbook case' where gaps were originally narrow due to a national curriculum but widened significantly after the system was changed to a less communal scheme.
key_passages: The French school system is a textbook case in which the gaps had been narrowed, but were subsequently enormously widened when France abandoned a national curriculum in favor of a less communal scheme.

chapters.description: France implemented major educational reforms in 1989 that adopted skill-based and individualized principles (the 'loi Jospin'), abandoning its previously effective national curriculum. According to 2007 data, this led to a significant decline in student verbal achievement and a sharp decrease in educational equity, transforming the system from one of the world's best to one of its worst among large nations.
key_passages: The data showed another educational system that, like our own, had adopted skill-based, individualized, educational principles, and subsequently declined from being among the best and most equitable large school systems in the world to being one of the worst and least equitable.
France had experienced a fully documented repetition of the US decline.
contestable_aspects: The degree to which the 1989 reforms are solely responsible for the decline vs other social factors
The characterization of the decline as a 'military defeat' style debacle

chapters.description: Following decades of gradual 'progressive' curriculum reforms starting in the 1930s, US verbal scores across all major assessments (SAT, ACT, Iowa tests) plummeted between 1960 and 1980. This decline occurred across all socioeconomic groups and was not primarily caused by changes in the demographics of test-takers.
key_passages: SAT verbal scores had plummeted half a standard deviation in the twenty years between 1960 and 1980.
The origins of our decline can be clearly traced to our having made similar intellectual mistakes half a century before.

chapters.description: A centrally controlled educational system with a specific national curriculum and universal preschool. It achieved high average scores and high equity, effectively educating a diverse and immigrant population.
key_passages: In the 1980s, France could celebrate (with Sweden) the best large school systems in Europe.
France exhibited the highest average student achievement with the greatest equity of any large, diverse country. It educated immigrants better than any other European nation.

chapters.description: A radical legal reform in France that decentralized the school system and abandoned the national curriculum in favor of individualized learning, following decades of progressive intellectual influence in teacher-training schools.
key_passages: the intellectual preparation for the catastrophic loi Jospin of 1989 had been brewing in French education schools since the 1960s.
Because French schooling was centrally controlled, it decentralized itself in 1989 in a wrenching, centralized manner.

chapters.description: A radical shift in the French education system where the traditional national curriculum was replaced by a decentralized, child-centered system. The reform created twenty-six autonomous districts and twenty-eight new teacher colleges to train educators in progressive pedagogy. It prioritized 'skills' over 'facts' and allowed schools to tailor curricula to local and individual characteristics.
key_passages: just twelve years later, in 1989, France completely overthrew that tradition by passing a law saying in effect that each child would go to a different primary school and middle school.

Structurally, a radical feature of the new law was decentralization. Instead of having everything emanate from Paris, metropolitan France would henceforth be divided into twenty-six autonomous school districts.
The emphasis was to be on skills, not facts, based on the statement in the Bourdieu-Gros Report that all-purpose skills are far more important than the piling up of knowledge.
contestable_aspects: The characterization of 'skills over facts' as the primary cause of later decline.
The description of IUFMs (education colleges) as purely for 'indoctrination' in progressive theory.

chapters.description: A radical legislative shift in French education that moved away from a uniform national curriculum to an individualized, child-centered system. It decentralized curriculum planning to individual schools and emphasized skills over knowledge content.
key_passages: The very preamble of the new 1989 law made clear that French education was not going to be “lockstep” any longer. It was going to be child-centered.
Pupils and students will elaborate their own curricula according to their own aspirations and capacities.

chapters.description: The historical development of schools intended to unify a diverse population ('out of many one') through shared knowledge.
key_passages: Historically, the United States invented both the modern democratic nation and the common school that sustained it.

chapters.description: Whole-nation comparisons used to show that a knowledge-based curriculum produces better verbal outcomes than a skills-based curriculum.
key_passages: the verbal scores of whole nations, including the United States, France, and Sweden, which have massively tried the two different kinds of curriculum—show that the knowledge curriculum develops vocabulary far better than the skills curriculum does.

chapters.description: The period of the 'Great American Test Score Decline' which Hirsch attributes to the 12-year schooling experience of students who were taught under the fully matured progressive/romantic ideology.
key_passages: By 1960, most twelfth graders in American public schools had experienced twelve years of schooling based on its ideas. The Great American Test Score Decline took place between 1960 and 1980.

chapters.description: A twenty-year natural experiment in which the French education system shifted toward American-style pedagogical methods (whole language, naturalism). The result was a significant decline in overall student performance and a sharp increase in the achievement gap, particularly in reading.
key_passages: The target population in 1987 was that of fifth-grade pupils enrolled in France in the public sector.
France has now demonstrated this forcefully.
Indoctrinated in the superiority of whole-language methods, younger teachers spurned the far more effective method of phonics in teaching decoding skill.

chapters.description: The current framework for state-level standards and testing, which Hirsch predicts will fail for the same reasons as NCLB if it continues the focus on skills over knowledge.
key_passages: high-stakes reading tests that accompany the new Common Core State Standards are going to have a similar nugatory or depressing effect on the reading competence of our seventeen-year-olds

chapters.description: The process by which over forty states adopted a set of standards that omitted specific content to avoid political conflict over a national curriculum or literary canon.
key_passages: They knew that including definite text titles would exclude gaining the adherence of over forty states, a remarkable achievement.

chapters.description: The set of educational standards adopted by most US states. Hirsch evaluates them as having potential (through informational text and vocabulary requirements) but failing in implementation due to a lack of specific content and a reliance on 'skill' rhetoric and flawed testing.
key_passages: I have shown that its skill provisions are less than ideal
The greatest virtues of the Common Core ELA Standards are their raising of the vocabulary difficulty of texts at each grade level, and their insistence that half of the texts be “informational.”
Districts and states can make the Common Core ELA Standards transformative... by proposing a specific and coherent curriculum.

cases.name: Santa Barbara School District move to Common Core

chapters.description: The district announced a shift in culture and teaching style to focus on curiosity and critical thinking over 'merely memorizing content' as they transitioned to the Common Core State Standards.
key_passages: The Santa Barbara Unified School District will focus on changing its culture and teaching style in the coming year... 'All of that fits into the nationwide move to new academic standards, which will embrace learning across all subjects, and have students practice critical thinking, curiosity and creativity instead of merely memorizing content,' Cash said.

chapters.description: A local example of the nationwide move toward academic standards that prioritize '21st-century skills' like curiosity and creativity over content memorization.
key_passages: ‘All of that fits into the nationwide move to new academic standards, which will embrace learning across all subjects, and have students practice critical thinking, curiosity and creativity instead of merely memorizing content,’ Cash said.

chapters.description: A Core Knowledge school in Minnesota that received a Blue Ribbon award. It demonstrated both high excellence (top city scores) and high equity (narrowest achievement gap), leading to a waiting list of over 1,000 students.
key_passages: it had been posting the best scores in the city, and also narrowed the achievement gap between advantaged and disadvantaged pupils better than any other local school.
With a total capacity of 351 students in grades K–5, the school had a waiting list of over 1,000.

chapters.description: A Core Knowledge elementary school in Rochester, Minnesota, that achieved the highest test scores in the city and received a Blue Ribbon award. Despite its success and a waiting list of 1,000 students, the city had not adopted the curriculum district-wide due to systemic and ideological resistance.
key_passages: The school, the Washington School, was to be featured because it had been posting the best scores in the city, and also narrowed the achievement gap between advantaged and disadvantaged pupils better than any other local school.
With a total capacity of 351 students in grades K–5, the school had a waiting list of over 1,000.
Great Schools’ ranking of the sixteen public elementary schools in Rochester gives just one of them a 10: the Core Knowledge Washington School.
contestable_aspects: Whether the success is attributable to the curriculum or to selected teachers/students
The accuracy of the test results used for the ranking

chapters.description: Hirsch recalls his education where local history (localism) was balanced by communal textbooks like the Everyday Classics Series (federalism), prior to the 1930s curriculum reforms toward individualism.
key_passages: Localism in Memphis was acknowledged through the teaching of Tennessee history (which I scarcely remember). Federalism was acknowledged through the use of deliberately communal textbooks

chapters.description: An educational project aimed at creating 'out of many one' through a common curriculum designed to preserve national unity and shared principles.
key_passages: The hopeful motto ‘out of many one’ was the central educational project of American schools in the nineteenth century. It was the urgent aim of the common curriculum in the common school.

chapters.description: In the 1940s, Dutch psychologist Adriaan de Groot tested the memory of chess masters and novices. Masters could perfectly recreate board positions from real games but were no better than novices at recreating boards where pieces were placed randomly.
key_passages: A novice could reproduce only four pieces correctly. A chess master could always reproduce the whole twenty-piece position without flaw. ... Masters and novices then performed the same. All placed only four pieces correctly—a surprising result.

chapters.description: A national movement led by progressive educators that replaced academic and historical content with a utilitarian, 'subject-centered' curriculum focused on children's immediate experiences. At least 37 states and hundreds of cities revised curricula in this period.
key_passages: The present K–3 curriculum was introduced in the 1930s, as part of a new approach to the teaching of social studies. At the same time, historical literature and imaginative historical activities were ousted from the curriculum of the early grades.

chapters.description: A transition from early 20th-century massive, civic-oriented school buildings with columns to post-WWII low-slung, welcoming buildings designed for the child's world.
key_passages: After the Second World War, under the influence of child-centered education, American school architecture changed. The design of the elementary school came to be centered on the child.

chapters.description: Hirsch notes historical characterizations of pre-integration segregated schools as having high standards and knowledge-based curricula that may have been more effective for disadvantaged students than the subsequent integrated schools.
key_passages: the segregated schools they attended, with their demanding teachers and no-nonsense, knowledge-based curriculums, were better than the integrated schools blacks attended after Brown v. Board.

chapters.description: James Coleman's follow-up research showing that Catholic schools achieved higher equity by maintaining orderly environments and strong, coherent curricula.
key_passages: He pursued further studies showing that Catholic schools were doing an excellent job in reducing the impact of family and poverty on educational achievement. That buttressed his larger point: schools can educate all children well, and greatly ameliorate disadvantage if they are good orderly schools that impart a strong, coherent curriculum as the Catholic schools did.

chapters.description: Schools that demonstrated high equity effects by providing a strong, coherent curriculum and an orderly environment, which significantly reduced the typical achievement gap associated with poverty.
key_passages: Catholic schools were doing an excellent job in reducing the impact of family and poverty on educational achievement.
The solution to the equity problem is basically to have good schools with coherent curricula, as the Catholic schools did.

chapters.description: The new generation of tests intended to replace state-specific tests. Hirsch argues that despite their novelty and expense, they replicate the old error of testing content-free 'skills' like close reading because they lack a specific knowledge-based curriculum to test against.
key_passages: the makers of the Common Core tests are placed in the same position as the makers of the previous tests that were based on state standards.
The sample items made public by the new test consortia for the Common Core amply fulfill my prediction that the tests will stress both main-idea finding and close reading.

chapters.description: A diverse group of schools (public, charter, and private) that implement a specific, content-rich curriculum and serve as a 'proof of concept' for knowledge-based education in the U.S.
key_passages: The Core Knowledge schools offer proof of concept for the knowledge-based school in the United States.
Whatever their administrative structures, they are highly similar in spirit and effectiveness.
contestable_aspects: The degree to which results are due to curriculum vs. self-selection of families

chapters.description: A specific implementation where the study of the European Middle Ages is integrated with Middle Ages art, literature (King Arthur), Islam, and China during the same period to reinforce vocabulary and concepts.
key_passages: In the Core Knowledge Sequence, in fourth grade, the study of the Middle Ages in Europe is accompanied by units on the pictorial art of the Middle Ages, on the King Arthur myths in literature, on Islam in the Middle Ages, and on China in the Middle Ages.

cases.name: Einstein and the existence of atoms

chapters.description: In the early 20th century, scientists like Ernst Mach doubted the existence of atoms. Einstein published a series of papers in 1905 calculating Avogadro's number from four independent physical phenomena. Because the calculations converged on the same number, it became irrational to disbelieve in atoms. Hirsch uses this as a model for how educational science has now triangulated on domain-specific knowledge.
key_passages: In the early years of the twentieth century a positivist like Ernst Mach could still deny the existence of atoms and molecules. ... Einstein proposed that if the computation of N should turn out the same from independent sources, then a rational person should cease to be a skeptic about the existence of atoms.

chapters.description: Spain and France used national academies to institute standardized national languages to unify the nation-state.
key_passages: In Spain and France the job was done in national academies—still very much in existence.

chapters.description: A universal, free preschool system in France that focuses on social training and a multi-year sequence of knowledge. Hirsch describes schools in low-income districts where immigrant children from North Africa, China, and Turkey achieve high levels of participation and social competence through a 'domain immersion' approach and a curriculum mapped out by the Ministry of Education.
key_passages: Essentially all the children understand all the activities in a French preschool and are engaged by them. That’s because the sequence of experiences has been planned with great care.
In France, underneath that happy activity lies a conscious, careful sequence—meticulously planned day-to-day, week-to-week, year-to-year.
contestable_aspects: The degree to which central planning actually results in 'universal joy'
Whether the curriculum is as strictly followed in all banlieues as described

chapters.description: After scoring poorly on the initial 2000 PISA assessment, Germany implemented specific curriculum guides grade-by-grade across its states (Länder). This focus on curriculum alignment and content specificity led to marked improvements in achievement and equity.
key_passages: Germany is an object lesson in the other direction. It has greatly improved its PISA standings in both achievement and equity.
Reacting to the Schock, Germany began to adopt well-defined curricula. After that it began to score above average for developed nations.

chapters.description: The author uses Google Translate as a real-time demonstration that high-level language performance requires contextual and domain knowledge. The system's improvement through crowdsourcing reflects the power of communal knowledge rather than the development of an all-purpose language skill.
key_passages: Google Translate, which daily shows that algorithms without specific contextual knowledge cannot yield accurate translations. ... Not even Google Translate is capable of an all-purpose language skill

chapters.description: Hirsch lists titles from this popular program (e.g., 'A Dragon Gets By', 'How Real Pigs Act') to illustrate the 'heterogeneous' and 'non-domain based' nature of current elementary reading curricula.
key_passages: pointing out that making them the cornerstone of a language arts curriculum in the earliest grades has exacted huge opportunity costs.

cases.name: Implementation of Common Core in Early Grades

chapters.description: Hirsch observes teachers advising each other on techniques like highlighting every word or heavily annotating margins in young children's books to satisfy 'close reading' standards.
key_passages: I find teachers advising each other about how to get their students to pay close atten- tion to the words of the text. I find images of young students’ books with every word highlighted; a printed page that was originally white is now mainly red or yellow.
contestable_aspects: Whether these Internet-sourced teacher tips represent standard or recommended practice under Common Core.

cases.name: Marietta Johnson's School for the Well-to-do

chapters.description: A school in Fairhope, Alabama, led by Marietta Johnson in the 1920s-30s, which implemented 'natural development' ideas. Hirsch notes that because the students were wealthy, they did not depend on the school for basic initiation into communal knowledge.
key_passages: Mrs. Johnson’s famous school in Fairhope, Alabama, was a school for the well- to-do, for whom basic initiation into communal knowledge was scarcely needed.
contestable_aspects: The claim that poor children are viewed as having 'slower development' in such systems.

chapters.description: An example of a progressive school for the wealthy where the need for communal knowledge was less pressing, masking the negative effects of natural-development theories on the poor.
key_passages: Mrs. Johnson’s famous school in Fairhope, Alabama, was a school for the well-to-do, for whom basic initiation into communal knowledge was scarcely needed.

chapters.description: A comparative study cited by Piaget where children in Martinique and Montreal used the same French curriculum. Despite the identical curriculum, the children in Martinique reached Piagetian developmental milestones four years later than their counterparts in Montreal. Hirsch uses this to argue that environmental and knowledge-based factors, rather than biology or climate, dictate developmental timing.
key_passages: Using the same curriculum, the French-speaking children in Montreal unfolded four years earlier than the French-speaking children in Martinique.
The experiences and language of the home and the curricular effectiveness of the school together explain the difference between Martinique and Montreal.

chapters.description: A period of intense social unrest in France that targeted established institutions, including the education system, and provided the political momentum for the critiques of thinkers like Bourdieu.
key_passages: a demand that had continued to animate French politics ever since the alarming student revolts of May 1968. These revolts criticized almost all of France’s established institutions.

chapters.description: New York State approved a curriculum (Core Knowledge Language Arts) that taught Mesopotamia to first graders. Critics attacked it as developmentally inappropriate, which Hirsch uses as an example of how 'DAP' theory is used to withhold high-level knowledge from students whose parents don't provide it at home.
key_passages: Some would delay, for example, the teaching of Mesopotamia in first grade and offer instead “my neighborhood” as being more age appropriate.
