all_claims.text: The American black-white achievement gap narrowed throughout the twentieth century until reaching its narrowest point in 1988, after which progress stalled.
all_claims.source_passage: The gap narrowed through the twentieth century, reaching its narrowest in 1988; then, it stalled.

all_claims.text: School desegregation following Brown v. Board of Education had an initial positive effect on the achievement gap that eventually ceased.
all_claims.source_passage: Desegregation had an initial positive effect, which then ceased.

all_claims.text: Approximately two-thirds of the causal effects for the achievement gap stasis remain unaccounted for after social factors like economic status and school integration are considered.
all_claims.source_passage: Grissmer et al. concluded that some two-thirds of the causal effects remain unaccounted for after such social factors as economic status and school integration are taken into account.

all_claims.text: Inside-the-school factors, including changes in school curriculums, are the likely cause of the unaccounted-for portion of the achievement gap.
all_claims.source_passage: The inside-the-school causes within that unaccounted-for two-thirds could not be factored in, because changes in school factors—including school curriculums—are unknown.

all_claims.text: Reading comprehension scores of seventeen-year-olds are very highly correlated with vocabulary size.
all_claims.source_passage: Reading comprehension scores of seventeen-year-olds (where the scores count for life chances) are very highly correlated with vocabulary size.

all_claims.text: Vocabulary size is the result of slow, multiyear accretion.
all_claims.source_passage: Vocabulary size is the product of slow, multiyear accretion.

all_claims.text: A school's contribution to vocabulary gain is largely determined by the breadth of the learned curriculum in pre-high school grades.
all_claims.source_passage: The school’s contribution to vocabulary gain is largely determined by the breadth of the learned curriculum in the pre–high school grades.

all_claims.text: The breadth of the learned curriculum is highly correlated with the breadth of the actual school curriculum if the school is responsible.
all_claims.source_passage: The breadth of the learned curriculum is correlated with the breadth of the actual school curriculum—highly correlated if the school does a responsible job.

all_claims.text: A broad curriculum taught to all students narrows vocabulary gaps because lower-vocabulary students learn more words per lesson than their peers who already know some of the content.
all_claims.source_passage: A broad curriculum that is effectively taught to all students inherently narrows vocabulary gaps, because the students who knew fewer words to start with are now learning more words per lesson than their advantaged peers who already knew some of the words in the lesson.

all_claims.text: The school's ability to narrow the verbal achievement gap depends on the breadth and cumulativeness of the elementary curriculum.
all_claims.source_passage: Conclusion: the school’s contribution to narrowing the verbal achievement gap depends greatly on the breadth and cumulativeness of the elementary curriculum.

all_claims.text: The policy of improving neighborhood schools can only succeed if they offer a well-taught, well-planned, multiyear curriculum that systematically builds knowledge.
all_claims.source_passage: What has not been successful is the policy of trying to make all neighborhood schools far better—an aim that can only be accomplished when schools offer a well-taught, well-planned, multiyear curriculum that systematically builds up knowledge.

all_claims.text: Interviews with parents show that they overwhelmingly prefer a nearby school if it is a good school.
all_claims.source_passage: Interviews with parents make overwhelmingly clear that parents prefer the nearby school especially if it is a good school.

all_claims.text: Only approximately 30 percent of parents in inferior schools desire to transport their children to schools outside their neighborhood.
all_claims.source_passage: At present, only some 30 percent of parents in inferior schools favor transporting their child to another school.

all_claims.text: The true principle of choice includes the 70 percent of parents who wish to stay in their neighborhood and deserve good schools for their children.
all_claims.source_passage: The other 70 percent of parents who wish to stick to their neighborhoods also deserve good schools for their children. That is what they would certainly choose—as would many of the 30 percent as well. This is the true principle of 'choice.'

all_claims.text: Segregated schools in the American South often had demanding teachers and knowledge-based curriculums that were superior to the integrated schools that followed.
all_claims.source_passage: I have heard from African American colleagues... that the segregated schools they attended, with their demanding teachers and no-nonsense, knowledge-based curriculums, were better than the integrated schools blacks attended after Brown v. Board.

all_claims.text: Parents overwhelmingly prefer nearby schools, especially if the school is high-performing.
all_claims.source_passage: parents prefer the nearby school especially if it is a good school.

all_claims.text: Educational reformers should prioritize improving neighborhood schools over busing or school choice programs.
all_claims.source_passage: Those reformers who emphasize the need for parental choice as well as those who favor renewed busing might well consider the parental voices that overwhelmingly favor improving the neighborhood school.

all_claims.text: The true principle of 'choice' for parents is the ability to send children to a safe, orderly, and high-performing local school.
all_claims.source_passage: This is the true principle of “choice.” Who wouldn’t prefer to send their young children in early grades to the closest school that is safe, orderly, caring, and high performing?

all_claims.text: International comparisons of national systems show that curriculum content is key to closing achievement gaps.
all_claims.source_passage: Support for the key importance of school effects in closing gaps comes from international comparisons of national systems in which curriculum content is well documented.

all_claims.text: Nations with high overall elementary school performance also tend to have the narrowest achievement gaps between economic groups.
all_claims.source_passage: Those nations whose elementary schools perform the best overall—Finland, Canada, Japan, Korea—also rank high in narrowing the achievement gaps between economic groups.

all_claims.text: High-performing nations share a characteristic where curriculum topics are clearly known to students, teachers, and parents.
all_claims.source_passage: In the highest-scoring nations the year-to-year curriculum topics are known to all—students, teachers, and parents.

all_claims.text: The adoption of 'new education' theories caused curriculum quality to decline and halted progress in educational equality in the US.
all_claims.source_passage: The “new education” caused curriculum quality to decline, stopping progress in equality, and putting a nation at risk.

all_claims.text: Schools account for 40% of the differences in student achievement when family background is statistically controlled.
all_claims.source_passage: Our results suggest that schools do indeed matter, in that when one examines the outcomes across the national sample of schools, fully 40% of the differences in achievement can be found between schools.

all_claims.text: The interpretation of the Coleman Report that 'family and poverty matter more than schools' provided an excuse for schools to avoid accountability.
all_claims.source_passage: That interpretation enabled schools to keep doing in good conscience what they had been doing, without feeling open to the reproach that they were injuring poor minority children.

all_claims.text: The Coleman Report's findings were limited by the fact that they were gathered during the era of child-centered educational dominance.
all_claims.source_passage: It had taken its soundings at a particular historical moment: in the 1960s after the child-centered takeover of American elementary schools.

all_claims.text: Catholic schools effectively reduced the impact of family background on student achievement.
all_claims.source_passage: He pursued further studies showing that Catholic schools were doing an excellent job in reducing the impact of family and poverty on educational achievement.

all_claims.text: Schools can greatly ameliorate educational disadvantage if they are orderly and impart a strong, coherent curriculum.
all_claims.source_passage: The data actually implied, Coleman said, that schools could indeed matter a great deal. He was so disturbed by the social determinism of the interpreters that he pursued further studies showing that Catholic schools were doing an excellent job in reducing the impact of family and poverty on educational achievement.

all_claims.text: The achievement of minority pupils depends more on the schools they attend than does the achievement of majority pupils.
all_claims.source_passage: To put it another way, the achievement of minority pupils depends more on the schools they attend than does the achievement of majority pupils.

all_claims.text: Improving the school of a minority pupil increases their achievement more than a similar improvement would increase the achievement of a white child.
all_claims.source_passage: The inference might then be made that improving the school of a minority pupil may increase his achievement more than would improving the school of a white child increase his.

all_claims.text: School quality affects disadvantaged students twice as much as it affects advantaged children.
all_claims.source_passage: Coleman’s insight that school quality affects disadvantaged students twice as much as advantaged children deserves to be memorialized as the 'Coleman Differential Effect.'

all_claims.text: Good schools inherently narrow the achievement gap because they benefit poor children significantly more than they benefit rich ones.
all_claims.source_passage: A good school narrows the achievement gap, because it benefits poor children more than it benefits rich ones. Thus, good schools inherently narrow the gap.

all_claims.text: The widening of the black-white reading gap between ages thirteen and seventeen is evidence that American K–12 schooling is of poor quality.
all_claims.source_passage: If K–12 schooling had been good schooling, that could not have occurred; the gaps would have consistently narrowed as children stayed in school. The finding of the Coleman Differential Effect is thus a reproach to the American school system as it now stands.

all_claims.text: NAEP data underestimates the true achievement gap at age seventeen because it excludes students who have dropped out.
all_claims.source_passage: Moreover, the current gap is really greater than appears at age seventeen because NAEP does not include the thousands of low-scoring students who drop out between thirteen and seventeen.

all_claims.text: The chief aim of preschool from the standpoint of social justice is to enhance children's knowledge, syntax, and vocabularies.
all_claims.source_passage: So, while there are many things that children should learn in preschool, the chief aim from the standpoint of social justice is to enhance their knowledge, syntax, and vocabularies.

all_claims.text: Domain immersion is the surest way to enhance knowledge and vocabulary in early childhood education.
all_claims.source_passage: The surest way to do that in preschool is the strategy of 'domain immersion,' described in chapter 3. It is a principle that should continue to be followed also in later grades.

all_claims.text: Vocabulary catch-up occurs by implicit word learning when domain immersion is applied consistently with a sequence of domains and independent readings.
all_claims.source_passage: When domain immersion is applied consistently with a careful sequence of domains over a full school year, and then in the next year and the year after that, with independent readings directed to the purpose, vocabulary catch-up occurs by implicit word learning.

all_claims.text: A coherent curriculum benefits all students but provides the greatest benefit to the least prepared students.
all_claims.source_passage: The coherence of the curriculum benefits all students. Crucially, it benefits the least prepared the most.

all_claims.text: Domain immersion requires a definite and coherent curriculum.
all_claims.source_passage: It requires a definite and coherent curriculum.

all_claims.text: Isolated study of words is significantly less effective for vocabulary building than studying words within the context of domains.
all_claims.source_passage: What does not work nearly as effectively for vocabulary building is the isolated study of words. Such study has its place, as it comes up in context within the study of domains, but not in isolation from that larger context.

all_claims.text: The intensive study of word lists in current schools is an inefficient fallback necessitated by the lack of common cumulative content in language arts classes.
all_claims.source_passage: The intensive study of word lists in our schools today is an inefficient, fallback position made necessary by the absence of common cumulative content in the language arts class in elementary school.

all_claims.text: Implicit word learning is much faster and more accurate than explicit word learning.
all_claims.source_passage: One quickly reaches the point of diminishing returns, because implicit word learning is much faster and more accurate than explicit word learning. That’s an unexpected finding.

all_claims.text: A typical young person gains approximately 15 words a day (5,475 words a year) through implicit means by age sixteen.
all_claims.source_passage: Experts estimate that by age sixteen, a typical young person has gained by implicit means about 15 words a day (including Saturdays, Sundays, and holidays, as well as summer vacation)—some 5,475 words a year.

all_claims.text: Explicit word study using a dictionary often promotes an inaccurate understanding of word connotations.
all_claims.source_passage: In the 1987 study, he and Gildea showed that explicit word study with a dictionary often encourages a quite inaccurate sense of word connotations.

all_claims.text: Accurate word connotations are constructed gradually through exposure to words in multiple past contexts.
all_claims.source_passage: An accurate sense of word connotations is built up gradually from multiple past contexts. We remember bits of those past contexts along with our memories of the word, and those remembered bits help us identify nuances of words among the range of multiple meanings each word potentially has.

all_claims.text: Explicit word study, while useful at times, can be distortive and time-consuming and should be used sparingly.
all_claims.source_passage: Explicit word study, while useful and even necessary at times, can be distortive as well as time-consuming. It should be used sparingly.

all_claims.text: The educational philosophy of child-friendly individualism has contributed to the stall in closing verbal achievement gaps.
all_claims.source_passage: There is thus a connection between the supposedly child-friendly individualism of the new education and the stall in the narrowing of verbal achievement gaps.

all_claims.text: Encouraging children to follow their own interests results in significant gaps in their knowledge and vocabulary.
all_claims.source_passage: Encouraging children to follow their interests leaves them with big holes in their knowledge and vocabulary.

all_claims.text: Drills in comprehension strategies and isolated word lists have failed to close the achievement gap and cannot work.
all_claims.source_passage: These holes are supposed to be filled by word lists, and by drills in comprehension strategies. The verdict is in on these ideas and methods: they have not worked. And a more probing analysis says they cannot work.

all_claims.text: There is a causal connection between the child-friendly individualism of modern education and the failure to narrow verbal achievement gaps.
all_claims.source_passage: There is thus a connection between the supposedly child-friendly individualism of the new education and the stall in the narrowing of verbal achievement gaps.

all_claims.text: Vocabulary drills and comprehension strategy drills have failed to fill the knowledge gaps created by individualistic education.
all_claims.source_passage: These holes are supposed to be filled by word lists, and by drills in comprehension strategies. The verdict is in on these ideas and methods: they have not worked.

all_claims.text: Vocabulary drills and strategy instruction cannot work as a matter of principle.
all_claims.source_passage: And a more probing analysis says they cannot work.

all_claims.text: Individualistic educational methods have created unpleasant and ineffectual schooling environments that specifically harm disadvantaged students.
all_claims.source_passage: They have led to highly unnatural and unpleasant forms of schooling, which have left disadvantaged students disadvantaged.

all_claims.text: Disadvantaged students drop out of school because the instruction is unpleasant, frustrating, and ineffectual.
all_claims.source_passage: Who can blame disadvantaged students for dropping out of that kind of unpleasant, frustrating, and ineffectual schooling?

all_claims.text: Whole-class domain immersion is the superior educational system for narrowing gaps.
all_claims.source_passage: Whole-class domain immersion is the better system, early and late.

all_claims.text: An educational domain is any larger context into which studied items are integrated conceptually and linguistically.
all_claims.source_passage: A domain is any larger context into which the things being studied are integrated and connected conceptually and linguistically.

all_claims.text: Fictional texts function as domains just as much as scientific systems like laws of motion.
all_claims.source_passage: So if a student is studying a fictional text—a poem or novel—that is a domain too, just as much as Newton’s three laws of motion.

all_claims.text: Prior domain knowledge is the essential condition for learning new words and concepts because it enables the formation of a situation model.
all_claims.source_passage: In language use it represents the relevant prior knowledge that contextualizes a discourse and enables a student to form a situation model for the meaning. That is the key condition for learning new words and new things.

all_claims.text: Knowledge is foundational to skills, a principle accepted by scientific consensus.
all_claims.source_passage: Domain has the further advantage of representing the scientifically accepted understanding of the knowledge that is foundational to a skill.

all_claims.text: A well-defined, cumulative curriculum is the primary key to closing the achievement gap.
all_claims.source_passage: Knowledge is the key to gap closing, and it is best gained coherently by the cumulative, year-by-year study of domains—by a well-defined, cumulative curriculum.

all_claims.text: The achievement of equity and equality of opportunity depends on technical psychological principles of curriculum design.
all_claims.source_passage: On such technical, nitty-gritty psychological principles hang great issues of equity and equality of opportunity.

all_claims.text: American test scores declined nationwide between 1960 and 1980.
all_claims.source_passage: We know this is true from the general nationwide test score decline between 1960 and 1980.

all_claims.text: Some national systems have narrowed the achievement gap more effectively than others.
all_claims.source_passage: Some national systems have narrowed the gap better than others.

all_claims.text: The black-white achievement gap offers fuller opportunities for historical analysis than other low-scoring groups due to the length of time it has been studied.
all_claims.source_passage: The reason the black-white gap was chosen for analysis by ETS instead of including all low-scoring groups was that the black-white gap has been studied longer, and offers fuller opportunities for historical analysis.

all_claims.text: Reading comprehension scores of seventeen-year-olds serve as a proxy for their future life chances.
all_claims.source_passage: Reading comprehension scores of seventeen-year-olds (where the scores count for life chances) are very highly correlated with vocabulary size.

all_claims.text: Busing and reintegration are logical social policies as long as a significant quality gap exists between schools for advantaged and disadvantaged children.
all_claims.source_passage: As a social policy, busing and reintegration make sense so long as the schools attended by advantaged children continue to be far superior to those attended by disadvantaged children.

all_claims.text: High-quality schools have the capacity to transform the atmosphere and sentiments of their surrounding neighborhood.
all_claims.source_passage: Such good schools can transform the atmosphere and sentiments of the neighborhood.

all_claims.text: The 30 percent of parents who desire busing should have viable choices as long as their local schools remain inferior.
all_claims.source_passage: Until we have good schools in poor neighborhoods—a possible goal, achieved in other nations—that 30 percent of parents who desire busing should certainly have viable choices, as choice advocates insist.

all_claims.text: The popular interpretation of the Coleman Report that family background matters more than schools contains a fundamental logical flaw.
all_claims.source_passage: But, as Coleman ruefully noted, there was always a logical flaw in the 'it’s not the schools' interpretation of the Coleman Report.

all_claims.text: The original Coleman data shows that minority pupils in the South were twice as dependent on school quality for their achievement as white pupils.
all_claims.source_passage: Thus, 20 percent of the achievement of Negroes in the South is associated with the particular schools they go to, whereas only 10 percent of the achievement of whites in the South is.

all_claims.text: The Coleman Differential Effect implies that poor children catch up slightly for every year they are enrolled in a high-quality school.
all_claims.source_passage: If poor children get twice as much academic benefit from a good school as rich children do, then in each year of good schooling that differential causes poor children to catch up a little.

all_claims.text: Isolated study of words is acceptable and useful when it occurs in the context of studying specific domains.
all_claims.source_passage: Such study has its place, as it comes up in context within the study of domains, but not in isolation from that larger context.

all_claims.text: Implicit word learning is more accurate than explicit word study because it correctly builds word connotations from multiple past contexts.
all_claims.source_passage: Another advantage of implicit word learning is that it’s more accurate than explicit study... An accurate sense of word connotations is built up gradually from multiple past contexts.

all_claims.text: Explicit word study should be used sparingly by schools because it can be distortive and consumes excessive time.
all_claims.source_passage: Explicit word study, while useful and even necessary at times, can be distortive as well as time-consuming. It should be used sparingly.

all_claims.text: In language use, a domain represents the relevant prior knowledge that contextualizes discourse and allows for the formation of a situation model.
all_claims.source_passage: In language use it represents the relevant prior knowledge that contextualizes a discourse and enables a student to form a situation model for the meaning.

all_claims.text: Great issues of equity and equality of opportunity depend on the application of technical psychological principles in the classroom.
all_claims.source_passage: On such technical, nitty-gritty psychological principles hang great issues of equity and equality of opportunity.

all_claims.text: The Common Core State Standards in language arts can improve American education significantly only if interpreted as a mandate for building knowledge.
all_claims.source_passage: The Common Core State Standards in language arts can improve American education significantly, but only if we interpret them productively.

all_claims.text: Common Core standards are similar to failed earlier state standards because they do not state the specific knowledge students should gain.
all_claims.source_passage: They are superior to earlier state standards in some respects but all too similar to them in not stating the specific knowledge that students should gain.

all_claims.text: Improvement in students' verbal abilities depends on their knowledge and vocabulary size rather than practicing reading skills.
all_claims.source_passage: Yet real improvement in students’ verbal abilities will not depend on practicing these skills; it will depend on how much students know and how big their vocabularies are.

all_claims.text: The long-range success of the Common Core depends on requiring a coherent approach to building knowledge in elementary school.
all_claims.source_passage: The long-range educational success of the new Common Core Standards in language arts will depend almost completely on interpreting them as requiring a coherent approach to building knowledge in the elementary school.

all_claims.text: Continuing to emphasize skills over knowledge will cause the Common Core to fail in improving student verbal abilities.
all_claims.source_passage: If, as is currently happening, the schools continue to emphasize skills, the new standards will fail to enlarge students’ vocabularies significantly and will therefore fail to improve verbal abilities significantly.

all_claims.text: Content-indeterminate standards and tests cause schools to de-emphasize systematic knowledge gain.
all_claims.source_passage: Content-indeterminate standards plus content-indeterminate tests have caused schools to de-emphasize systematic knowledge gain.

all_claims.text: Standards must be complemented by a well-developed, content-rich curriculum to be effective.
all_claims.source_passage: The Standards . . . do not—indeed, cannot—enumerate all or even most of the content that students should learn. The Standards must therefore be complemented by a well-developed, content-rich curriculum.

all_claims.text: Students gain a foundation of knowledge only when the curriculum is intentionally and coherently structured across grades.
all_claims.source_passage: Students can only gain this foundation when the curriculum is intentionally and coherently structured to develop rich content knowledge within and across grades.

all_claims.text: The knowledge-centric statements in the Common Core have no direct operational impact on tests.
all_claims.source_passage: They have no direct operational impact on tests. Although the statements fairly shout that “rich” knowledge and vocabulary are the goals toward which schools should aim, those sentiments cannot affect the topics of test items

all_claims.text: Test makers cannot include specific topics on tests because they cannot know which topics individual schools have taught.
all_claims.source_passage: since test makers cannot know which topics in each grade level are the ones to which “an adequate number of titles” have been devoted in the schools.

all_claims.text: The Common Core standard requiring 'logical inferences' is defective because textual inferences are contextual rather than logical.
all_claims.source_passage: This logical inference standard is defective on two grounds. . . the inferences one draws in interpretation are not logical, except in a few specialized texts involving syllogisms or other formal principles.

all_claims.text: A text does not say anything 'explicitly' until it has been interpreted by the reader.
all_claims.source_passage: No text says anything “explicitly” until after it has been interpreted.

all_claims.text: The 'close-reading' skill distracts conscious attention away from the message of a text to its medium of expression.
all_claims.source_passage: But paying conscious attention to surface features can be a quite cumbersome habit since it distracts conscious attention away from the message to its medium of expression

all_claims.text: Raising Lexile levels of random, disconnected texts will not automatically increase student knowledge and vocabulary.
all_claims.source_passage: There’s little reason to hope that this will happen simply from raising the Lexile levels of helter-skelter texts.

all_claims.text: Reading comprehension is not a general skill, whereas listening comprehension is recognized as topic-dependent.
all_claims.source_passage: The policy aim of test uniformity was predicated on the widely held but mistaken idea that reading comprehension is a general skill. Would the mistake have been made if the skill were listening comprehension? Probably not, since common sense would urge that listening comprehension is topic-dependent.

all_claims.text: The Common Core standards have added a 'close-reading' skill that requires students to make logical inferences from text.
all_claims.source_passage: To this main-idea-finding skill, the new Core standards have added a ‘close-�reading’ skill requiring students to make ‘logical’ inferences from the text.

all_claims.text: Operationally, text 'complexity' in the Common Core has been defined as using texts with higher Lexile scores.
all_claims.source_passage: Operationally this has meant using texts that have higher Lexile scores than schools used before, a potential step forward to the extent that it builds student knowledge and vocabulary more rapidly.

all_claims.text: Common Core standards have made tests harder to pass without improving the actual ability of students to pass them.
all_claims.source_passage: The net result of the new standards so far has been to make the tests harder to pass, but not to make students better able to pass them.

all_claims.text: Teaching formal close-reading skills as a preparation for tests may lower mature reading scores by consuming class time.
all_claims.source_passage: I will explain in this chapter why the addition of close reading to main-idea finding will turn out in the end not to raise mature reading scores, and could even lower them by encouraging schools to usurp yet more class time in practicing formal close-reading skills in preparation for close-reading test items.

all_claims.text: Common Core developers omitted specific content to ensure political adoption by over forty states.
all_claims.source_passage: They knew that including definite text titles would exclude gaining the adherence of over forty states, a remarkable achievement.

all_claims.text: Reading proficiency does not require mastering a specific literary canon.
all_claims.source_passage: It is not necessary for a student to master a specific literary canon to score well on a reading test, nor to be college and career ready, nor to become a competent citizen.

all_claims.text: Reading proficiency requires mastering a specific canon of knowledge and vocabulary shared by competent readers.
all_claims.source_passage: On the other hand, there is a canon of knowledge and vocabulary known to all highly competent readers and writers. That is axiomatic; possessing that knowledge is what makes them competent.

all_claims.text: Reading tests are actually knowledge tests in disguise.
all_claims.source_passage: ‘Reading tests are knowledge tests in disguise’ is Daniel Willingham’s elegant formulation.

all_claims.text: Reading tests must use passages on domains not studied in school because the school curriculum is unstated and unknown to test makers.
all_claims.source_passage: They are compelled to offer texts on domains of knowledge not studied in school, since the school domains are unstated and unknown.

all_claims.text: Only curriculum-based reading tests can be fair and educationally productive.
all_claims.source_passage: If the test is not curriculum based, it will not be productive.

all_claims.text: States and localities are currently the only jurisdictions capable of creating fair and productive tests because only they determine curriculum content.
all_claims.source_passage: Since only states and localities can currently determine the knowledge domains of the curriculum, they are currently the only jurisdictions in a position to make fair and productive tests.

all_claims.text: No text has 'explicit' meaning until it has been interpreted through a situation model.
all_claims.source_passage: No text says anything ‘explicitly’ until after it has been interpreted.

all_claims.text: Nationwide reading tests that are both fair and productive of sound school practice are a political impossibility as long as a nationwide elementary content core is absent.
all_claims.source_passage: as long as a nationwide elementary content core is a political impossibility, we will have to give up the pipe dream of nationwide reading tests that are both fair and productive of sound school practice.

all_claims.text: The aim of creating better readers trumps the policy aim of establishing a common yardstick for state-to-state measurement.
all_claims.source_passage: But getting a common yardstick and more accurate data is not as important an aim as making better readers.

all_claims.text: Valid high-stakes reading tests must be composed of passages that align with the specific domains of the local curriculum.
all_claims.source_passage: To be educationally valid, high-stakes reading tests would need to be composed of passages on domains from the local curriculum.

all_claims.text: The poor performance of seventeen-year-olds on the NAEP and SAT suggests that general reading skills do not exist as taught.
all_claims.source_passage: If such all-purpose reading skills existed, our seventeen-year-olds, who have practiced such skills for years, would be performing better on NAEP and the SAT, not worse.

all_claims.text: The all-purpose skills advocated in the Common Core language arts standards are myths.
all_claims.source_passage: the all-purpose skills advocated in the Common Core language arts standards are largely myths just like the all-purpose skills of earlier state standards.

all_claims.text: There is no general main-idea-finding skill, complexity-managing skill, or close-reading skill.
all_claims.source_passage: I will show that there is no general main-idea-finding skill, no complexity-managing skill, and no close-reading skill.

all_claims.text: The knowledge-based character of verbal skills is an unalterable principle that policy makers cannot repeal.
all_claims.source_passage: It is not in the power of any policy maker, legislator, standards maker, or anyone else involved in educational policy to repeal the knowledge-based character of verbal skills.

all_claims.text: The main-idea-finding skill was devised for the convenience of test makers rather than to benefit students.
all_claims.source_passage: the chief virtue of the main-idea concept is its convenience for grownups, not for students, who have not been much helped under its regime.

all_claims.text: The ability to find the main idea of a text depends entirely on the student's prior knowledge and vocabulary.
all_claims.source_passage: But there is no significant main-idea-finding skill apart from the prior ability to comprehend a text—an ability that depends on the relevant knowledge and vocabulary already possessed by the student taking the test.

all_claims.text: Main-idea finding is a circular skill: you cannot find the main idea until you already understand what the text means.
all_claims.source_passage: From an analytical view, the main-idea-finding skill is a circularity. You can’t deduce the main idea until you have already construed what the text means.

all_claims.text: Disadvantaged children would perform better on tests if they studied subject matter rather than practicing finding main ideas.
all_claims.source_passage: They would perform far better on the passage if they had studied question-relevant subject matter in school. And in that case they would not need to practice finding the main idea.

all_claims.text: Close reading is often implemented in ways that distract students from the actual message of the text.
all_claims.source_passage: paying conscious attention to surface features can be a quite cumbersome habit since it distracts conscious attention away from the message to its medium of expression

all_claims.text: Students lacking requisite knowledge and vocabulary will not be able to answer reading comprehension questions regardless of skill practice.
all_claims.source_passage: acking req- uisite knowledge and vocabulary will not be able to answer the ques- tion. They would perform far better on the passage if they had studied question-relevant subject matter in school.

all_claims.text: Studying question-relevant subject matter is more effective for reading performance than practicing finding the main idea.
all_claims.source_passage: They would perform far better on the passage if they had studied question-relevant subject matter in school. And in that case they would not need to practice finding the main idea.

all_claims.text: Close reading is a specialized activity suitable for advanced literary analysis in later grades, not for early reading instruction.
all_claims.source_passage: Close reading is an activity that is suitable for appreciating the finer points of literature in later grades... But to encourage the practice as a mode of actual reading of texts or the testing of reading comprehension is a mistake unjustified by credible research.

all_claims.text: Close reading produces inferior comprehension and memory for meaning compared to reading for general meaning.
all_claims.source_passage: Close reading had produced inferior comprehension and memory for meaning.

all_claims.text: Paying close attention to surface text debases comprehension by consuming limited working memory resources.
all_claims.source_passage: With most texts, paying close attention to the text itself debases comprehension by usurping limited mental resources that would be bet- ter applied to pondering the substantive implications and the validity of what the text is saying.

all_claims.text: Human working memory is limited.
all_claims.source_passage: The first is that hu- man working memory is limited.

all_claims.text: People remember what they pay attention to; focusing on words prevents readers from considering important unstated implications.
all_claims.source_passage: The second finding is that we remember what we pay attention to. If we pay attention to words, those are what we will consider and remember rather than important unstated implications.

all_claims.text: Interpreted meaning is not reliably bound to the specific words that produce it.
all_claims.source_passage: Recent work on text comprehension shows that interpreted meaning is not reliably bound to the words that produce it

all_claims.text: Ambiguity is a critical feature of human language that allows for flexibility.
all_claims.source_passage: That’s because words are ambiguous—an absolutely critical feature of human language that allows it to become a hugely flexible instrument.

all_claims.text: The relationship between close reading and meaning is inherently indeterminate.
all_claims.source_passage: since the relation between close reading and meaning is inherently indeterminate.

all_claims.text: Inferences drawn from text are contextual and dependent on situation models rather than being strictly logical deductions from words.
all_claims.source_passage: The inferences we usually draw from a text are not logical but con- textual—dependent upon the situation model that we construct, not upon the mere words on the page.

all_claims.text: A text does not speak its own meaning; meaning is constructed by combining text with the reader's unspoken knowledge.
all_claims.source_passage: the text does not speak its own meaning. That meaning is a construct composed of what the text says plus the addi- tional relevant, unspoken knowledge that the reader brings to it.

all_claims.text: Close reading is often used by readers merely to find support for meanings they have already acquired.
all_claims.source_passage: When I examine what people actually do when they use close reading in order to write about texts, I find that they use the close-reading technique to support the meanings that they have already gained before they began to close-read.

all_claims.text: The close-reading strand in the Common Core is scientifically questionable.
all_claims.source_passage: The unfortunate consequences of the scientifically questionable close-reading strand in the Common Core is the effect it has had on the tests and the time-wasting preparation for them.

all_claims.text: The PARCC assessments employ defective test items that assume meaning can be logically inferred from textual 'clues' in isolation.
all_claims.source_passage: The Partnership for Assessment of Readiness for College and Careers (PARCC), one of the two Common Core test consortia, has created two-part close- reading questions. One part asks about the meaning of a word, the other about the feature of the text from which one can logically infer that meaning.

all_claims.text: Standardized test questions that ask students to infer word meanings solely from context are based on an inadequate concept of reading comprehension.
all_claims.source_passage: The public has already started to protest these odd and sometimes unanswerable test items that have been constructed on an inadequate concept of reading comprehension.

all_claims.text: Logical inference derived purely from close reading is a 'mirage' because understanding depends on prior knowledge of word meanings.
all_claims.source_passage: No, her answer that misery means exhaustion is wrong because logical inference from close reading is a mirage. What she needed to know to answer the question was the usual meaning of the word misery.

all_claims.text: Paying attention to specific word features is a less effective strategy than reading for meaning.
all_claims.source_passage: This close-reading standard in the Common Core should be recast (or excised) to conform with the scientific finding that paying attention to word features is less effective than reading for meaning

all_claims.text: Common Core's focus on complex texts is a technical attempt to improve comprehension while avoiding the political difficulty of prescribing specific knowledge domains.
all_claims.source_passage: It tries to induce a more challenging and productive curriculum under the political constraint that the actual knowledge domains and texts of the curriculum must not be prescribed.

all_claims.text: A primary cause for the decline in reading abilities was the use of overly undemanding texts in schools.
all_claims.source_passage: One of the theories for the decline in reading abilities had been that the texts students have been asked to read are too undemanding.

all_claims.text: Lexile measures were historically used to 'dumb down' the elementary curriculum.
all_claims.source_passage: It would follow, then, that if in the past we used readability measures like Lexiles to dumb down the elementary curriculum, we might use those same measures at a higher level to smarten up the curriculum.

all_claims.text: Students are often capable of handling more difficult texts than educators typically assume.
all_claims.source_passage: But in actuality they can do more than we often think they can.

all_claims.text: Student progress with complex texts is strictly limited when the topic of the text is unfamiliar, regardless of its technical difficulty score.
all_claims.source_passage: But there are strict limits to the progress students can make if the text, regardless of its Lexile score, is on a topic that is unfamiliar.

all_claims.text: The skills-based approach in the Common Core conflicts with its requirement for a rich, coherent knowledge curriculum.
all_claims.source_passage: This is where the knowledge imperative and the skill imperative of the Common Core come into conflict. Intellectually, the skills-approach lives uncomfortably with the new, more communal approach of Common Core Standards that require a rich, coherent curriculum.

all_claims.text: Lexiles and complexity measures are indifferent to subject matter and can be used to avoid content-rich curricula.
all_claims.source_passage: Complexity and Lexiles are largely indifferent to subject matter. They can equally sponsor a coherent curriculum or a fragmented one. That is why they can be used as a technical crutch for content avoidance

all_claims.text: The individualism of classroom libraries is antithetical to the rich communal curriculum required by the Common Core standards.
all_claims.source_passage: Such individualism is antithetical to the rich communal curriculum required by the standards.

all_claims.text: The only way to effectively implement a rich, coherent curriculum is for students in early grades to read mostly the same texts.
all_claims.source_passage: The requirement that 'the curriculum is intentionally and coherently structured to develop rich content knowledge within and across grades' can only be fulfilled if students in the early grades mostly read the same texts.

all_claims.text: A student's ability to master complex syntax and rare vocabulary increases as they become familiar with the subject matter.
all_claims.source_passage: As topics become familiar, student ability to master rarer words and longer sentences (the chief elements of complexity) increases.

all_claims.text: The Common Core standards contain an internal self-contradiction regarding the structure of the curriculum and student text selection.
all_claims.source_passage: This self-contradiction within the Common Core Standards should be resolved in favor of commonality of texts in the classroom, since that’s the only way to effectively implement a rich, coherent, and cumulative curriculum.

all_claims.text: Domain immersion—using a sequence of texts from the same domain—induces progress in reading complex texts.
all_claims.source_passage: It induces progress in reading complex texts not because an all-purpose complexity-managing skill has increased in two weeks, but because students’ domain familiarity has increased.

all_claims.text: There is no such thing as an all-purpose, content-independent complexity-managing skill.
all_claims.source_passage: Getting familiar with a lot of domains is the best way to enhance ability to read complex texts, since there is no all-purpose complexity-managing skill.

all_claims.text: Domain familiarity is the primary skill required to manage text complexity.
all_claims.source_passage: Domain familiarity is the chief complexity-managing skill.

all_claims.text: Schools should prioritize the acquisition of knowledge as the primary method for improving reading comprehension.
all_claims.source_passage: the reliable way to achieve technical reading comprehension skills for all students, including the ability to read highly complex and challenging texts, is for the school to stress knowledge front and center as the chief reading comprehension skill

all_claims.text: The Common Core requirement that roughly half of texts be informational is one of its most beneficial standards.
all_claims.source_passage: The most criticized element in the Common Core language arts standards is one of the best standards of all—that about half of the texts read should be “informational” texts.

all_claims.text: Increasing informational texts reduces the prevalence of vacuous stories and enhances systematic knowledge and vocabulary.
all_claims.source_passage: This immediately reduces the number of vacuous stories about picnics at the beach with the Beaver family, and it potentially enhances the curriculum with more systematic knowledge and vocabulary in multiple domains.

all_claims.text: The distinction between 'literary' and 'informational' texts is artificial; good fiction provides information and good information can be literature.
all_claims.source_passage: The distinction need not be between “literary” and “informational” texts. Good works of fiction are informational. And good informational texts can be literature.

all_claims.text: Narratives are the most effective psychological format for conveying knowledge to students.
all_claims.source_passage: Narratives, fictional or nonfictional, are psychologically “privileged.” They are the most effective way to convey knowledge.

all_claims.text: The quality of fictional literature depends on its aim toward truth and its grounding in factual knowledge of the world.
all_claims.source_passage: if fiction isn’t informational, it usually isn’t very good. Good fictional stories and novels aim at truth.

all_claims.text: Understanding literature like 'To Kill a Mockingbird' requires significant prior knowledge of history and social context that should be taught in earlier grades.
all_claims.source_passage: To Kill a Mockingbird, widely taught in seventh grade, depends on students’ having a lot of prior knowledge about the social and historical world that should have been gained in the earlier grades.

all_claims.text: Deep comprehension of literary texts requires specific background knowledge in history, geography, sociology, science, and technology.
all_claims.source_passage: Fictional stories are composed in historical time. They allude to history, geography, sociology, science, and technology.

all_claims.text: Fiction and poetry are not fact-free uses of language that can be mastered through content-independent word study and comprehension sessions.
all_claims.source_passage: Fiction and poetry are not fact-free uses of language that can be readily understood after the requisite number of word study and comprehension sessions.

all_claims.text: Before the Romantic movement, the term 'literature' was used broadly to include almost all genres of writing.
all_claims.source_passage: Indeed, before the romantic movement the word literature embraced almost all genres of writing.

all_claims.text: Good literature aims to communicate truth in a manner comparable to history and science.
all_claims.source_passage: Good fiction and poetry aim at truth no less than do history and science.

all_claims.text: The visualization of 'imaginative' worlds in literature still depends on the reader's knowledge of the real world.
all_claims.source_passage: even fairy tales and science fiction, depend on knowledge of this world in order to be realized in the imagination of the reader.

all_claims.text: Early American literacy materials, such as Noah Webster's and W.H. McGuffey's readers, integrated poetry and fiction with biographies, history, and science to form moral and patriotic citizens.
all_claims.source_passage: In earlier times, the readings in Noah Webster’s Blue Back Speller and W. H. McGuffey’s Eclectic Readers were directed to making moral and patriotic citizens. They included poetry and fiction, but also biographies of great persons, histories of the United States, science, health, and moral tales.

all_claims.text: The educational goal of 'cultivating the imagination' became an accepted standard in the United States around the 1850s due to European Romantic influence.
all_claims.source_passage: The fairy tale proponents won out in due course, because by the 1850s “cultivation of the imagination” had started to become an accepted educational goal.

all_claims.text: American early literacy instruction has been dominated by a near-monopoly of 'imaginative literature' for over a century.
all_claims.source_passage: For more than a century in the United States a dominant emphasis in early literacy classes has been placed on “imaginative literature,” with a high value given to developing the child’s “creativity and imagination.”

all_claims.text: The fierce opposition to Common Core’s inclusion of informational text is caused by the historical shift toward Romantic educational ideals.
all_claims.source_passage: It explains the almost hysterical response to the Common Core’s breaking up the near-monopoly of fiction and poetry in language arts.

all_claims.text: The view that balanced literacy (50/50 fiction and nonfiction) is sacrilegious is a result of Romantic pantheism rather than traditional educational values.
all_claims.source_passage: The view that a 50/50 balance between fiction and nonfiction should seem sacrilegious to language arts teachers is entirely owing to the influence of European romanticism on American education.

all_claims.text: Biblical and early American educational perspectives viewed 'imagination' as potentially deceptive or evil rather than inherently virtuous.
all_claims.source_passage: The Bible says that “the imagination of man’s heart is evil from his youth.” No positive uses of the word imagine are to be found in the Bible.

all_claims.text: The contemporary assumption that imagination is always positive and life-enhancing is an uncritical belief derived from Romanticism.
all_claims.source_passage: But the idea that imagination is always positive and life-enhancing is an uncritical assumption that has crept into our discourse from the pantheistic effusions of the romantic period

all_claims.text: The educational focus on developing imagination represents a shift from communal education to the Romantic idea of natural individualism.
all_claims.source_passage: The educational aim of developing the child’s imagination represented a shift from the communal conception of early education to the romantic idea of natural individua

all_claims.text: Teaching children substantial knowledge about the world is more valuable to their well-being, creativity, and imagination than emphasizing fanciful stories intended to encourage imagination.
all_claims.source_passage: teaching them substantial knowledge about the world is far more valuable to their future well-being—and to their creativity and imagination—than emphasizing fanciful and sometimes not-very-good stories whose only virtue is that they are 'imaginative'

all_claims.text: Coherent integration of nonfiction, poetry, and fiction in the elementary curriculum makes children better communicators and more active citizens.
all_claims.source_passage: if it is applied in a coherent way and integrated with the other disciplines in the elementary curriculum, children will become much abler communicators. And that... will make them better students and wealthier, more active citizens—and abler readers of literature.

all_claims.text: The American public's initial approval of the Common Core was based on the belief that it meant a common grade-by-grade curriculum.
all_claims.source_passage: They thought it meant a core of common subject matter grade-by-grade in the elementary school, and that idea made sense to them. They did not know that the language arts standards were empty of specific grade-by-grade content.

all_claims.text: The concept of commonality in early schooling is a deeply rooted American tradition.
all_claims.source_passage: The idea of commonality in early schooling is as American as cherry pie.

all_claims.text: During the 'Silver Age' of American education (1930s-40s), textbooks provided a national curriculum that balanced localism with federalism.
all_claims.source_passage: Federalism was acknowledged through the use of deliberately communal textbooks like the Everyday Classics Series and other, similar books that had wide currency throughout the schools of the nation.

all_claims.text: The public elementary school is the primary remaining institution capable of ensuring all children learn the shared knowledge required for a nation to be operational.
all_claims.source_passage: It is still the place where all children have the possibility of learning the shared knowledge and values that make a nation operational.

all_claims.text: American competence depends upon mastering the national dimension of the public sphere rather than local or regional variations.
all_claims.source_passage: What makes an American competent—whether in Kittery, Maine, or Cleveland, Ohio, or Oakland, California—is mastery of the national dimension of our public sphere.

all_claims.text: The modern nation-state requires a standardized national language created through school-based instruction.
all_claims.source_passage: When the modern nation state was created in the seventeenth and eighteenth centuries in Europe, each nation instituted a standardized national language to be promulgated and learned in the schools.

all_claims.text: American English is a deliberate, invented national culture founded on the work of Dr. Johnson and Noah Webster.
all_claims.source_passage: Today we learn and speak, read and write Johnsonian-Websterese. I use this invented term to stress the deliberate, invented character of a shared national culture

all_claims.text: The creation of the modern nation-state in the 17th and 18th centuries necessitated the institution of standardized national languages in schools.
all_claims.source_passage: When the modern nation state was created in the seventeenth and eighteenth centuries in Europe, each nation instituted a standardized national language to be promulgated and learned in the schools.

all_claims.text: Modern nations depend on authoritative dictionaries to stabilize pronunciation, grammar, and vocabulary to enable communication across space and time.
all_claims.source_passage: the formation of the modern nation depended upon authoritative dictionary makers to stabilize pronunciation, grammar, spelling, and word meaning so people could reliably communicate over space and time.

all_claims.text: Standard American English refers to a normalization of unspoken knowledge as much as it refers to grammar and spelling.
all_claims.source_passage: Since the 1960s a further insight has become widely accepted among socio- and psycholinguists—that the normalization of language has entailed a normalization of the unspoken knowledge that makes the language meaningful to its users.

all_claims.text: The formal register of language taught in schools is the essential instrument of communication in the public sphere.
all_claims.source_passage: This formal register (taught in schools) is the instrument of communication in the public sphere.

all_claims.text: A student cannot master Standard American English without mastering standard American knowledge.
all_claims.source_passage: In short, a student cannot master Standard American English without mastering standard American knowledge.

all_claims.text: Global education must be conducted through the medium of a national language built upon shared knowledge.
all_claims.source_passage: But a global education must be conducted through the medium of a national language built upon shared knowledge.

all_claims.text: Unspoken knowledge is a necessary, silent component of every language transaction.
all_claims.source_passage: Unspoken knowledge must be silently shared in every language transaction.

all_claims.text: Schools that ignore the necessity of shared knowledge exclude disadvantaged children from language mastery.
all_claims.source_passage: When schools ignore the educational imperatives of that psychological truth, they exclude disadvantaged children from language mastery.

all_claims.text: Providing universal linguistic competence via shared knowledge is the foundational duty of democratic elementary schools.
all_claims.source_passage: To provide this universal linguistic competence is the foundational aim and duty of the elementary schools of a democracy.

all_claims.text: The concept of 'spontaneity' in education falsely assumes the child's brain develops like a ball of string unwinding naturally.
all_claims.source_passage: It produced curious aberrations like ‘spontaneity,’ which supposes that the child’s brain is like a ball of string that the teacher should help to unwind.

all_claims.text: Education is a struggle against elemental biological instincts intended to produce an 'up-to-date' person for the current era.
all_claims.source_passage: Education must oppose the elemental biological instincts of nature; it is a struggle against nature, to dominate it and produce the ‘up-to-date’ person of the new era.

all_claims.text: The educational concept of child development as a 'natural unfolding' has interfered with the primary goal of early education, which is socialization.
all_claims.source_passage: Gramsci makes a key observation about why the new ideas about child development as an unfolding of the child have interfered with the chief point of early education—socialization

all_claims.text: Schools must constantly update the shared, taken-for-granted knowledge of the 'new era' to ensure community and competence.
all_claims.source_passage: The cloud of taken-for-granted knowledge in “the new era” constantly needs updating in the schools to ensure competence and community.

all_claims.text: Imparting the communal knowledge of the public sphere should not be considered cultural imperialism.
all_claims.source_passage: We must not be intimidated by the reproach that imparting the communal knowledge of the public sphere is “cultural imperialism.”

all_claims.text: The United States was historically conceived as an 'internal empire' of quasi-sovereign states rather than an external imperial power.
all_claims.source_passage: The United States was conceived as a mutually beneficial internal empire of quasi-sovereign states. Some of the founders used the term empire for the nation as an internal, not external, idea.

all_claims.text: Universities should create an inventory of the specific knowledge and vocabulary required for college and career readiness.
all_claims.source_passage: The universities could greatly help education and the public sphere by creating an inventory of the knowledge and vocabulary that makes a person college and career ready.

all_claims.text: Twenty-first-century skills depend on high verbal abilities, which are themselves dependent on a shared inventory of knowledge and words.
all_claims.source_passage: Those skills depend upon high verbal abilities, which in turn depend upon possessing the shared inventory of knowledge and words that make Standard American English useable and effective.

all_claims.text: The Common Core language arts standards should be interpreted as requiring an elementary curriculum that fosters enabling knowledge.
all_claims.source_passage: The language arts standards of the Common Core are best interpreted as requiring an elementary curriculum that fosters such enabling knowledge.

all_claims.text: All modern national cultures are multicultural, artificial constructs sustained by schools and mass communication.
all_claims.source_passage: All modern national cultures are multicultural. Philosopher and social anthropologist Ernest Gellner brilliantly showed that they are artificial constructs sustained by schools and mass communication.

all_claims.text: Modern nations utilize a common written language, sustained by dictionaries and schools, to break the traditional connection between language and tribe.
all_claims.source_passage: To achieve this social-political result, the traditional connection between language and tribe had to be broken. In the eighteenth century new modern nations devised a common written language as the basis for the new national political entity, artificially sustained by dictionaries and schools.

all_claims.text: The American 'multicultural' campaign of the 1980s and 90s was a conscious political effort to diminish male WASP dominance and elevate minorities.
all_claims.source_passage: The slow mixing process in America became both conscious and political in the 1980s and 1990s, as part of a leveling campaign to diminish male WASP dominance, and to elevate the status of women and nonwhite, non-Anglo minorities.

all_claims.text: Terms enter the national 'cultural literacy' list based on their frequency of use by writers and schools without further definition.
all_claims.source_passage: If schools taught it across the land, and if print and TV writers and now bloggers used the phrase often without defining it, then indeed it would come to appear on such a list.

all_claims.text: Educational inequality is diminished by teaching 'have-nots' the specific knowledge that the 'haves' currently possess exclusively.
all_claims.source_passage: My practical suggestion was that a sense of community would be served and inequality diminished if the schools taught everyone, both the haves and have-nots, the knowledge that the haves alone currently possessed.

all_claims.text: The knowledge differential between social classes is a major cause of the income differential.
all_claims.source_passage: I claimed, and still do, that this knowledge differential is a major cause of the income differential. (That there is a strong correlation no one denies.)

all_claims.text: The 1987 Cultural Literacy list described the actual knowledge of literate, high-income Americans rather than a theoretical ideal.
all_claims.source_passage: The list at the back of the book was actually a list of what literate Americans who made a good living and could communicate with other Americans in fact did know in 1987, but which less fortunate Americans, who did not make a good living, tended not to know.

all_claims.text: Teaching the specific knowledge possessed by the middle class to all students is a means to diminish inequality.
all_claims.source_passage: My practical suggestion was that a sense of community would be served and inequality diminished if the schools taught everyone, both the haves and have-nots, the knowledge that the haves alone currently possessed.

all_claims.text: Possessing a specific shared vocabulary provides economic prowess because it enables communication and learning.
all_claims.source_passage: People who have that vocabulary are privileged partly because they possess that vocabulary. It enables them to communicate and learn, giving them economic prowess.

all_claims.text: Educational changes to the shared vocabulary must not come at the expense of depriving poor children of current intellectual currency.
all_claims.source_passage: To change that vocabulary is a fine thing to do, so long as poor children are not disenfranchised and kept poor in the meantime by being deprived of that powerful income producer.

all_claims.text: Skills-based multiculturalism has failed to benefit the social groups it was intended to help over the last thirty years.
all_claims.source_passage: The alternative—skills-based multiculturalism—has, after all, been tried over the past thirty years with little help to the groups it aimed to benefit.

all_claims.text: When a multicultural term becomes part of shared national knowledge, it is no longer 'multicultural' in the strong sense of being separate.
all_claims.source_passage: But note that when such language politics is successful, and the phrase catches on, it is no longer exactly 'multicultural'; it has then become part of what we know in common.

all_claims.text: The culture of the home is distinct from the common language required in the public sphere.
all_claims.source_passage: The culture of the home is not the public sphere. The public sphere deploys a common language.

all_claims.text: So-called 'WASP language' is multicultural in its origins and not inherently tied to a specific race or gender.
all_claims.source_passage: So-called WASP language was already multicultural in its origins. It was not inherently WASP or male.

all_claims.text: Cultural essentialism is a form of tribalism that contradicts the aims of the American experiment.
all_claims.source_passage: Cultural essentialism is tribalism—the very thing the American experiment was created to eliminate.

all_claims.text: Multiculturalists of the 80s and 90s incorrectly believed students could function in society as separate cells if they possessed critical-thinking skills.
all_claims.source_passage: These cells could then all function together if students achieved critical-thinking skills. But neither the critical-thinking idea nor curricular fragmentation has worked out for the social groups that these ideas were supposed to help.

all_claims.text: Liberal democracies are particularistic in that they require a specific, shared communicative instrument.
all_claims.source_passage: Liberal democracies are particularistic in their own special way. They each include a particular shared subset of elements that are often wildly diverse in their origins. Each national democracy has its own particular language as a communicative and unifying instrument.

all_claims.text: Equality of opportunity depends upon all citizens having complete access to shared national knowledge.
all_claims.source_passage: All citizens should have complete access to that shared knowledge. Equality of opportunity depends upon it.

all_claims.text: The multiculturalist movement was animated by a protest against particularism, seeking equal status for groups previously discriminated against.
all_claims.source_passage: It was a protest against particularism—that is, against some narrow, tribal group taking charge, instead of equal status being open to all, including those who had been discriminated against.

all_claims.text: Multicultural societies are themselves particularistic, and liberal democracies include a specific shared subset of diverse elements.
all_claims.source_passage: But as it was put into effect, a subtle truth was lost—as such truths tend to be in fierce activisms and polarizations. That truth is that multicultural societies are particularistic too. Liberal democracies are particularistic in their own special way. They each include a particular shared subset of elements that are often wildly diverse in their origins.

all_claims.text: Each national democracy possesses its own particular language that serves as a communicative and unifying instrument.
all_claims.source_passage: Each national democracy has its own particular language as a communicative and unifying instrument that is changing, but at any period it is what it is.

all_claims.text: The public sphere is sustained by the vocabulary and conventions of Standard American English and the communal knowledge that enables its use.
all_claims.source_passage: But there is also the public sphere, sustained by the vocabulary and conventions of Standard American English, and the communal knowledge that enables its effective use.

all_claims.text: Proficiency in the language of the public sphere bestows financial and communicative advantages to all who master it.
all_claims.source_passage: Proficiency with that instrument bestows financial and communicative advantages to all who master it.

all_claims.text: Schools deny students equality of opportunity if they do not provide the knowledge needed to wield the language of the public sphere.
all_claims.source_passage: If schools do not provide that knowledge, they are denying their students equality of opportunity.

all_claims.text: Romantic multiculturalism must be revised to prevent the economic exclusion of those who have not mastered shared knowledge.
all_claims.source_passage: Though well meant, romantic multiculturalism needs to be revised and transformed so it doesn’t perpetuate the economic exclusion of those who have not mastered the shared knowledge required by effective reading, writing, speaking, and listening.

all_claims.text: A shared school curriculum is the primary historical instrument of national unity and equal opportunity.
all_claims.source_passage: A well-rounded, constantly updated school curriculum that transmits the shared knowledge of the public sphere has always been the primary instrument of national unity and equal opportunity.

all_claims.text: The Common Core's skill-based provisions can work against its goals of providing a rich, coherent curriculum.
all_claims.source_passage: I have shown that its skill provisions are less than ideal, and they sometimes work against its provisions favoring a rich, coherent curriculum.

all_claims.text: Common Core's current testing models are as unproductive as the failed tests under No Child Left Behind.
all_claims.source_passage: Its current tests are no more productive in principle than the kinds of tests that have already failed under No Child Left Behind.

all_claims.text: The greatest virtues of Common Core ELA are increasing text difficulty and requiring that half of the texts be informational.
all_claims.source_passage: The greatest virtues of the Common Core ELA Standards are their raising of the vocabulary difficulty of texts at each grade level, and their insistence that half of the texts be “informational.”

all_claims.text: Common Core can only become transformative if districts and states implement a specific, grade-by-grade knowledge-building curriculum.
all_claims.source_passage: Districts and states can make the Common Core ELA Standards transformative, should they choose to do so, by proposing a specific and coherent curriculum that builds knowledge systematically from one grade to the next, starting in the earliest grades.

all_claims.text: Current state and Common Core language standards are vague 'Rorschach blots' that require specific content to become reality.
all_claims.source_passage: Our state language standards and Common Core language standards are currently Rorschach blots that must be formed into a reality, whatever name we give them.

all_claims.text: Higher achievement and reduced inequality are only possible through specific content standards.
all_claims.source_passage: It’s the only feasible route to higher average achievement and a reduction of educational inequality. Specificity and courage are needed.

all_claims.text: The practice of marking texts with highlighters in early grades is an ineffective strategy for increasing knowledge and vocabulary.
all_claims.source_passage: The task of close reading in early grades has caused a big boost in the sale of highlight markers, which enable children to practice marking up their books. That is not a very good way to increase knowledge and vocabulary.

all_claims.text: Test makers are morally blameless for providing texts on unknown domains because the school curriculum is unstated.
all_claims.source_passage: Since that uncertainty is not their fault, the test makers are also morally blameless in this situation. They are compelled to offer texts on domains of knowledge not studied in school, since the school domains are unstated and unknown.

all_claims.text: State-of-the-art translation programs like Google Translate fail at understanding because the relationship between close reading of words and actual meaning is inherently indeterminate.
all_claims.source_passage: That’s why state-of-the-art translation programs such as Google Translate are so unreliable. They are very good at close reading, but poor at understanding, since the relation between close reading and meaning is inherently indeterminate.

all_claims.text: The Bible contains no positive uses of the word 'imagine' or its derivatives.
all_claims.source_passage: No positive uses of the word imagine are to be found in the Bible. Imagination is most likely to be an evil lie, given the unsavory character of unredeemed human nature.

all_claims.text: Sir Philip Sidney's theory of literature posits that its essence is fixing moral truth in the mind more effectively than history or philosophy.
all_claims.source_passage: The sixteenth-century English poet and scholar Sir Philip Sidney said that the very essence of good literature is that it fixes moral truth in the mind and heart better than history and philosophy, not that it invents some new world.

all_claims.text: The elementary school acts as a 'single channel' of shared experience that all children must listen to for several hours every day.
all_claims.source_passage: The elementary school is a single channel that children must listen to many hours of the day. It is still the place where all children have the possibility of learning the shared knowledge and values that make a nation operational.

all_claims.text: Standard Spanish is globally updated and fixed by a specific two-volume dictionary from the Academy in Madrid.
all_claims.source_passage: Standard Spanish, all over the world, at least in its formal written manifestations, is still fixed and updated by the two-volume dictionary published by the Academy in Madrid, Diccionario de la lengua española de la Real Academia Española.

all_claims.text: The United States was historically conceived as an 'internal empire' that should tolerate local attachments while spreading systematic ideas.
all_claims.source_passage: There have been in history benign empires (the Roman Empire at times) that did not force people to give up their local languages, modes, and customs, and the United States has been one of them. The United States was conceived as a mutually beneficial internal empire of quasi-sovereign states.

all_claims.text: Membership in the public sphere's language community is automatically granted to anyone who possesses the necessary shared knowledge.
all_claims.source_passage: Membership in the language community of the public sphere is granted to any and all who possess the knowledge needed to wield that instrument of power and opportunity.

all_claims.text: Schools must prioritize knowledge-centric statements in the Common Core over skill-centric ones if they expect to see superior results in twelfth-grade students.
all_claims.source_passage: Those knowledge-centric statements are the ones that must predominate if progress is to be made. If the Core standards that focus on knowledge are interpreted as the dominant ones, overshadowing the statements about skills, then we can expect twelfth-grade results to be superior...

all_claims.text: The educational decline in French elementary schools following 1989 constitutes a 'débâcle' equivalent in gravity to France's 1940 military defeat.
all_claims.source_passage: To speak of “The Educational Fall of France” seems at first rhetorical overkill—equating mere lower scores in French elementary schools with the military defeat of France in World War II. But it isn’t just an over-the-top play on words. It was the explicit theme of an important 2007 book edited by Laurent Lafforgue and Liliane Lurçat: La Débâcle de l’École: Une Tragédie Incomprise.

all_claims.text: The decline of the French school system was caused by avoidable intellectual mistakes made by top intellectuals in 1989.
all_claims.source_passage: His view, which I agree with, is that top French intellectuals made big avoidable mistakes in 1989, just as higher-ups had made serious, avoidable military mistakes in 1940.

all_claims.text: France's adoption of skill-based, individualized educational principles caused it to decline from one of the best and most equitable systems to one of the worst and least equitable.
