all_claims.source_passage: Noteworthy, too, is the Japanese inclusion of aesthetic and evaluative elements in the study of science, just as they include “morality” as an element in humanistic subjects—an indication of their view that education is socialization.

all_claims.text: The American fear of 'indoctrination' leads to the hiding of values, which in turn breeds suspicion of schools.
all_claims.source_passage: The American concern to avoid “indoctrination” simply places implicit values out of sight and raises suspicions about the schools, because the values are not openly stated in the curriculum.

all_claims.text: Teaching civic virtues and values in a state curriculum does not violate the separation of church and state.
all_claims.source_passage: The separation of church and state is not the separation of honesty, diligence, kindness, loyalty, and other civic virtues from the state. It does not mean abandoning the teaching of civics.

all_claims.text: Failing to teach values leads to rootlessness and incivility in a society.
all_claims.source_passage: To teach no values at all is to evade an elemental duty, and lead to rootlessness and incivility.

all_claims.text: The school curriculum should be an 'open covenant'—publicly stated and transparent for the community.
all_claims.source_passage: “Open covenants openly arrived at” should be the watchword of the published school curriculum. For every community, what could be more important?

all_claims.text: Typical American state science standards are non-analytical, amateurish, and stay on a 'phenomenal' level that is the antithesis of true science.
all_claims.source_passage: Note that this document stays on the non-analytical phenomenal level, which is the antithesis of science... This sloppiness is characteristic of amateurish state “standards”

all_claims.text: The developmental approach in American education causes curriculum sloppiness by trying to avoid teaching concepts too early.
all_claims.source_passage: amateurish perhaps because the developmental approach to education tries to avoid “premature” concepts.

all_claims.text: American state science standards lack a multiyear, cumulative plan for building student knowledge.
all_claims.source_passage: Moreover, the phenomenal observations, which many children already know, do not fit into any larger multiyear, cumulative plan.

all_claims.text: The Japanese Grade 3 science curriculum aims to develop ideas about weight, wind, force, rubber, light, magnets, and electricity through investigative comparison of phenomena.
all_claims.source_passage: To develop perspectives and ideas about the properties of weight, wind, force of rubber, light, and magnets and electricity through investigation comparing phenomena involving these matters

all_claims.text: The Japanese curriculum explicitly teaches students that an object's weight remains unchanged even if its shape changes.
all_claims.source_passage: a) the weight of an object remains unchanged even when the shape changes

all_claims.text: The Japanese curriculum requires Grade 3 students to learn that objects with identical volumes may have different weights.
all_claims.source_passage: b) objects with the same volume may differ in weight.

all_claims.text: The Grade 3 curriculum specifies that light travels in particular ways and that sunlight produces varying levels of brightness and warmth upon striking objects.
all_claims.source_passage: exploring the way light travels and its brightness and warmth when it strikes an object: a) sunlight can be collected and reflected; b) the brightness and warmth of sunlight changes when it strikes an object.

all_claims.text: Grade 3 students are taught the specific biological fact that insect bodies consist of three parts: the head, thorax, and abdomen.
all_claims.source_passage: a) insects grow in accordance with a fixed order of growth, and their body parts consist of the head, thorax, and abdomen

all_claims.text: The Japanese science curriculum mandates that students learn the cardinal directions (east, west, north, and south) specifically in the context of the sun's movement.
all_claims.source_passage: In addition, the four directions: east, west, north, and south are dealt with when exploring the movement of the sun.

all_claims.text: The Japanese curriculum requires pupils to create at least three distinct types of learning materials when studying Matter and Energy.
all_claims.source_passage: (1) In teaching “A. Matter/Energy” in Content, pupils must make at least three kinds of learning materials.

all_claims.text: Grade 4 students learn the scientific distinction that air in a closed space is compressible while water is not.
all_claims.source_passage: b) air in a closed space can be compressed, but water cannot be compressed.

all_claims.text: The Grade 4 curriculum teaches that heat transfer in metals occurs through conduction spreading from a point, whereas heat in air and water moves to raise the temperature of the whole.
all_claims.source_passage: b) the temperature of metals goes up gradually, spreading from the point being heated, but heated air and water move, raising the temperature of the whole

all_claims.text: The curriculum specifies that students must raise actual insects and plants to develop their understanding of growth patterns and body structures.
all_claims.source_passage: a) with regard to items a and b pupils must raise insects and grow plants

all_claims.text: The Japanese Grade 4 science curriculum aims to develop pupils’ ideas about the properties of air and water by exploring volume and pressure in a closed space.
all_claims.source_passage: To develop pupils’ ideas about the properties of air and water by exploring the changes in their volume and pressure in compressing air and water in a closed space

all_claims.text: Air in a closed space can be compressed, but water cannot be compressed.
all_claims.source_passage: air in a closed space can be compressed, but water cannot be compressed.

all_claims.text: The volume of metals, water, or air changes when heated or cooled.
all_claims.source_passage: the volume of metals, water, or air changes when heated or cooled

all_claims.text: The volume of water increases when it freezes into ice.
all_claims.source_passage: When water becomes ice, its volume increases.

all_claims.text: The brightness of a bulb and the rotation of a motor are dependent on the number or circuit configuration of dry batteries.
all_claims.source_passage: the brightness and the rotation of a motor change as the number or circuit of dry batteries changes

all_claims.text: The human body moves through the combined functions of bones and muscles.
all_claims.source_passage: the human body can move due to the functions of bones and muscles.

all_claims.text: Plant growth and animal activities are seasonal and vary based on temperature.
all_claims.source_passage: the activities of animals change depending on the season (warm/cold); b) the growth of plants changes depending on the season (warm/cold).

all_claims.text: Water temperature and weather conditions determine the process of evaporation and condensation.
all_claims.source_passage: water evaporates from the surface of water or the ground and turns into vapor in the air. In addition, vapor in the air may turn back into water drops (condensation).

all_claims.text: While the alignment of stars remains constant, their position in the sky changes throughout the day.
all_claims.source_passage: the alignment of a cluster of stars does not change, but the position of the cluster changes throughout the day.

all_claims.text: Japanese science education requires students to engage in hands-on creation of learning materials for matter and energy.
all_claims.source_passage: In teaching “A. Matters/Energy” in Content, pupils must make at least two kinds of learning materials.

all_claims.text: Grade 5 science focuses on the regularity of change in objects and substances by investigating causality.
all_claims.source_passage: To develop perspectives and ideas about the regularity of change in objects and substances... focusing on the causes of their changes

all_claims.text: The Japanese Grade 5 science curriculum requires students to understand that the weight of water and a solute remains unchanged when the solute is dissolved.
all_claims.source_passage: c) the weight of water and a solute remains unchanged when the solute is dissolved in water.

all_claims.text: Solubility limits are determined by the temperature and volume of the solvent.
all_claims.source_passage: the solubility limits change according to the temperature and amount of water or solutes

all_claims.text: The Japanese curriculum teaches Grade 5 students that the swing time of a pendulum is affected by the length of the string but not by the weight of the pendulum.
all_claims.source_passage: a) the time taken for a weight on a string to swing back and forth does not change if the weight changes, but it does change when the length of the string changes.

all_claims.text: The Japanese curriculum requires Grade 5 students to learn that the polarity of an electromagnet changes when the direction of the electric current changes.
all_claims.source_passage: a) a coil with an electric current magnetizes an iron core, and when the direction of the electric current changes, the polarity of the electromagnet changes

all_claims.text: The Japanese science curriculum specifies that Grade 5 students must learn the specific roles of sunlight, fertilizer, water, air, and temperature in plant growth and germination.
all_claims.source_passage: b) it is water, air, and temperature that influence plant germination; c) sunlight and fertilizer affect plant growth

all_claims.text: The Japanese curriculum mandates that Grade 5 students study human growth inside the mother.
all_claims.source_passage: c) humans grow inside the mother until they are born.

all_claims.text: Japanese Grade 5 students are taught the specific geological functions of running water, including erosion (cutting), transportation, and deposition (piling up).
all_claims.source_passage: a) running water has functions to cut into the ground, transport, and pile up pebbles and soil

all_claims.text: The Japanese curriculum includes pedagogical constraints that limit the scope of specific topics to ensure developmental appropriateness or focus, such as limiting the study of seed nutrition to starch.
all_claims.source_passage: a) with regard to item (1a), “nutrition within seeds,” only starch should be dealt with

all_claims.text: The Japanese science curriculum explicitly prohibits dealing with the process of human fertilization in Grade 5.
all_claims.source_passage: (3) With regard to item (2c) in “B. Life/the Earth” in Content, do not deal with the process of fertilization.

all_claims.text: Grade 6 science objectives in Japan include fostering a 'respect for life' alongside analytical perspectives on physical functions.
all_claims.source_passage: (2) To foster an attitude of respect for life and to develop perspectives and ideas about the physical functions of living things

all_claims.text: The Japanese science curriculum requires students to understand the chemical mechanism of combustion, specifically that plants burn by consuming oxygen and producing carbon dioxide.
all_claims.source_passage: Mechanism of combustion. To develop pupils’ ideas about the mechanism of combustion, by burning objects and by exploring the changes in them: a) when plants burn, oxygen in the air is used and carbon dioxide is produced.

all_claims.text: Students in the Japanese system are expected to distinguish between alkaline, acid, and neutral aqueous solutions and understand their effects on metals.
all_claims.source_passage: Properties of aqueous solutions. To develop pupils’ ideas about the properties and functions of aqueous solutions... a) there are alkaline, acid, and neutral aqueous solutions; b) gas is dissolved in some aqueous solutions; c) some aqueous solutions change the properties of metals.

all_claims.text: The curriculum mandates a mathematical and physical understanding of the lever, including the regularity between force point, strength, and power when balanced.
all_claims.source_passage: Regularity of a lever. To develop pupils’ ideas about the regularity of a lever... a certain regularity exists among the force point, the strength, and the power; c) tools using this regularity of a lever can be found in everyday life.

all_claims.text: The Japanese science curriculum teaches the generation, storage, and transformation of electricity into light, sound, and heat.
all_claims.source_passage: Use of electricity. To develop pupils’ ideas about the properties and the function of electricity... a) electricity can be generated and stored; b) electricity can be transformed into light, sound, heat, etc.

all_claims.text: The amount of heat generated by a heat wire is explicitly linked to the thickness of the wire in the grade-level curriculum.
all_claims.source_passage: c) the amount of heat generated by a heat wire depends on the thickness of the wire

all_claims.text: The curriculum requires students to understand human anatomy and physiology, including the specific functions of respiration, digestion, excretion, and circulation.
all_claims.source_passage: Structure and functions of the human body. To develop pupils’ ideas about the structure and function of the human body and other animals... exploring the functions of respiration, digestion, excretion, and circulation

all_claims.text: Japanese science education includes the specific transport mechanisms of blood, including the role of the heart as a pump for oxygen and carbon dioxide.
all_claims.source_passage: c) blood travels through the body, is pumped through its course by the heart, and transports nutrition, oxygen, and carbon dioxide.

all_claims.text: The curriculum emphasizes the chemical process of photosynthesis, specifically that sunlight hitting leaves produces starch.
all_claims.source_passage: Nutrition of plants and pathway of water... a) when sunlight hits leaves, starch is produced in the leaves

all_claims.text: Environmental science in the Japanese curriculum includes the concept of the 'eat-or-be-eaten' world and the circulation of water.
all_claims.source_passage: Living things and the environment... b) animals live in an eat-or-be-eaten world. ... it should be mentioned that water circulates.

all_claims.text: The curriculum covers complex geological concepts, including the formation of strata through running water and volcanic eruptions.
all_claims.source_passage: Formation and change of land... b) geological strata are formed by running water and volcanic eruptions, some of which contain fossil remains

all_claims.text: The Japanese curriculum treats fossils not just as artifacts, but as specific evidence that geological strata were piled up by running water.
all_claims.source_passage: b) fossils in (4b) should be dealt with as evidence that strata had been piled up by running water.

all_claims.text: Astronomy in the curriculum focuses on the relative positional relationship between the sun and the moon when viewed from Earth to explain moon phases.
all_claims.source_passage: The moon and the sun... a) the sun is located on the bright side of the moon, and the moon phase changes depending on the positional relationship between the moon and the sun

all_claims.text: Teaching 'Handling of Content' requires students to engage in tactile, constructive learning by making at least two kinds of objects for physical science units.
all_claims.source_passage: In teaching “A. Matter/Energy” in Content, pupils must make at least two kinds of objects.

all_claims.text: The Japanese curriculum mandates the teaching of specific, high-level vocabulary for internal organs, including the liver and kidneys, at the elementary level.
all_claims.source_passage: b) with regard to (1d), lungs, the stomach, small intestines, large intestines, liver, kidneys, and heart should be dealt with as major organs.

all_claims.text: The overarching goal of the Japanese syllabus design is to consolidate scientific knowledge and concepts to develop scientific perspectives through observation.
all_claims.source_passage: In designing the syllabus, consideration should be given to the following: (1) Some considerations should be given to consolidating scientific knowledge and concepts and to developing scientific perspectives and ideas by enriching observation

all_claims.text: Scientific knowledge and concepts should be consolidated through the enrichment of observation, experiments, and direct experience in nature.
all_claims.source_passage: Some considerations should be given to consolidating scientific knowledge and concepts and to developing scientific perspectives and ideas by enriching observation, experiments, experience in nature, and scientific experience in teaching the Content of each grade

all_claims.text: Pupils should be required to explain natural events and phenomena using specific scientific terms and concepts.
all_claims.source_passage: Some considerations should be given to enrich activities that pupils can organize and in which they can examine the results of observations and experiments, and can think and explain natural events and phenomena by using scientific terms and concepts.

all_claims.text: Effective science education requires active partnership and cooperation with museums and science centers.
all_claims.source_passage: Some considerations should be given to utilize museums and science centers actively by seeking partnership and cooperation with them.

all_claims.text: Science instruction should be explicitly aligned and related to the objectives of national moral education.
all_claims.source_passage: The instructions should be in accordance with the characteristics of science and should be related to the period for moral education.

all_claims.text: Appropriate instructional devices, including computers and audio-visual aids, should be used during science observations and experiments.
all_claims.source_passage: In giving instructions on observations, experiments, cultivation, raising animals, and making learning materials, appropriate devices should be used, such as computers and audio-visual aids.

all_claims.text: Frequent field opportunities and experiential activities are necessary to familiarize pupils with nature and develop an attitude of environmental conservation.
all_claims.source_passage: it is necessary to give pupils enough opportunities to go to the field and have experiential activities to familiarize them with nature, and at the same time, to help pupils develop an attitude of cherishing nature and contributing to the conservation of the natural environment.

all_claims.text: Science education should encourage pupils to take initiative in solving problems independently.
all_claims.source_passage: It is necessary to encourage every pupil to take initiatives in solving problems

all_claims.text: Connections must be made between scientific learning outcomes and everyday life to achieve a realistic understanding of phenomena.
all_claims.source_passage: to make connections between the outcomes of learning and everyday life, and to help them gain realistic understanding about natural events and phenomena.

all_claims.text: The curriculum must specify exactly which biological organs (lungs, stomach, heart, etc.) and geological types (sandstone, mudstone, etc.) are to be treated as major content items.
all_claims.source_passage: lungs, the stomach, small intestines, large intestines, liver, kidneys, and heart should be dealt with as major organs... conglomerates, sandstone, and mudstone should be dealt with as rocks

all_claims.text: Fossils should be taught specifically as evidence of the geological process of strata being accumulated by running water.
all_claims.source_passage: fossils in (4b) should be dealt with as evidence that strata had been piled up by running water.

all_claims.text: Japanese elementary schools allocate significantly more instructional time to music and art than they do to science.
all_claims.source_passage: More time is allocated to music and art than to science. Moreover, science study does not even begin until grade 3, and yet Japanese students score at the top of the rankings in science.

all_claims.text: The common definition of dissolving as 'disappearing' in American state standards is a scientific misstatement characteristic of an amateurish approach to education.
all_claims.source_passage: And it makes misstatements: 'To dissolve is to “disappear.”' This sloppiness is characteristic of amateurish state 'standards'—amateurish perhaps because the developmental approach to education tries to avoid 'premature' concepts.

all_claims.text: Civic virtues such as honesty, diligence, kindness, and loyalty are not exclusively religious and should not be separated from state education.
all_claims.source_passage: The separation of church and state is not the separation of honesty, diligence, kindness, loyalty, and other civic virtues from the state. It does not mean abandoning the teaching of civics.

all_claims.text: A primary objective of early science education should be to nurture students' hearts and minds with a genuine affection for the natural world.
all_claims.source_passage: To enable pupils to become familiar with nature and to carry out observations and experiments with their own viewpoint, as well as to develop their problem-solving abilities and nurture hearts and minds that are filled with an affection for the natural world...

all_claims.text: In Grade 3, the Japanese curriculum explicitly mandates teaching that the sun moves specifically from east to west.
all_claims.source_passage: With regard to item (3a), 'movement of the sun,' in 'B. Life/the Earth,' it should be regarded that the sun moves from east to west.

all_claims.text: The Japanese Grade 4 science curriculum requires the longitudinal observation of at least two different animals and two different plants over the course of an entire year.
all_claims.source_passage: With regard to item (2) in 'B. Life/the Earth' in Content, the activities of at least two animals and the growth of at least two plants should be observed over the course of one year.

all_claims.text: Elementary science instruction must include the specific physiological correlation between human heartbeats and pulsations.
all_claims.source_passage: With regard to (1c), it should be mentioned that there is a correlation between heartbeats and pulsations;

all_claims.text: The Japanese curriculum identifies conglomerate, sandstone, and mudstone as the specific types of rocks to be mastered in Grade 6 earth science.
all_claims.source_passage: with regard to (4a), conglomerates, sandstone, and mudstone should be dealt with as rocks;

all_claims.text: Science instruction has a specific duty to prevent accidents during practical activities such as cultivation, raising animals, and experiments.
all_claims.source_passage: In giving instructions on observations, experiments, cultivation, raising animals, and making learning materials... special care must be taken to prevent accidents.

all_claims.text: To teach no values in a school curriculum is to evade an elemental duty and leads to societal rootlessness and incivility.
all_claims.source_passage: To teach no values at all is to evade an elemental duty, and lead to rootlessness and incivility.

all_evidence.description: Research identifying the achievement gap as a knowledge and language gap.
all_evidence.source_reference: John Guthrie, Betty Hart, and Todd Risley

all_evidence.description: A 1790 pamphlet advocating for common education for children to support egalitarianism.
all_evidence.source_reference: Condorcet, A Common Education for Children (1790)

all_evidence.description: Historical letter urging teachers to impart knowledge common and indispensable to all.
all_evidence.source_reference: Jules Ferry, Letter to Teachers (1883)

all_evidence.description: Public statement asserting that identical knowledge for all children reduces inequality.
all_evidence.source_reference: Giscard d’Estaing (1977)

all_evidence.description: Radical shift in French law moving from national to local/individualized curriculum.
all_evidence.source_reference: Loi Jospin (1989)

all_evidence.description: French Ministry of Education longitudinal studies comparing 10-year-olds' achievement before and after 1989.
all_evidence.source_reference: French Ministry of Education researchers (cited in Endnote 7)
all_evidence.source_passage: The broad new law enabled the Ministry of Education to conduct longitudinal studies comparing the effects of the communal elementary curriculum before 1989 with those of the individualized, skill-centered curriculums that followed.

all_evidence.description: Comparison of sample sizes between French longitudinal studies (4,200/1M) and NAEP (8,700/6M).
all_evidence.source_reference: NAEP and French Ministry studies (cited in Endnote 11)
all_evidence.source_passage: The French longitudinal studies are based on a sample size of forty-two hundred students from an age cohort of one million... the two samplings yield very similar levels of confidence.

all_evidence.description: Objective measures of French teacher quality, budgets, and school buildings remaining constant over time.
all_evidence.source_reference: Positions du grip sur la crise de l'école (Endnote 8)
all_evidence.source_passage: Teacher quality stayed the same by objective measures. School buildings and budgets did not change significantly.

all_evidence.description: NAEP records showing verbal scores for 17-year-olds have remained low and flat since 1971.
all_evidence.source_reference: National Assessment of Educational Progress (NAEP), 1971-present
all_evidence.source_passage: The verbal scores of our seventeen-year-olds have stayed low and largely unchanged ever since NAEP began recording them in 1971.

all_evidence.description: Research showing the narrowing of the black-white education gap until recent decades.
all_evidence.source_reference: John Bishop, 'Is the Test Score Decline Responsible for the Productivity Growth Decline?' (1989)
all_evidence.source_passage: Moreover, as John Bishop has long pointed out, the education gap between blacks and whites had narrowed steadily until recent decades.

all_evidence.description: Cross-national studies of science achievement showing US performance in the mid-20th century.
all_evidence.source_reference: J. P. Keeves, 'Learning Science in a Changing World' (1992)
all_evidence.source_passage: As late as the 1940s and 1950s the public education of the United States, for all its racial and social shortcomings, scored near the top among nations in both achievement and equality.

all_evidence.description: Historical reporting on the 'A Nation at Risk' commission following the 1970s score decline.
all_evidence.source_reference: National Commission on Excellence in Education, 'A Nation at Risk' (1983)
all_evidence.source_passage: Those puzzling disasters caused the Reagan administration to convene a national commission that produced the famous alarmist report A Nation at Risk (1983).

all_evidence.description: Comparison of community-centered schools in Finland and Japan vs. child-centered schools in the US and France.
The Core Knowledge Sequence (1990) as a functional exemplification of communal knowledge.
French student performance data from 1975-1985 showing high achievement and equality.
The historical presence of common historical and cultural vocabulary in early grade curricula in American districts prior to the 'expanding environments' model.
PISA 2012 results showing American students below nations with communal curricula in problem-solving abilities.
International assessment data showing that differentiated curricula and tracking are negatively associated with student performance.
all_evidence.source_reference: Elliott A. Medrich, International Mathematics and Science Assessment: What Have We Learned?, NCES 92-011, 1992
all_evidence.source_passage: Use of a differentiated curriculum based on tracking is negatively associated with student performance on the international assessments and also reduces opportunities for some students to be exposed to more advanced curriculum.

all_evidence.description: The Cambridge Handbook of Expertise and Expert Performance, summarizing findings that refutes the concept of content-independent general skills.
all_evidence.source_reference: K. Anders Ericsson et al., The Cambridge Handbook of Expertise and Expert Performance (2006)
all_evidence.source_passage: research on thinking skills is now a well-developed field, and its findings are fatal to this crucial refuge of current educational theory.

all_evidence.description: A review of cognitive science literature showing that evidence for the existence of learning styles (Visual, Auditory, Kinesthetic) is non-existent.
all_evidence.source_reference: Daniel Willingham, 'Ask the Cognitive Scientist,' American Educator (2005)
all_evidence.source_passage: The evidence for individual learning styles is weak to nonexistent.

all_evidence.description: A comprehensive summary of research in cognitive psychology rejecting the idea of general abilities independent of content.
all_evidence.source_reference: Paul J. Feltovich, Michael J. Prietula, and K. Anders Ericsson, 'Studies of Expertise from Psychological Perspectives,' in The Cambridge Handbook of Expertise and Expert Performance (2006)
all_evidence.source_passage: Research clearly rejects the classical views on human cognition in which general abilities such as learning, reasoning, problem solving, and concept formation correspond to capacities and abilities that can be studied independently of the content domains.

all_evidence.description: Asserts that developing a general skill is a scientific impossibility.
all_evidence.source_reference: Anders Ericsson and Robert Pool, Peak: Secrets from the New Science of Expertise (2016)
all_evidence.source_passage: There is no such thing as developing a general skill.

all_evidence.description: PISA scores showing that countries with communal, content-rich curricula outperform the US in creative problem-solving.
all_evidence.source_reference: PISA 2012 Results: Creative Problem Solving Students’ Skills in Tackling Real-Life Problems (2014)
all_evidence.source_passage: The most recent PISA scores for the problem-solving abilities of fifteen-year-olds place American students well below Korea, Japan, Canada, and Finland, nations with set, communal curricula in early grades

all_evidence.description: Scholarly works tracing the shift and decline in French elementary education, attributing it to the intellectual underpinnings of the 1960s and the 'destruction' of Jules Ferry's model.
all_evidence.source_reference: Philippe Nemo (1991); Liliane Lurçat (1998); Jean-Claude Milner (1984); Jean-Pierre Le Goff (1999); Marc Le Bris (2004)

all_evidence.description: Calculated statistical confidence intervals for French and US longitudinal education data (French: .01509; US: .01050) and relative standard errors (French: 1.54; US: 1.07).
all_evidence.source_reference: French Ministry methodology and NAEP comparison

all_evidence.description: Calculated standard deviation for NAEP scores, which shifted from approximately 50 points prior to 1996 to approximately 35 points thereafter.
all_evidence.source_reference: National Assessment of Educational Progress (NAEP) scoring records

all_evidence.description: Historical analysis of 19th-century American schoolbooks and early grade curricula in history, geography, and civics that shared a common cultural vocabulary prior to the 'expanding environments' model.
all_evidence.source_reference: Diane Ravitch, 'Tot Sociology' (1987); Ruth Miller Elson, 'Guardians of Tradition' (1964)

all_evidence.description: Cross-cultural research demonstrating that while physical development follows universal tracks, different cultures begin imposing differential social and mental impositions from the earliest months of infancy.
all_evidence.source_reference: Patricia M. Greenfield et al., 'Cultural Pathways Through Universal Development,' Annual Review of Psychology 54 (2003): 461–490

all_evidence.description: A meta-analysis of experimental work on teaching science problem-solving which supports the domain-specific nature of expertise over general thinking skills.
all_evidence.source_reference: R. Taconis, M. G. M. Ferguson-Hessler, and H. Broekkamp, 'Teaching Science Problem Solving: An Overview of Experimental Work,' Journal of Research in Science Teaching 38, no. 4 (2001): 442–468

all_evidence.description: NAEP 2012 long-term trend report showing a statistically significant three-point drop in reading scores for 17-year-olds compared to 1988-1992.
all_evidence.source_reference: NAEP 2012 Long Term Trend Reading and Mathematics Assessments
all_evidence.source_passage: Figure 1.1 is taken from the 2012 long-term trend report of the National Assessment of Educational Progress (NAEP)—the latest nationwide analysis we have for long-term reading trends among seventeen-year-olds. It shows a statistically significant drop of three points in the reading abilities of seventeen-year-olds...

all_evidence.description: Analysis of the Armed Forces Qualification Test (AFQT) showing that vocabulary size is the strongest predictor of comprehension and overall competence.
all_evidence.source_reference: Pamela Ing, Carole A. Lunney, and Randall J. Olsen, Reanalysis of the 1980 AFQT Data from the NLSY791 (2007)
all_evidence.source_passage: Analyses of the Armed Forces Qualification Test (AFQT) showed that the vocabulary size test and the paragraph comprehension test correlate so well that they can be considered to represent the same underlying competence, which was most accurately measured by vocabulary size!

all_evidence.description: Correlation between basic skills (specifically verbal ability) and the black-white earnings gap.
all_evidence.source_reference: William R. Johnson and Derek Neal, 'Basic Skills and the Black-White Earnings Gap' (1998)
all_evidence.source_passage: See also: William R. Johnson and Derek Neal, 'Basic Skills and the Black-White Earnings Gap,' in The Black-White Test Score Gap...

all_evidence.description: NAEP (National Assessment of Educational Progress) results showing stagnant or declining scores and widening gaps in older students.
A Nation at Risk (1983) report documenting the decline of American education in the 1960s and 70s.
The Council of Great City Schools report showing urban students take about eight standardized tests a year, occupying 4% of school time.
all_evidence.source_reference: Council of Great City Schools, 2015

all_evidence.description: US Department of Education report (October 2015) expressing concern over excessive testing.
all_evidence.source_reference: US Department of Education, 2015

all_evidence.description: Metastudies on the plateau effect of reading strategy instruction.
all_evidence.source_reference: Rosenshine and Meister, 'Reciprocal Teaching', 1994; Willingham and Lovette, 2014

all_evidence.description: NAEP data showing stagnant reading scores for seventeen-year-olds despite decades of strategy-focused instruction.
all_evidence.source_reference: National Assessment of Educational Progress (NAEP)
all_evidence.source_passage: That’s how we know that our nine- and thirteen-year-olds have improved, and our seventeen-year-olds have not.

all_evidence.description: Internal reliability correlations of approximately .9 for well-calibrated reading tests like Gates and Iowa.
all_evidence.source_reference: Gates-MacGinitie; Iowa Test of Basic Skills
all_evidence.source_passage: Gates and Iowa each show strong internal correlations between different forms of the same test (about .9 on a scale in which 1 is perfect).

all_evidence.description: Study showing that vocabulary size is a stronger predictor of comprehension than formal ability to deal with syntax.
all_evidence.source_reference: Ing, Lunney, and Olsen, 'Reanalysis of the 1980 AFQT Data', 2007
all_evidence.source_passage: This result anticipated recent work showing that topic familiarity, often indicated by word familiarity, is a stronger predictor of comprehension than formal ability to deal with difficult syntax.

all_evidence.description: Lack of improvement in student reading scores despite pervasive schooling in 'main-idea finding' strategies.
Consensus among cognitive scientists that knowledge is the primary component of reading skill post-decoding.
Reports of students in Palm Beach County and elsewhere experiencing physical and mental distress (Xanax use, school refusal) due to testing stress.
all_evidence.source_reference: Alvarez, Lizette. 'States Listen as Parents Give Rampant Testing an F,' New York Times, Nov 9, 2014

all_evidence.description: Comparison of Texas Grade 3-5 math standards (which specify equivalent fractions and unequal denominators) vs. Texas Grade 3-8 reading standards (which repeat the abstract task of summarizing main ideas).
A comparison of Grade 3 through Grade 8 reading standards showing identical 'main idea' requirements across years.
A list of Virginia reading standards (Grades 2-8) that all use the exact phrase 'Identify the main idea.'
Analysis of Common Core standards showing minimal incremental change (e.g., from 'one main idea' to 'two or more') across several grade levels.
Released test 'stems' from Texas state exams that phrase questions in terms of 'main idea' and 'inferencing skills.'
all_evidence.source_reference: Texas tests, released items for 5th and 7th graders

all_evidence.description: A review of research on strategy instruction showing that it reaches a plateau quickly and is most effective when limited to a short period (approx. 10 lessons).
all_evidence.source_reference: Willingham and Lovette, 'Can Reading Comprehension Be Taught?' (2014)
all_evidence.source_passage: The evidence is summarized in a recent review article by Willingham and Lovette, “Can Reading Comprehension Be Taught?” They answer: “Not really.”

all_evidence.description: Sample items released from Texas state tests showing the focus on 'main idea' and 'inferencing' stems.
all_evidence.source_reference: Released test questions in Texas for fifth and seventh graders
all_evidence.source_passage: I offer examples from Texas and Virginia... these stems were taken from released test questions in Texas for fifth and seventh graders.

all_evidence.description: Research showing that making students self-conscious about strategy use can be nonadaptive and distract from performance.
all_evidence.source_reference: Robert S. Siegler, 'Adaptive and Nonadaptive Characteristics of Low-Income Children’s Mathematical Strategy Use'
all_evidence.source_passage: making young students become highly self-conscious about applying strategies distracts their attention and degrades their performance.

all_evidence.description: The persistent lack of improvement in national reading abilities despite decades of strategy-based accountability policies.
all_evidence.source_reference: Noted as 'brute fact' by author
all_evidence.source_passage: A further piece of evidence is the simple brute fact that the policy has not improved the reading abilities of our students when they emerge from our schooling.

all_evidence.description: Sample Grade 3 PARCC test question asking students to define 'avenge' and provide supporting details.
Sample Grade 4 Smarter Balanced question asking for the meaning of 'disgorging' based on context clues about exploding stars.
Comments from teachers on Hirsch's Huffington Post piece stating they must perform test preparation or risk their jobs.
Reanalysis of 1980 AFQT data showing vocabulary size tests and paragraph comprehension tests correlate so highly they measure the same underlying competence.
all_evidence.source_reference: Pamela Ing, Carole A. Lunney, and Randall J. Olsen, Reanalysis of the 1980 AFQT Data from the NLSY791 (2007)
all_evidence.source_passage: Analyses of the Armed Forces Qualification Test (AFQT) showed that the vocabulary size test and the paragraph comprehension test correlate so well that they can be considered to represent the same underlying competence.

all_evidence.description: Evidence that skill-based policies have failed to improve reading abilities in students graduating from the US school system.
all_evidence.source_reference: Willingham and Lovette, 'Can Reading Comprehension Be Taught?' (2014)
all_evidence.source_passage: A further piece of evidence is the simple brute fact that the policy has not improved the reading abilities of our students when they emerge from our schooling.

all_evidence.description: A study examining the evolution of mental ability and its relationship to economic success and schooling, providing empirical data on the long-term predictive value of verbal and mental skills.
all_evidence.source_reference: Christopher Winship and Sanders D. Korenman, 'Economic Success and the Evolution of Schooling and Mental Ability' (1999)

all_evidence.description: An empirical study investigating how syntactic and lexical complexity specifically affects the comprehension of elementary science texts among students.
all_evidence.source_reference: Diana J. Arya, Elfrieda H. Hiebert, and P. David Pearson, 'The Effects of Syntactic and Lexical Complexity on the Comprehension of Elementary Science Texts' (2011)

all_evidence.description: A critical consumer guide and meta-analysis of various reading comprehension assessments used for adolescents, evaluating their technical reliability and construct validity.
all_evidence.source_reference: Leila Morsy, Michael Kieffer, and Catherine Snow, 'Measure for Measure: A Critical Consumers’ Guide to Reading Comprehension Assessments for Adolescents' (2010)

all_evidence.description: A foundational research report from the Educational Testing Service defining the theoretical framework for the validity of test interpretation and use.
all_evidence.source_reference: Samuel Messick, 'Validity of Test Interpretation and Use' (1990)

all_evidence.description: Statistical reporting on the growth of the 'opt-out' movement in New York State as a reaction against Common Core testing.
all_evidence.source_reference: Elizabeth A. Harris and Ford Fessenden, 'Opt Out Becomes Anti-Test Rallying Cry in New York State,' New York Times, May 20, 2015

all_evidence.description: The landmark 1983 report marking the start of the modern national education reform movement.
A study showing that charter school results are highly uneven and not significantly better than regular schools overall.
all_evidence.source_reference: Philip Gleason et al., The Impact of Charter Schools: Final Report (2010)

all_evidence.description: A report finding that education schools fail to teach teachers how to instruct reading effectively.
all_evidence.source_reference: Kate Walsh, Deborah Glaser, and Danielle Dunne Wilcox, What Education Schools Aren’t Teaching About Reading (2006)

all_evidence.description: The implementation of the Core Knowledge curriculum at Three Oaks School led to improved teacher morale and student test outcomes.
Comparison of effectiveness between teachers in content-incoherent US schools and content-cumulative Japanese schools.
Comparison between Japanese teachers who work within a coherent curriculum and American teachers who do not.
The failure of computers to accurately translate language despite decades of investment.
all_evidence.source_reference: Bar-Hillel (1960)

all_evidence.description: Research summaries showing that curriculum choice can be more effective than teacher quality.
all_evidence.source_reference: Chingos and Whitehurst, 'Choosing Blindly' (2012)

all_evidence.description: SAT scores of undergraduate education majors ranking 25th out of 29 majors.
all_evidence.source_reference: Governing magazine (June 2013)

all_evidence.description: The mathematical formula used for Value-Added Measures (VAM) in teacher evaluation.
all_evidence.source_reference: Hanushek and Rivkin (2010)

all_evidence.description: The Value-Added Measure (VAM) formula used to isolate teacher fixed effects from family and peer factors.
all_evidence.source_reference: Hanushek and Rivkin (2010)
all_evidence.source_passage: τj is a teacher fixed effect that provides a measure of teacher value added for teacher j.

all_evidence.description: Individual-level reading tests show an average intertest correlation of only about 70 percent.
all_evidence.source_reference: Morsy, Kieffer, and Snow (2010)
all_evidence.source_passage: Reading tests at best are 70 percent accurate at the individual level.

all_evidence.description: Teacher satisfaction in the US has declined from 62% in 2008 to 39% in 2012.
all_evidence.source_reference: The MetLife Survey of the American Teacher (2013)
all_evidence.source_passage: Teacher satisfaction has declined 23 percentage points since 2008, from 62% to 39% very satisfied

all_evidence.description: Anecdote of a Japanese fourth-grade math teacher using a specific analogy to help a struggling student, illustrating collaborative planning.
all_evidence.source_reference: Harold Stevenson (personal communication)
all_evidence.source_passage: One especially vivid story about collaboration in the Japanese elementary school was told to me directly by the late Professor Harold Stevenson... He had observed the event in a fourth-grade math class.

all_evidence.description: A report on faculty termination and disciplinary issues within university settings regarding the limits of tenure.
all_evidence.source_reference: Donna R. Euben, Faculty Termination & Disciplinary Issues, American Association of University Professors, 2004
all_evidence.source_passage: But even in universities there is no tenure protection for “failure to meet a specified norm of performance or productivity”

all_evidence.description: Research proposing Latent Semantic Analysis (LSA) as a computational theory for how humans acquire and represent knowledge from context, though still struggling with the 'unstated context' problem in machine translation.
all_evidence.source_reference: T. K. Landauer and S. T. Dumais, 'A Solution to Plato’s Problem: The Latent Semantic Analysis Theory of the Acquisition, Induction, and Representation of Knowledge,' Psychological Review 104 (1997): 211–240.

all_evidence.description: A policy paper arguing that curriculum choice is a neglected but highly cost-effective lever for improving student achievement compared to other popular reforms.
all_evidence.source_reference: Grover J. 'Russ' Whitehurst, 'Don’t Forget Curriculum,' Brookings.edu, October 2009

all_evidence.description: The Head Start Impact Study: Final Report (2010), showing academic benefits disappear after two years of primary school.
Reports from Georgia and Oklahoma showing null results for preschool long-term outcomes.
The French natural experiment (1987-2012) where preschools narrowed the gap until the 1990 Jospin law changed elementary schooling.
NAEP 2012 Trends in Academic Progress, showing verbal score gains at age 13 disappear by age 17.
Research by Thomas G. Sticht showing reading/writing abilities lag listening/speaking until grade 7.
Latent change score modeling studies on vocabulary growth (Quinn et al., 2015).
Project Follow Through, a massive longitudinal study comparing early literacy methods, which found Direct Instruction superior to developmental and natural-growth models.
all_evidence.source_reference: Project Follow Through (1970s and 1980s)

all_evidence.description: A follow-up study of Direct Instruction students in grades 5 and 6 showing that early gains in reading comprehension disappeared, resulting in a 'null effect' despite better decoding skills.
all_evidence.source_reference: Becker and Gersten (1982)

all_evidence.description: Evaluation of model programs under the Investing in Innovation program, showing Success for All schools did not outperform control groups on reading fluency or comprehension tests when scaled up.
all_evidence.source_reference: Janet Quint et al., Scaling Up the Success for All Model of School Reform (2015)

all_evidence.description: Research by Anderson, Miller, Stanovich, Guthrie et al. showing contextualized experiences are best for vocabulary gain.
all_evidence.source_reference: Richard Anderson, George A. Miller, Keith Stanovich, John Guthrie, et al.
all_evidence.source_passage: The best route to vocabulary gain is firmly established by the work of Richard Anderson, George A. Miller, Keith Stanovich, John Guthrie, and others...

all_evidence.description: Project Follow Through findings on the relative effectiveness of communal vs naturalistic preschools.
all_evidence.source_reference: Project Follow Through
all_evidence.source_passage: There is far less evidence that an individualized, go-at-your-own-pace naturalistic preschool can help disadvantaged children nearly as well. Project Follow Through established that.

all_evidence.description: Journalistic observation of Reggio Emilia's middle-class student body and lack of formal instruction.
all_evidence.source_reference: Alessandra Stanley, New York Times, 2001
all_evidence.source_passage: Stanley observed that Reggio Emilia has declined to study the relative effectiveness of its methods.

all_evidence.description: Diane Ravitch's historical analysis of the removal of history and literature from K-3 curriculum.
all_evidence.source_reference: Diane Ravitch, 'Tot Sociology', 1987
all_evidence.source_passage: In 1982 I began to research the condition of history instruction in the public schools. The more closely I examined the social studies curriculum, the more my attention was drawn to the curious nature of the early grades, which is virtually content-free.

all_evidence.description: Historical account of the rise of progressive education and the 'tot sociology' curriculum in the 1920s-1930s.
all_evidence.source_reference: Diane Ravitch, 'Tot Sociology,' 1987

all_evidence.description: A French national survey of 20,000 students (who were sixth graders in 1980) comparing preschool attendance to school failure rates.
all_evidence.source_reference: Duthoit, 'L’Enfant et l’ecole', 1988

all_evidence.description: Author's personal observation of a two-year-old in a low-income French 'banlieue' preschool demonstrating sophisticated social training and neatness.
all_evidence.source_reference: E.D. Hirsch, personal research in late 1980s

all_evidence.description: YouTube videos of full days in French écoles maternelles showing universal participation and engagement regardless of race or social class.
Parental guides from the French Ministry of Education (2010-2011) explicitly detailing the systematic sequence for vocabulary and knowledge acquisition.

John Guthrie's research on Concept-Oriented Reading Instruction (CORI), demonstrating that students in classrooms focused on sustained complex knowledge domains (like ecology or solar systems) outperform students in strategy-instruction classrooms.
all_evidence.source_reference: John Guthrie et al. (2004)

all_evidence.description: Longitudinal research of 2,000 French children from early years to age ten, comparing outcomes based on preschool start ages (two, three, or four).
all_evidence.source_reference: Jarousse, Mingat, and Richard, 'La Scolarisation Maternelle à Deux Ans' (1992)
all_evidence.source_passage: The longitudinal research traced some two thousand children from their earliest years up to age ten, comparing age-ten outcomes of students who started preschool at different ages: four, three, or two.

all_evidence.description: Figure 3.1 showing the gap-closing effects of preschool start age on fifth-grade reading and math scores in France in the 1980s.
all_evidence.source_reference: NA (Figure 3.1 in text)
all_evidence.source_passage: Note how in the 1980s the gap between the top and bottom group has been cut almost in half by starting early.

all_evidence.description: A longitudinal study of 2,000 children tracing them from preschool to age 10, showing that early starts (age 2) significantly closed or reversed achievement gaps in a knowledge-based system.
all_evidence.source_reference: Jarousse, Mingat, and Richard, 'La Scolarisation Maternelle à Deux Ans' (1992)
all_evidence.source_passage: traced some two thousand children from their earliest years up to age ten... results showed that if a disadvantaged child started at age two, that same child by age ten will be ahead of a middle-class child

all_evidence.description: A natural experiment created by the 1989 French educational reform (loi Jospin), which changed primary school instruction while leaving preschools intact, resulting in a shift from equity to fadeout.
all_evidence.source_reference: Hirsch's analysis of French educational history (1980-2007)
all_evidence.source_passage: It is an unintended twenty-seven-year longitudinal study with millions of young subjects in one group in France from 1980 to 1987, and millions in the experimental group from 1987 to 2007.

all_evidence.description: Modern French research characterizing current preschool effects as 'meager' following the shift to naturalistic primary education.
all_evidence.source_reference: Linda Ben Ali, 'La Scolarisation à Deux Ans' (2012)
all_evidence.source_passage: Researchers now pronounce the preschool effect to be mince—'meager.'

all_evidence.description: Scientific research on the mechanics of word acquisition through context and repetition.
all_evidence.source_reference: Nagy and Scott (2000); Miller (1999); Miller and Gildea (1987)
all_evidence.source_passage: Vocabulary gain is a very slow, incremental process requiring multiple exposures to a word in multiple contexts.

all_evidence.description: The Coleman Report (1966) and subsequent research on Catholic schools identifying 'shining exceptions' to the typical link between socioeconomic status and academic outcomes.
all_evidence.source_reference: Coleman Report (1966)

all_evidence.description: Studies on word predictability and reading time showing that 'cloze probability' (the likelihood of a word appearing in context) has a logarithmic effect on the speed of word recognition.
all_evidence.source_reference: Staub et al. (2015); Smith and Levy (2013)

all_evidence.description: Empirical analysis showing that different reading comprehension tests vary significantly in their dependence on decoding skills versus oral comprehension/knowledge.
all_evidence.source_reference: Keenan, Betjemann, and Olson (2008)

all_evidence.description: A cross-national analysis of early childhood programs and their outcomes across different nations.
all_evidence.source_reference: Sarane Spence Boocock (1995)

all_evidence.description: Research on partial word knowledge growth through reading, demonstrating the semantic-syntactic mechanics of how children gradually acquire word meanings.
all_evidence.source_reference: Wagovich, Hill, and Petroski (2015)

all_evidence.description: A study showing a 47% reduction in class time for subjects beyond math and reading during the NCLB era.
all_evidence.source_reference: Jennifer McMurrer, Choices, Changes, and Challenges (2007)
all_evidence.source_passage: In one study, the researcher noted that there had been a 47 percent reduction in class time devoted to subjects beyond math and reading.

all_evidence.description: Article documenting the disappearance of art, music, and PE due to high-stakes testing regimes.
all_evidence.source_reference: Tim Walker, 'The Testing Obsession and the Disappearing Curriculum,' NEA Today (2014)
all_evidence.source_passage: The law is uniformly blamed for stripping curriculum opportunities, including art, music, physical education and more...

all_evidence.description: Historical documentation of the failure of child-centered reforms and their consolidation in the mid-20th century.
all_evidence.source_reference: Diane Ravitch, Left Back: A Century of Failed School Reforms (2000)
all_evidence.source_passage: These curriculum principles were introduced in the 1930s, and consolidated throughout the land by the 1950s.

all_evidence.description: Piaget's observational study of his own children leading to the identification of four stages of mental development.
all_evidence.source_reference: Jean Piaget (1896–1980)
all_evidence.source_passage: He detected four stages in their mental development: the “sensorimotor stage” from infancy to age two, the “preoperational stage” from ages two to seven, the “concrete operational stage” from seven to eleven, and the “formal operational stage” in adolescence and maturity.

all_evidence.description: Anthropological research showing that adults in some cultures prioritize understanding human motivations and power over the logic of formal operations.
all_evidence.source_reference: Maynard and Greenfield, 'Implicit Cognitive Development in Cultural Tools and Children' (2003)
all_evidence.source_passage: “Who knows?” say highly able adults in some cultures, where the mark of maturity is not the manipulation of formal operations but the understanding of human motivations, personalities, and power relationships.

all_evidence.description: A study showing a two-year delay in children reaching developmental stages in rural Iranian villages compared to cities.
all_evidence.source_reference: Jean Piaget, 'Cognitive Development in Children' (1964)
all_evidence.source_passage: In Iran, furthermore, in the city of Teheran, they found approximately the same ages as we found in Geneva, but there is a systematic delay of two years in the children in the country.

all_evidence.description: A comparison between children in Montreal and Martinique using the same curriculum, showing a four-year developmental delay in Martinique.
all_evidence.source_reference: Monique Laurendeau and Father Adrien Pinard, cited in Piaget (1964)
all_evidence.source_passage: Canadian psychologists who redid our experiments... found a delay of four years in all the experiments and this in spite of the fact that the children in Martinique go to a school set up according to the French system.

all_evidence.description: World Health Organization data confirming no protein or vitamin deficiencies in Martinique, ruling out malnutrition as a cause for developmental delays.
all_evidence.source_reference: Pan American Health Organization (PAHO), 2012
all_evidence.source_passage: The World Health Organization does not report child malnutrition in Martinique: “There have been no reports of disorders associated with protein or vitamin deficiencies.”

all_evidence.description: Comparison of children in Martinique and Montreal showing that schooling/home environment, not maturation, explains the onset of the concrete operational stage.
Jerome Bruner's hypothesis that any subject can be taught in an intellectually honest form to any child at any stage.
all_evidence.source_reference: Jerome Bruner, The Process of Education (1960)

all_evidence.description: Research showing that universal mental development is a cultural process as much as a natural one.
all_evidence.source_reference: Patricia M. Greenfield et al. (2003)

all_evidence.description: Public debate regarding the teaching of Mesopotamia to first graders in New York, used to illustrate how DAP is used to delay content.
all_evidence.source_reference: Syracuse Post-Standard (2013)

all_evidence.description: Evidence that the claim of 'harm' from developmentally inappropriate topics is a myth exploded by psychologists.
all_evidence.source_reference: Judith J. Carta et al. (1991)

all_evidence.description: A meta-analysis showing that theories of multiple intelligences and learning styles lack validating empirical support and concordance with neuroscience.
all_evidence.source_reference: Lynn Waterhouse, 'Inadequate Evidence for Multiple Intelligences Mozart Effect, and Emotional Intelligence Theories,' Educational Psychologist 41, no. 4 (2006): 247–255.
all_evidence.source_passage: One meta-analysis concludes as follows: “In the absence of adequate validating empirical support, and in the absence of concord with neuroscience findings, [such theories] should not be applied in education.”

all_evidence.description: Historical example of a 1917 language arts series that explicitly defined its purpose as creating a common stock of knowledge for socialization and unity.
all_evidence.source_reference: Franklin T. Baker and Ashley H. Thorndike, 'Introduction,' Everyday Classics (New York: Macmillan, 1917).
all_evidence.source_passage: A best-selling language arts series stated: “We have chosen what is common, established, almost proverbial... what, in brief, every child in the land ought to know, because it is good, and because other people know it.”

all_evidence.description: Theoretical critique by Antonio Gramsci regarding the flaws in child-centered 'spontaneous development.'
all_evidence.source_reference: Antonio Gramsci, Notebook 1, 1929, Quaderni del Carcere, vol. 1.
all_evidence.source_passage: Antonio Gramsci so wittily observed when he said that the new education conceived “the child’s brain as a ball of string that the teacher should help to unwind.”

all_evidence.description: George A. Miller's 1983 review of multiple intelligences in the New York Times Review of Books, stating the theory is underevidenced.
all_evidence.source_reference: George A. Miller, 'Varieties of Intelligence', 1983

all_evidence.description: Carol Dweck's research on 'mindset' showing that belief in malleable intelligence leads to better results than belief in fixed intelligence.
all_evidence.source_reference: Carol Dweck, Mindset (2007)

all_evidence.description: The 2012 PISA study analysis showing that nations with common subject-matter curricula best narrowed achievement gaps.
all_evidence.source_reference: OECD, Low-Performing Students (2016)

all_evidence.description: A 'report from the field' describing how differentiated instruction results in fragmented lessons and 'specious' activities.
all_evidence.source_reference: Mike Schmoker, 'When Pedagogic Fads Trump Priorities', Education Week, 2010

all_evidence.description: Research showing prior domain knowledge is a more significant predictor of learning new content than general aptitude.
all_evidence.source_reference: Beier & Ackerman (2005); Hall & Edmondson (1992)

all_evidence.description: Evidence that whole-class, concept-oriented instruction improves reading engagement and comprehension.
all_evidence.source_reference: John T. Guthrie et al. (2004)
all_evidence.source_passage: Increasing Reading Comprehension and Engagement Through Concept-Oriented Reading Instruction [CORI]

all_evidence.description: Demonstration that oral comprehension levels (auding) are the primary predictor of later reading comprehension.
all_evidence.source_reference: Thomas G. Sticht (1975)
all_evidence.source_passage: Auding and Reading: A Developmental Model

all_evidence.description: Critique of 'instructional level theory' (leveled books) showing that students can learn from texts at various difficulty levels and that the theory lacks evidence.
all_evidence.source_reference: Timothy Shanahan (2011)
all_evidence.source_passage: basically we have put way too much confidence in an unproven theory.

all_evidence.description: Data showing that regular school libraries have declined while classroom collections have increased.
all_evidence.source_reference: American Library Association (2010)
all_evidence.source_passage: The State of America’s Libraries

all_evidence.description: Analysis of popular reading programs (e.g., Houghton Mifflin) showing they consist of heterogeneous, non-domain-based content.
all_evidence.source_reference: E.D. Hirsch (The Knowledge Deficit)
all_evidence.source_passage: the contents are thoroughly heterogeneous, not domain based.

all_evidence.description: The Lexile formula (old version) which uses logs of mean sentence length and word rarity to calculate difficulty.
all_evidence.source_reference: Endnote 36

all_evidence.description: Research by Tim Shanahan concluding that instructional level theory and leveled books lack scientific evidence and efficacy.
all_evidence.source_reference: Shanahan, 'Rejecting Instructional Level Theory' (2011)

all_evidence.description: General reference to successful practices in 'good primary schools over the world' where students learn new things through vigorous discussion.
all_evidence.source_reference: Not specified beyond 'good primary schools over the world'

all_evidence.description: A survey of mission statements from various US school districts (Milwaukee, Oakland, Tucson, etc.) showing a uniform focus on skills over content.
all_evidence.source_reference: Hirsch's random sample of district mission statements

all_evidence.description: The inaugural statement of the Santa Barbara Unified School District superintendent emphasizing critical thinking over content memorization in preparation for Common Core.
all_evidence.source_reference: Giana Magnoli, 'Superintendent Says Culture Will Change in Santa Barbara School District,' Santa Barbara Noozhawk, 2013

all_evidence.description: Popular reform literature that argues for the prioritization of problem-solving and teamwork for the global economy.
all_evidence.source_reference: Tony Wagner, The Global Achievement Gap

all_evidence.description: Tony Wagner's book 'The Global Achievement Gap' which advocates for 21st-century skills like problem-solving and teamwork.
NAEP verbal scores for seventeen-year-olds used as a proxy for the achievement of communication skills.
The idiom 'You beat me to the punch' as an example of unspoken shared connotations in the public sphere.
Cognitive science research dating back to the 1940s regarding domain specificity.
George A. Miller's work on the limitations of 'looking things up.'
all_evidence.source_reference: George A. Miller (2000)

all_evidence.description: Research by Levy and Steel (2015) regarding the functionality and distractions of electronic dictionaries.
all_evidence.source_reference: Levy and Steel (2015)

all_evidence.description: Research on the limitations and distractions of electronic language dictionaries for learners.
all_evidence.source_reference: Levy and Steel, 'Language Learner Perspectives on the Functionality and Use of Electronic Language Dictionaries' (2015)
all_evidence.source_passage: I checked the literature and found that indeed, still in 2015, both researchers and students themselves are “keenly aware of the limitations” and “distractions” of using online dictionaries

all_evidence.description: Studies showing children's frustration and loss of context when using paper dictionaries to find word meanings.
all_evidence.source_reference: George A. Miller and Patricia M. Gildea, 'How Children Learn Words' (1987)
all_evidence.source_passage: In the time it took children to find a dictionary word and construe its meanings, they usually forgot the original context and never found their way back.

all_evidence.description: Research on the cognitive distractions and 'delinkification' caused by hyperlinks in digital reading.
all_evidence.source_reference: Nicholas Carr, 'Experiments in Delinkification' (2010)
all_evidence.source_passage: Those who have done research in the digital age have reported similar frustration with the distractions of hy- perlinks.

all_evidence.description: Cognitive science research demonstrating that novices have difficulty learning from reference materials due to working memory constraints.
all_evidence.source_reference: Jill Larkin et al., 'Expert and Novice Performance in Solving Physics Problems' (1980)
all_evidence.source_passage: It’s extremely hard for a novice to learn very much in a reason- able time by looking things up.

all_evidence.description: The application of the 'Matthew Effect' to reading and literacy acquisition.
all_evidence.source_reference: Keith E. Stanovich, 'Matthew Effects in Reading' (1986)
all_evidence.source_passage: Education is bootstrapping, gov- erned by the “Matthew Effect,” that unfair paradox in the Book of Matthew

all_evidence.description: The Duncker radiation experiment showed that people cannot solve the same structural problem when it is moved to a different domain.
Adriaan de Groot's chess experiment showed that masters' superior memory was only for valid game positions, not random placements, indicating domain-specific knowledge.
A 2011 National Academy of Sciences report found little evidence for critical thinking as a general skill.
Psychologist Nathan R. Kuncel argues research does not support critical thinking as a universal skill independent of domain knowledge.
Third-grade reading score increases attributed to improved decoding instruction.
all_evidence.source_reference: N/A

all_evidence.description: The National Reading Panel of 2000 recommendations which included strategy instruction.
all_evidence.source_reference: National Reading Panel (2000)

all_evidence.description: The 'Situation Model' theory describing how readers construct meaning using prior knowledge.
all_evidence.source_reference: Teun van Dijk and Walter Kintsch, Strategies of Discourse Comprehension (1983)

all_evidence.description: Study showing 'poor readers' with baseball knowledge outperformed 'good readers' without it when reading about baseball.
all_evidence.source_reference: Donna R. Recht and Lauren Leslie (1988)

all_evidence.description: Study demonstrating that topic-relevant soccer knowledge determines comprehension performance more than IQ.
all_evidence.source_reference: Wolfgang Schneider et al. (1989)

all_evidence.description: A study showing that low-IQ students with high soccer knowledge outperformed high-IQ students with low soccer knowledge on reading tasks related to soccer.
all_evidence.source_reference: Schneider et al. (1999/1989)
all_evidence.source_passage: The low-IQ students who knew a lot about soccer outperformed the high-IQ students who had little soccer knowledge. Moreover, when both low- and high-IQ students were soccer experts, the low-IQ students performed just as well as those with high-IQs!

all_evidence.description: Research showing that text complexity (syntactic and lexical) had little effect on comprehension when third graders were familiar with the topic (e.g., toothpaste, jelly beans) but significantly hindered them when the topic was unfamiliar (e.g., tree frogs, soil).
all_evidence.source_reference: Arya, Hiebert, and Pearson (2011)
all_evidence.source_passage: With topics unfamiliar to third graders, like tree frogs and soil, text complexity slowed reading and debased understanding. But when the topics were familiar, as with toothpaste and jelly beans, the complexity of the text had little effect on accuracy or speed of comprehension.

all_evidence.description: The theoretical framework explaining that meaning is constructed from text-provided info and reader-contributed info.
all_evidence.source_reference: Teun A. van Dijk and Walter Kintsch (1983)
all_evidence.source_passage: The Kintsch/van Dijk insight into “situation models” explains why this is so.

all_evidence.description: A study identifying that the domain specificity of skills means individuals perform formal operations significantly better when they are tied to concrete experience.
all_evidence.source_reference: Paul Sheldon Davies, James H. Fetzer, and Thomas R. Foster, 'Logical Reasoning and Domain Specificity,' Biology and Philosophy 10, no. 1 (1995): 1–37.

all_evidence.description: A historical example of early 20th-century progressive education theory that used biological comparisons of internal organs to argue that children's 'mental organs' require different nutrition (content) than adults.
all_evidence.source_reference: Nathan Oppenheim, The Development of the Child (1898)

all_evidence.description: Research demonstrating that the time required for experts to memorize new information (five seconds versus sixty) is heavily dependent on their prior domain-specific knowledge.
all_evidence.source_reference: Fernand Gobet and Herbert A. Simon, 'Five Seconds or Sixty? Presentation Time in Expert Memory,' Cognitive Science 24 (2000): 651–682.

all_evidence.description: An empirical study investigating how existing subject-matter knowledge directly influences both the interest levels and the recall ability of students.
all_evidence.source_reference: Patricia A. Alexander, Jonna M. Kulikowich, and Sharon K. Schulze, 'How Subject-Matter Knowledge Affects Recall and Interest,' American Educational Research Journal 31 (1994): 313–337.

all_evidence.description: Historical media evidence from 1936 documenting that thousands of American schools had already shifted focus toward individuality and critical thinking over specific content.
all_evidence.source_reference: March of Time newsreel (1936), cited in Hirsch endnote 41

all_evidence.description: Thorndike's research found that learning Latin did not provide general mental training, but rather only improved the specific ability to read Latin, supporting the concept of domain-specific learning.
all_evidence.source_reference: Edward Thorndike, cited in endnote 42

all_evidence.description: Experimental research by Gick and Holyoak on analogical problem solving, demonstrating the difficulties of transferring structural problem-solving methods between different contexts.
all_evidence.source_reference: M. L. Gick and K. J. Holyoak, 'Analogical Problem Solving' (1980)

all_evidence.description: Neuroscience research identifying domain-specificity within elemental brain processors during visual perspective-taking tasks.
all_evidence.source_reference: Matthias Schurz et al., 'Clarifying the Role of Theory of Mind Areas During Visual Perspective Taking' (2015)

all_evidence.description: Research by Helfenstein and Saariluoma exploring the role of mental contents in the transfer of skills between different domains.
all_evidence.source_reference: Sacha Helfenstein and Pertti Saariluoma, 'Mental Contents in Transfer' (2006)

all_evidence.description: A study demonstrating that the effects of syntactic and lexical complexity on reading comprehension are secondary to the reader's familiarity with the subject matter in elementary science texts.
all_evidence.source_reference: Diana J. Arya, Elfrieda H. Hiebert, and P. David Pearson, 'The Effects of Syntactic and Lexical Complexity on the Comprehension of Elementary Science Texts,' International Electronic Journal of Elementary Education 4, no. 1 (2011): 107–125.

all_evidence.description: Analysis suggesting that word rarity and sentence length (text complexity) can effectively increase as students become more familiar with a topic within a coherent lesson unit.
all_evidence.source_reference: E.D. Hirsch, Why Knowledge Matters, Endnote 65

all_evidence.description: Report showing the black-white achievement gap narrowed until 1988 then stalled.
all_evidence.source_reference: Educational Testing Service (ETS), The Black-White Achievement Gap: When Progress Stopped, 2010

all_evidence.description: Massive study concluding that 2/3 of achievement gap causes are not explained by social factors like economic status or school integration.
all_evidence.source_reference: David Grissmer et al., 1994

all_evidence.description: Data showing that only 30 percent of parents in inferior schools desire busing/transport to other schools.
all_evidence.source_reference: Christine H. Rossell et al., School Desegregation in the 21st Century, 2002

all_evidence.description: Memoir cited as evidence of a brilliant student overcoming poverty through schooling.
all_evidence.source_reference: Sonia Sotomayor, My Beloved World, 2013

all_evidence.description: Reference to the general nationwide test score decline in the US between 1960 and 1980.
all_evidence.source_reference: Hirsch, endnote 2

all_evidence.description: Christine H. Rossell's study on school desegregation found that 70% of parents prefer neighborhood schools over busing.
all_evidence.source_reference: Christine H. Rossell et al., School Desegregation in the 21st Century (2002)
all_evidence.source_passage: At present, only some 30 percent of parents in inferior schools favor transporting their child to another school.

all_evidence.description: PISA 2012 results volume focusing on excellence through equity and narrowing gaps in top-performing nations.
all_evidence.source_reference: PISA 2012 Results: Excellence Through Equity Giving Every Student the Chance to Succeed, Volume II (2013)
all_evidence.source_passage: The 2012 PISA studies of fifteen-year-olds across the globe have devoted a volume to that subject.

all_evidence.description: The 1966 Coleman Report, Equality of Educational Opportunity, which sampled 650,000 students.
all_evidence.source_reference: James S. Coleman et al., Equality of Educational Opportunity (1966)
all_evidence.source_passage: It sampled some 650,000 students.

all_evidence.description: Borman and Dowling's 2010 reanalysis of Coleman data using new methodology to isolate school vs. family effects.
all_evidence.source_reference: Geoffrey Borman and Maritza Dowling, 'Schools and Inequality' (2010)
all_evidence.source_passage: Even after statistically taking into account students’ family background, a large proportion of the variation among true school means is related to differences explained by school characteristics.

all_evidence.description: Coleman's study comparing public and Catholic schools, showing the latter's success in closing achievement gaps.
all_evidence.source_reference: James S. Coleman, Thomas Hoffer, and Sally Kilgore, High School Achievement (1982)
all_evidence.source_passage: Catholic schools were doing an excellent job in reducing the impact of family and poverty on educational achievement.

all_evidence.description: Coleman's 1966 data showing that 20 percent of Southern Negro achievement is associated with their school compared to only 10 percent for white students.
all_evidence.source_reference: James S. Coleman et al., Equality of Educational Opportunity (1966)
all_evidence.source_passage: Thus, 20 percent of the achievement of Negroes in the South is associated with the particular schools they go to, whereas only 10 percent of the achievement of whites in the South is.

all_evidence.description: A study showing Catholic schools reduced the impact of family background and poverty on educational achievement through a coherent curriculum.
all_evidence.source_reference: James S. Coleman, Thomas Hoffer, and Sally Kilgore, High School Achievement (1982)
all_evidence.source_passage: He pursued further studies showing that Catholic schools were doing an excellent job in reducing the impact of family and poverty on educational achievement.

all_evidence.description: NAEP data showing the black-white equity gap in reading increases between age thirteen and age seventeen.
all_evidence.source_reference: National Assessment of Educational Progress (NAEP)
all_evidence.source_passage: And indeed, the National Assessment of Educational Progress (NAEP) shows that the black-white equity gap in reading consistently increases between age thirteen and age seventeen.

all_evidence.description: Systematic evaluation of domain immersion (Concept-Oriented Reading Instruction) showing higher reading comprehension and motivation.
all_evidence.source_reference: John Guthrie et al., 'Increasing Reading Comprehension and Engagement Through Concept-Oriented Reading Instruction' (2004)
