The link between language mastery and knowledge mastery implies that lack of knowledge leads to communicative incompetence and poverty.
The slow rate of change in central shared knowledge makes it a stable, necessary target for escaping poverty.
The claim that mental stages are influenced by education is historically supported by Piaget's own later theoretical reversals.
The fact that education can shift development by four years proves that content, not just biology, drives mental stages.
The slow growth of expertise is explained by the fundamental memory requirement for long practice and domain-specific knowledge.
The inability of computers to bypass domain knowledge reinforces the claim that such knowledge is an essential requirement for skill consolidation.
The preference for basic science over classroom research is used to discredit the 'romantic ideas' that currently dominate American education.

The principle of intellectual triangulation provides the methodological basis for trusting the disconfirmation of American schooling ideas because that disconfirmation comes from multiple independent fields.
The author prioritizes basic scientific research over school-level research to justify the claim that the foundational ideas of schooling have been scientifically disconfirmed.
Developmental psychology's findings against natural development are one of the independent perspectives that disconfirm current schooling ideas.
Psycholinguistic research's findings on domain-specificity are another independent perspective disconfirming the 'all-purpose skills' idea.
If there are no shortcuts to competence, a comprehensive, knowledge-specific curriculum becomes the only viable path to imparting that competence.
The necessity of a knowledge-specific curriculum follows from the scientific failure of its alternatives (naturalism and skill-centrism).
The normative necessity of the curriculum justifies the pragmatic prediction that its successful implementation in one district will force a national shift.
The claim that natural development was 'new' in the 19th century depends on the observation that the terminology of 'growth' and 'development' was absent in the prior century.
Horace Mann's mid-century shift serves as the chronological marker for when these ideas began to permeate American educational thought.
The definition of the classical aim as 'correcting nature' is the necessary baseline from which the romantic aim deviates.
The interpretation of school architecture as a symbol of community importance relies on the theoretical premise that ideas are reflected in physical structures.
The interpretation of post-war architecture as 'child-centered' relies on the theoretical premise that ideas (specifically pantheistic/romantic ones) are reflected in architecture.
The specific pedagogical practice of hands-on learning instantiates the broader romantic aim of following nature's lead.
The claim that natural growth is 'morally wrong' and 'factually incorrect' rests on the prior establishment that it is factually incorrect.
If socialization is more natural than internal unfolding, then theories favoring unfolding are factually incorrect.
The argument that socialization is more natural relies on the empirical premise that growth requires external culture.
The moral wrongness of natural development is supported by its 'dangerous social underside'—the assumption that poor children lack development.
The intensification of social inequality serves as evidence for the moral wrongness of the theory.
Historical human practice (induction into the tribe) is used to show that socialization is the 'natural' state of human education.
The historical optimism of the revolutions provided the cultural context for viewing Providence as manifest in individual development.
Wordsworth's influence on Dewey explains the persistence of Romantic ideals in 20th-century progressive education.
Hegel's secularization of progress provides the specific concept ('aufheben') that the author critiques as being logically groundless.
Hegel's philosophy of 'necessarily' progressive development provided the logical framework for American progressive thinkers.
Froebel's status as the most influential European author on early education establishes his role as a chief intellectual influence.
Dewey's view of cognitive development as the reproduction of a universal mind is a direct application of his early Hegelian philosophy.
Froebel's theory that prescriptive education destroys the divine unfolding of the child provides the primary theological rationale for child-centeredness.
Dewey's identification of the child's development with the 'universal mind' reinforces the theological justification of child impulses.
The shift to an 'inner light' model of imagination is premised on the belief that child impulses are a manifestation of divine will.
The modern focus on imagination is identified as the historical 'husk' of the Romantic model that persists in modern practice.
The secular substitution of 'imagination' for 'God' explains how the structure of the theory survives the loss of its theological foundation.
The claim that the 'inner light' tradition persists as a secularized value requires the historical context of its transition from Noah Webster's instructive model to Romantic natural development.
C66's conclusion that the US must overcome religious certitudes assumes C53's finding that these certitudes (the 'inner light') are what currently dominate schooling.
If modern science does not support the Piagetian interpretations used in schools, the individualistic aspects of progressive education are scientifically unsound.
The resistance to phonics and facts (C60) is explained by the persisting sentiment that children are innocent beings coming from God (C63).
The claim that beliefs are impervious to evidence (C61) follows from the identification of these beliefs as 'Froebelian fundamentalism' (C60).
The dismissal of Core Knowledge data by Chancellor Fariña serves as an example of metaphysical belief being impervious to empirical evidence.
Moving toward socialization and fairness (C70) requires acknowledging that current individualistic principles are harmful to social justice (C69).
The empirical fact that growth requires external training into a specific culture justifies the shift toward communal socialization principles.
The overall decline in performance provides the context for evaluating how that decline was distributed across demographic groups.
C6's claim of a 'four times greater' gap is the direct calculation derived from the data in C22 (0.4 SD for reading vs 0.1 SD for math).
The empirical observation that the equity gap widened drastically more in reading than math suggests that reading equity is more sensitive to the quality of schooling.
The fact that math is a 'school subject' explaining uniform decline helps isolate why reading's non-uniform decline is an equity crisis.
Since reading depends on vocabulary and knowledge (partially acquired at home), a failure in school instruction hits those with less home-based knowledge much harder.
If poor schooling (skills-based) destroys reading equity, then the inverse (knowledge-based) is posited as the solution for equity.
By eliminating the 'skills-based' alternative as ineffective for vocabulary growth, the author strengthens the case for a knowledge-based curriculum.
The effectiveness of knowledge-based curricula for equity creates a normative obligation for schools to adopt them to fulfill social goals.
The empirical doubling of writing errors provides the statistical basis for claiming a decline in writing skills.
This specific instance of writing decline in France supports the broader conclusion that France's shift to skill-based principles caused a systemic educational decline.
The decline in writing performance is a component of the overall 0.4 standard deviation drop in literacy scores attributed to the new curriculum.
The rejection of American reforms (C2) is a response to the specific reforms implemented between 1945 and 1952 (C1).
The reasons for the return to centralization (C3) explain the public rejection of the American-led reforms (C2).
The specific Grade 3 mandate for teaching the sun's path is an example of the intentional structure and content richness claimed in C8.
The Grade 4 requirement for longitudinal observation serves as evidence of the coherent structure of the Japanese curriculum.
The identification of specific rock types in Grade 6 demonstrates the curriculum's commitment to building specific content knowledge across grades.
The view of education as a process of socialization (C9) provides the theoretical basis for the claim that schools have an elemental duty to teach values (C82).
The argument that civic virtues are non-religious and belong in state education (C75) supports the broader claim that curricula should not be value-neutral (C82).
The Japanese belief in the necessity of moral training after the failure of decentralization provides a historical precedent for the duty to teach values.
The claim that hiding values leads to suspicion (C10) supports the necessity of explicitly teaching values to avoid societal incivility (C82).
If teaching values is legally permissible under the separation of church and state, it removes a primary barrier to establishing a publicly stated, value-rich curriculum.
The necessity of an 'open covenant' curriculum is predicated on the idea that failing to teach values has negative social consequences like rootlessness.
The claim that US standards are amateurish is logically supported by the identification of the 'developmental approach' as the specific cause of that sloppiness.
The lack of a multiyear cumulative plan is cited as a defining feature of the 'amateurish' and 'non-analytical' state of American science standards.
The Japanese curriculum's focus on investigative comparison of specific physical phenomena serves as a counter-model to the 'amateurish' American standards.
The specific details of battery configurations and motor rotation illustrate the domain-specific nature of physics knowledge.
The requirement to understand mass conservation during dissolution is an example of domain-specific cognitive development.
Teaching specific functions of running water (erosion, transportation, deposition) provides the domain-specific schema required for geological reasoning.
The limitation of seed nutrition study to starch demonstrates the 'selective' characteristic of successful national curricula.
Prohibiting human fertilization topics in Grade 5 suggests a curricular decision based on developmental focus rather than biological incapacity.
The specific physics content regarding electricity and energy transformations provides the 'scientific knowledge' that the syllabus aims to consolidate.
Detailed requirements for human anatomy and physiology serve as the 'concepts' through which scientific perspectives are developed.
Complex geological concepts like strata and volcanic activity are part of the specific knowledge base meant to be consolidated.
The methodological principle of enrichment through observation and experiments provides the practical mechanism for achieving the overarching goal of knowledge consolidation.
If the goal is to consolidate specific knowledge and concepts, then requiring students to use those specific terms in explanations is a logical pedagogical requirement.
Understanding the 'eat-or-be-eaten' world and water circulation provides the conceptual foundation for developing an attitude of environmental conservation.
Direct experience in nature and experiments are the means by which students connect scientific outcomes to everyday life.
C71 provides a concrete example of the 'topic-specific' and 'coherent' characteristics found in successful PISA nations like Japan.
By tying fossil study to a specific geological mechanism (running water), the curriculum treats science as domain-specific knowledge rather than a general inquiry skill.
The level of detail in the Japanese curriculum regarding specific organs and rocks demonstrates the 'hard, specific thought' the author argues is necessary for early education.

all_missing_steps.missing_step: Establishing that the 'knowledge possessed by successful citizens' is a stable, neutral, and universally agreed-upon body of information.
Establishing that the centralized, uniform egalitarianism of the French model is compatible with the decentralized, federated educational tradition of the United States.
Proof that the specific implementation of 'Americanized' ideas in France is functionally identical to the pedagogical practices in US classrooms between 1960 and 1980.
A mechanism explaining why a curriculum that harms everyone (elite and poor) necessarily harms the poor more severely than a uniform communal curriculum.
A single, local curriculum is the only or most effective mechanism for delivering the shared knowledge children deserve.
Theological or 'providential' views of child development logically necessitate a focus on content-independent skills that leads to test-prep focus.
Proof that the cultural and social conditions that allow uniform curricula to thrive in Finland/Japan are present or can be replicated in the United States.
Establishing that individualization has no non-cognitive benefits (such as motivation or emotional regulation) that might outweigh the lack of general-skill development.
A clear distinction between 'lower-order' skills (which might transfer) and 'higher-order' thinking (which may not).
Establishing that the 'drills' used in schools were a faithful and competent implementation of the general-skills theory.
Establishing that a 'well-defined, knowledge-based curriculum' is the only viable method to deliver the knowledge cognitive science requires.
The assumption that the factors predicting individual success (verbal scores) are the same factors that define the quality of an entire institutional system.
Demonstrating that parental demand is an effective or sufficient mechanism to override the professional risks and bureaucratic mandates teachers face.
Establishing that the vocabulary deficit at age seventeen is primarily caused by school curriculum rather than outside-of-school factors like the 'word gap' at home or media consumption.
A formal analysis of Common Core assessments proving they rely on the same 'skills-based' logic as previous NCLB state tests.
Schools lack the institutional autonomy or courage to ignore test formats in favor of long-term knowledge building.
Test makers are aware that their tests probe knowledge yet intentionally market them as skill-based to satisfy political or educational demands.
It is impossible for schools to teach all the varied 'general knowledge' that might appear on a standardized test.
The failure of students to improve under skills-based standards is evidence that the skills themselves do not exist or do not transfer.
A test based on a flawed theory of inference cannot serve as a useful proxy for reading ability, even if scores correlate with general intelligence or knowledge.
Implementing content-specific standards in reading will be as manageable and effective as it is in math, despite reading being an 'open' domain and math being 'closed.'
A shared, grade-by-grade curriculum is the only feasible way to ensure all students have equal access to the 'topic familiarity' required for fair testing.
Removing punitive threats will naturally lead teachers back to knowledge-rich instruction rather than other forms of non-productive activity.
Proof that no other non-curricular factors (such as socioeconomic status or school funding) are the primary reasons for the failure of structural reforms.
Demonstrating that charter schools actually utilized significantly different instructional theories than public schools, rather than just different management.
The cumulative effect of a shared curriculum must be shown to outweigh the statistical variance caused by differences in individual teacher talent.
Even if curriculum is the 'content' of learning, it must be shown that the delivery mechanism (the teacher) is not the primary bottleneck for that content's reception.
Judges must accept that statistical 'validity' (the test measures something) is not the same as 'consequential fairness' (the test measures what the teacher did).
A content-based curriculum can only be effectively implemented if funding is specifically diverted from other areas to pedagogical support.
The quality and consistency of American preschools must be comparable to French preschools for the 'curriculum-only' explanation to hold in the US.
Fixing the elementary curriculum is a more cost-effective or feasible policy lever than increasing preschool intensity or duration.
A communal-knowledge-based curriculum is the only or most effective type of curriculum to replace the current failing one.
The 'latent knowledge' missed by tests in early grades is specifically domain-specific knowledge rather than general cognitive development or linguistic exposure.
Perceptions of 'developmental appropriateness' are the primary reason alternative curricula were not adopted, rather than resource constraints or teacher training.
A communal, subject-centered curriculum is the unique or best way to provide 'systematically contextualized' experiences.
Non-curricular factors in France (e.g., teacher status, national healthcare, income equality) are not the primary drivers of the reduced failure rate.
Establishing that closing the vocabulary gap is sufficient to overcome other socio-economic barriers to equal opportunity.
A bridge explaining why a catch-up triggered at age two is sustained rather than 'fading out' as students encounter higher-order cognitive tasks in later grades.
The success of a centralized French curriculum is directly replicable in the highly decentralized United States educational system.
Middle-school fadeout in America is caused by the same specific curricular mechanisms as primary-school fadeout in post-1989 France.
Establishing that a single, unified national curriculum is the only way to implement 'guiding ideas' of knowledge, as opposed to diverse local knowledge-rich curricula.
Demonstrating that the 'guiding ideas' were the primary driver of dilution in the specific period of NCLB, rather than NCLB being the catalyst that allowed those ideas to flourish.
Proof that no other environmental factors (nutrition, stress, economic stability) account for the 4-year delay in Martinique besides the 'knowledge base.'
The transition from 'any topic can be taught' to 'age is irrelevant' ignores potential physiological constraints on working memory capacity that correlate with biological age.
A demonstration that the 'standard language' is the only or best vehicle for the 'shared knowledge' required for democracy.
Evidence that the 'fragmentation' observed in language arts is specifically caused by 'hyper-individualism' rather than other factors like lack of resources or testing pressures.
A communal curriculum is the only or most effective way to deliver high-quality oral comprehension training to all children.
Educational practices that work in high-performing PISA nations are culturally and logistically transferable to the decentralized US school system.
Establishing that a 'systematic approach to content' is the only way to provide the topic-specific knowledge identified as necessary in C76.
Demonstrating that 'advantaged' students are receiving coherent subject-matter learning elsewhere (e.g., at home), which creates the gap when schools provide fragmented curricula to everyone.
National reading scores for seventeen-year-olds are a comprehensive proxy for the diverse range of 'twenty-first-century skills' like teamwork and creativity.
Short-term performance degradation on unfamiliar tasks proves that no long-term structural improvement in thinking processes has occurred.
It must be established that NCLB's focus on general skills was the primary reason for the lack of comprehension improvement, rather than other factors like socioeconomic stressors or test anxiety.
That 'all-around reading skill' is a realistic or necessary goal for all students, as opposed to high proficiency in specific, relevant domains.
Establishing that a school system can practically provide a sufficient breadth of domain knowledge to cover the vast array of topics encountered in adult life.
Proving that a 'coherent curriculum' is the ONLY method, rather than one of several necessary components (such as teacher training or socio-economic support).
National curriculum is the primary or sole determinant of 'school quality' in the context of narrowing gaps.
Technical psychological principles in the classroom are more impactful than structural social reforms (like housing or income support) in achieving equity.
Evidence that school curriculum changed specifically during the period when progress on the achievement gap stalled.
Establishing that 'neighborhood sentiment' and parent preference for local schools can overcome the deep-seated economic and social factors that currently define 'bad' neighborhoods.
Proof that the 40% variance is primarily driven by curriculum coherence rather than teacher quality or school funding.
Establishing that the widening reading gap is caused specifically by the adoption of 'new education' rather than external economic factors or the end of desegregation.
Demonstrating that domain immersion is the *only* or *most* effective way to trigger implicit word learning compared to other content-rich approaches.
Frustration with instruction is the primary cause of dropping out, as opposed to economic necessity or social environment.
Whole-class delivery is more effective than small-group or individualized domain immersion for narrowing achievement gaps.
Standardized tests are the primary and definitive driver of what actually occurs in American classrooms.
The 'logical inferences' demanded by Common Core are structurally identical to the formal logic that fails in textual comprehension, rather than a different kind of text-based reasoning.
A demonstration that a 'nationwide' core is the ONLY way to achieve fairness, rather than a system of state-level curriculum-aligned tests.
Test makers lack the technical ability to design tests that isolate 'process' from 'content' even if they wanted to.

While close reading may reduce initial comprehension speed or recall, it is not established that these trade-offs are 'unsuitable' for early instruction if the goal is different from general meaning-seeking.
The author assumes that because inferences are contextual, PARCC's attempt to isolate text for 'clues' is a logical impossibility rather than just a very difficult task.
The use of content-indifferent tools like Lexiles necessarily results in a fragmented or content-poor curriculum in practice.
A 'communal curriculum' must be delivered through identical texts rather than through different texts covering the same knowledge domain.
Decreasing 'vacuous stories' and increasing 'informational texts' necessarily leads to a higher quality of classroom instruction.
The Romantic focus on imagination is inherently and exclusively individualistic, precluding communal knowledge building.
The public's preference for commonality is not merely nostalgic but is the only viable path for modern national operation.
There is a finite and identifiable 'standard' body of American knowledge that aligns perfectly with Standard American English.
Establishing that the artificial construction of a national culture is morally or socially superior to the preservation of existing 'tribal' or local cultures.
Proving that teaching the knowledge of the middle class to lower classes will result in the same economic payout, rather than simply moving the 'goalposts' of elite signaling.
The school system is the only institution capable of or responsible for providing this specific knowledge at the scale required for equality.
National content standards can be drafted and implemented without being captured by the very 'tribalism' or 'essentialism' the author critiques.
Proof that the 1989 'Loi Jospin' was actually implemented as intended in the majority of French classrooms.
Establishing that the sociological factors influencing learning in the US (decentralized) operate the same way as in France (historically centralized) despite similar population sizes.
A demonstration that student demographics in France remained sufficiently stable between 1989 and the subsequent decline to isolate policy as the sole variable.
The assumption that granting schools autonomy automatically results in them abandoning the national cultural heritage rather than simply teaching it through different methods.
Evidence that the school system's curriculum is the primary cause of these class-based disparities, rather than external socio-economic factors.
Verification that the upward mobility observed in the 1950s was a direct result of the curriculum rather than the post-war economic boom (The Trente Glorieuses).
Establishing that American educational decline was actually caused by naturalism/skills-centrism, rather than just coinciding with them.
The cultural and linguistic differences between France and the US do not invalidate the transferability of educational data.
The cultural and systemic factors that allow a communal curriculum to function in France must be present or replicable in the decentralized US system.
Establishing that the curriculum disintegration was driven by political ideology rather than administrative error or well-intentioned scientific misunderstanding.
Establishing that the US educational trajectory and current policy landscape are sufficiently similar to the Swedish 1994 reforms for the comparison to hold.
Proof that the decline in reading and arithmetic was caused specifically by the 'skills' focus rather than the 'market competition' or 'voucher' aspect of the reform.
Establishing that Germany did not implement other major structural reforms alongside the curriculum change that could explain the PISA improvement.
A demonstration that the decline in Swedish equity was specifically caused by the 'skills slogans' rather than the simultaneous introduction of market choice/vouchers.
That the historical 'common school' model of the American founding is still the most effective mechanism for achieving democratic stability in a modern, pluralistic context.
That differentiated curricula (tracking) are the primary or sole expression of 'providential individualism' in these specific countries.
The word-count gap identified by Hart-Risley consists specifically of the types of 'shared knowledge' required for democratic participation.
A common curriculum alone is sufficient to eliminate the demand for school choice, regardless of other cultural or political differences between the US and Finland/Japan.

The author assumes that 'adding in' new cultural figures can be done without displacing the 'traditional knowledge' necessary for the language of power, despite C19 suggesting Rosa Parks 'pushed out' John Jay.
Establishing that the 'stable nucleus' of knowledge found in language is the same knowledge required for social mobility and civic participation.
Administrative arrangements (governance) do not facilitate or hinder the implementation of a coherent knowledge curriculum.
Establishing that the US political and labor environment allows for the degree of curricular standardization found in high-performing nations.
Establishing that curricular intervention alone can override the external socio-economic factors that also influence life chances.
That vocabulary gained through school-based knowledge instruction possesses the same signaling or functional value as vocabulary gained through family/social environment.
A curriculum can effectively manage the logistics of a split day where decoding and content are taught separately without causing confusion or losing instructional time.
Low-vocabulary students must have a higher 'catch-up' rate of word acquisition when immersed in a domain than high-vocabulary students.
Reduced teacher and student mobility contributes to long-term equity by providing social and pedagogical stability for at-risk students.
Evidence that other specific knowledge sequences (besides Core Knowledge) produce identical results in the US context.
A direct causal link showing that 'reduced alienation' is a primary driver of the 'narrowed achievement gap' in Core Knowledge schools.
The primary reason for American educational failure is a lack of clarity regarding expectations for stakeholders.
The 'public sphere' is constituted by the same set of humanistic knowledge that ameliorates class distinctions.
Administrative and logistical barriers to district-wide coordination are surmountable and less costly than the status quo of school-level autonomy.
A communal curriculum is the only or most efficient way to acquire the specific vocabulary and knowledge found on standardized tests.
Establishing that intellectual 'dethroning' of an idea is a sufficient condition to change the actual institutional practices of thousands of autonomous school districts.
A bridge explaining how the 'Think Globally, Act Locally' slogans (often associated with progressivism/environmentalism) are compatible with the author's communal, nation-focused curriculum.
A communal curriculum is the only or most efficient way to acquire these shared conventions of knowledge.
Providing central knowledge to the poor does not necessitate the erasure of their own cultural identity or cause psychological harm.
The fact that mental stages are influenced by education implies a duty for the state to standardize that education for the purpose of national unity.
A knowledge-specific curriculum must be demonstrated to be logistically and politically feasible to implement across diverse American contexts to 'overcome' inequality.
The success of a single school district can overcome the 'intellectual monopoly' of child-centered doctrines and the institutional inertia of state departments of education.
Establish that 18th-century thinkers lacked the *concept* of natural development, not just the specific vocabulary words 'growth' and 'development'.
Demonstrate that Horace Mann's shift in language actually reflected a revolutionary change in classroom practice across the nation, rather than just a shift in his own rhetorical style.
Human nature is defined by historical and anthropological continuity rather than potential biological shifts.
A theory's historical origin in secularized religion (Hegelianism/Pantheism) makes its current application in schools inherently invalid.
The psychological assumption that poor children are 'less developed' directly translates into specific instructional choices that result in lower achievement.
A causal account of how 19th-century German metaphysics was institutionalized into 21st-century American classroom protocols.
Evidence that Francis Parker's 'demanding curriculum' was a viable alternative that could have survived the romantic shift in American education.
A communal curriculum does not inherently conflict with a humane, child-respecting teaching style.
The persistence of these unexamined cultural values is the primary or necessary factor blocking educational progress.

Establishing that the increased reading gap was caused by the curriculum shift specifically, rather than external factors that disproportionately affected reading (e.g., changes in home literacy environments).
Evidence that the 'skills-based' methods adopted by teachers were the primary reason for the decline, rather than a decline in the quality of teacher training or student motivation.
Evidence that the student population in 2007 was demographically and linguistically comparable to the 1987 population.
Establishing that the science curriculum specifically is a primary driver of Japan's high PISA scores rather than general literacy or cultural work ethic.
Connecting the high allocation of time for music and art to the successful transmission of moral values or civic virtues.
Bridging the gap between the structure of a science curriculum (building content) and the necessity of a moral/values-based curriculum (building character).
Establishing that high-quality technical standards are impossible without a centralized, 'open covenant' curriculum model.

Demonstrating that the high PISA scores of Japanese students are directly caused by the Grade 3-6 science curriculum content rather than other factors like social homogeneity or extra-curricular tutoring.
Evidence that the Japanese curriculum is delivered with high fidelity across all socioeconomic districts to ensure the vocabulary gap narrows.
Verification that mathematical understanding of levers in Grade 6 directly contributes to the 'teamwork and communication' skills cited as 21st-century requirements.
The inclusion of specific content in a syllabus document translates directly into classroom priority and student mastery of that content.
Observation and direct experience are inherently directed toward knowledge consolidation rather than being open-ended exploration for its own sake.
Establishing that the specific list of organs (heart, lungs, etc.) is the optimal list for achieving the stated scientific literacy goals.
Evidence that the high performance of Japan is specifically caused by this curricular specificity rather than teacher training or social factors.

top_20_claims.text: The elementary curriculum is the primary cause of the fadeout problem at all ages.
Imparting a well-rounded, knowledge-based curriculum is the solution to preschool fadeout and the achievement gap.
The disintegration of the primary school curriculum is the primary cause of the French educational crisis.
Schools deny students equality of opportunity if they do not provide the knowledge needed to wield the language of the public sphere.
France's earlier success in overcoming inequality was due to universal preschool attendance and a communal primary school with a common curriculum.
The rise in French primary students failing to read and reckon in the 1970s and 80s was a consequence of new progressive methods, not the old rigorous curriculum.
Emphasizing individuality in early education is a paradox that thwarts actual individuality by failing to deliver shared knowledge.
France's adoption of skill-based, individualized educational principles caused it to decline from one of the best and most equitable systems to one of the worst and least equitable.
The current problems in American schooling are largely attributable to a diluted and aimless elementary curriculum emphasizing hands-on natural development.
All three forms of fadeout can be forestalled by following a communal-knowledge-based preschool with a communal-knowledge-based elementary school.
The Japanese science curriculum is a model of an intentionally and coherently structured curriculum designed to build rich content knowledge across grades.
Socialization is more natural to the human child than 'natural unfolding' is.
Domain immersion is the most effective strategy for enhancing knowledge and vocabulary in preschool.
Fiction and poetry are not fact-free uses of language that can be mastered through content-independent word study and comprehension sessions.
The knowledge-centric statements in the Common Core have no direct operational impact on tests.
Current standardized reading tests are based on a wrong theory of inference and reading comprehension.
Naturalistic and individualistic primary school principles are the primary cause of preschool fadeout.
The failures caused by progressive methods were paradoxically used as evidence to argue for more progressive reforms and against the national curriculum.
A knowledge-based approach to early education is better at narrowing differences in academic achievement and later earnings than a skills-based approach.
The long-range success of the Common Core depends on requiring a coherent approach to building knowledge in elementary school.

unsupported_load_bearing_claims.text: All three forms of fadeout can be forestalled by following a communal-knowledge-based preschool with a communal-knowledge-based elementary school.
Current early schooling practices have inadvertently depressed the vocabulary growth of students by age seventeen.
Common Core State Standards tests will continue to depress reading competence unless schooling prioritizes knowledge acquisition in early grades.
Current reading tests fail to accurately gauge the specific value a teacher has added to a student's reading comprehension over a year.
Natural-development programs and intensive-skills programs are similar in that they both offer a fragmented curriculum from the standpoint of knowledge building.
Piaget's developmental stages are highly co-determined by schooling and culture rather than being a mark of physiological maturation alone.
Great issues of equity and equality of opportunity depend on the application of technical psychological principles in the classroom.
Common Core can only become transformative if districts and states implement a specific, grade-by-grade knowledge-building curriculum.
The idea that education is a 'natural growth' or 'unfolding' is factually incorrect and morally wrong.
Every demographic group in France experienced a steep decline in achievement following the 1989 educational reforms.

well_supported_peripheral_claims.text: Child-centered curriculum principles were introduced in the 1930s and consolidated across the United States by the 1950s.
The French elementary school system was historically characterized by an egalitarian uniformity where all children studied the same national curriculum.
Jean Piaget's theories are currently used by educators to justify the delay of academic content based on 'developmental inappropriateness.'
The impact of the narrow testing regime has been particularly devastating for students in low-income communities.
Content-indeterminate standards and tests cause schools to de-emphasize systematic knowledge gain.
The educational decline in French elementary schools following 1989 constitutes a 'débâcle' equivalent in gravity to France's 1940 military defeat.
Progressive education views sin as a result of social customs imposed on an innocent being rather than an inherent trait.
The immediate causes of the incoherent primary curriculum are the pedagogical principles of individualism and skill-centrism.
Skills are domain-specific rather than general, meaning formal operations are performed better when tied to concrete experience.
The Common Core standard requiring 'logical inferences' is defective because textual inferences are contextual rather than logical.
